| **Noise** | Traffic, construction, airports | Day < 55 dBA, Night < 40 dBA (CPCB) | Mumbai, Chennai exceed by 15–20 dBA |
| **Land/Ecology** | Wetland loss, urban heat island, solid waste | Wetland cover > 30% baseline; per capita waste 0.5 kg/day | Bengaluru lost 78% wetlands in 40 years |

**Conclusion:** Environmental problems of urban growth are **interlinked** — e.g., vehicular emissions cause both air and noise pollution, and impervious surfaces cause both UHI and waterlogging.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B (b): Numerical Problem**

**Given:**
- $P_0 = 5{,}00{,}000$ (2011)
- $r = 3.0\% = 0.03$ per annum
- $n = 20$ years (to 2031)
- $Q_{supply} = 110$ MLD
- BIS standard $q_{std} = 135$ lpcd

**Step 1: Compute 2031 population**

$$P_{2031} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (1.03)^{20}$$

Compute $(1.03)^{20}$:

$$\ln(1.03)^{20} = 20 \times \ln(1.03) = 20 \times 0.02956 = 0.5912$$
$$(1.03)^{20} = e^{0.5912} = 1.8061$$

$$P_{2031} = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 1.8061 = 9{,}03{,}050 \approx 9.03 \text{ lakh}$$

**Step 2: Compute current per capita supply (at 2011 base, 110 MLD)**

$$q_{pc} = \frac{110 \times 10^6}{5{,}00{,}000 \times 10^3} = \frac{110{,}000{,}000}{500{,}000{,}000} = 0.220 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$
$$q_{pc} = 220 \text{ lpcd}$$

(Currently meets the BIS standard of 135 lpcd.)

**Step 3: Compute per capita supply in 2031 with same reservoir**

$$q_{pc}^{2031} = \frac{110 \times 10^6}{9{,}03{,}050 \times 10^3} = \frac{110{,}000{,}000}{903{,}050{,}000} = 0.1218 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$
$$q_{pc}^{2031} = 121.8 \text{ lpcd}$$

**Step 4: Comparison with BIS standard**

$$q_{pc}^{2031} = 121.8 \text{ lpcd} < 135 \text{ lpcd (BIS)}$$

**Shortfall:**
$$\Delta q = 135 - 121.8 = 13.2 \text{ lpcd}$$

**Required additional supply:**
$$Q_{required} = 9{,}03{,}050 \times 13.2 = 11{,}92{,}02{,}660 \text{ L/day} \approx 11.92 \text{ MLD}$$

**Conclusion:** The city will be in a **water deficit of 11.92 MLD by 2031** unless supply augmentation (new reservoir, recycling, rainwater harvesting) is planned. This is a typical KTU board problem demonstrating **planning failure** when growth outstrips infrastructure.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert MLD to litres** before dividing. *110 MLD = 110 × 10⁶ litres/day*. Unit errors cost 2–3 marks. Always show the unit conversion step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep)**

- [x] **Urban Growth Problem = Growth > Carrying Capacity** (memorize this one-liner)
- [x] **4D Framework** — Density, Deficiency, Degradation, Disparity
- [x] **Sectoral classification** — Physical, Environmental, Social, Economic (use as answer spine)
- [x] **6 Causes of urban growth** — Migration, Natural Increase, Industrialization, Service Sector, Reclassification, Transport Nodes
- [x] **Key standards to quote:**
  - Water supply = **135 lpcd (BIS)**
  - PM2.5 = **< 40 µg/m³ (NAQI)**
  - Noise day = **< 55 dBA**, night **< 40 dBA**
  - Wetland cover = **> 30% baseline**
  - Slum upgradation = **in-situ PMAY-U**
- [x] **Formulas (must memorize):**
  - $P_n = P_0 (1 + r)^n$
  - $D = P/A$
  - $BAR = A_{built}/A_{total}$
  - $FAR = \sum \text{Floor Area} / \text{Plot Area}$
  - $K = R/q_{pc}$
- [x] **Key Kerala case studies:** Kochi Smart City, Kazhakkoottam IT Corridor, Trivandrum Urban Sprawl, Calicut Coastal Erosion
- [x] **Key schemes:** PMAY-U, AMRUT, SBM-U 2.0, Smart Cities Mission, NULM (now DAY-NULM)
- [x] **Key policies:** Kerala Town & Country Planning Act, CRZ Notification 2019, Wetland Act 2023
- [x] **Causal loop diagram** — always draw with **+ / − polarity** on arrows
- [x] **Numerical problems** — show all steps; watch for **unit conversions (MLD ↔ litres)**
- [x] **One-line definition to memorize:** *"Urban Sprawl is the uncontrolled, low-density, leapfrog expansion of a city into surrounding rural and agricultural land."*
- [x] **One-line for UHI:** *"Urban Heat Island is the phenomenon of urban areas being 2–8°C warmer than surrounding rural areas due to anthropogenic heat and modified surface energy balance."*
- [x] **Cincinnati/Trivandrum analogy** — always use Kerala-context examples for full marks in B.Tech PECET527

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For a 14-mark question, structure your answer as **Intro → 4D classification → 2 case examples → diagram → standards quoted → conclusion**. This framework virtually guarantees 12+ marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beginning of Town Planning Acts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Beginning of Town Planning Acts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Town Planning Acts** are statutory legislative instruments enacted by sovereign governments (colonial or independent) that confer legal authority upon designated local or state authorities to regulate the physical, functional, and developmental growth of urban and rural areas through zoning, building bylaws, land-use control, and preparation of statutory plans.

In the Indian context, the **"Beginning of Town Planning Acts"** refers to the historical genesis (circa **1888–1920**) of legislated urban governance, starting with the establishment of Improvement Trusts, culminating in the first dedicated Town Planning Act — the **Bombay Town Planning Act of 1915**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a city as a giant, continuously growing **jigsaw puzzle**. Without rules, every person buys a piece and builds whatever they want — towers in residential lanes, factories next to schools, drains flowing backwards. **Town Planning Acts are the "rulebook"** that tells everyone which piece fits where, how tall their building can be, and where the roads must go. Before these rulebooks existed (pre-1888), Indian cities grew chaotically; epidemics, fires, and overcrowding forced the British colonial government to formally legislate urban order.

## 1.3 The Indian Genesis: Key Chronological Anchors

| Year | Legislative Milestone | Jurisdiction |
|------|-----------------------|---------------|
| **1888** | Bombay Improvement Act (Local Act) — precursor | City of Bombay |
| **1898** | Bombay Improvement Trust established (under the 1898 Act) | Bombay Presidency |
| **1911** | Royal Commission on Decentralisation | All-India |
| **1915** | **Bombay District Municipal Act (T.P. provisions) — First formal T.P. Act** | Bombay |
| **1919** | Madras Town Planning Act | Madras Presidency |
| **1920** | Nagpur Improvement Trust | Central Provinces |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Students must specifically remember that the **1915 Bombay Act** is universally recognized as the **"Mother of Indian Town Planning Legislation"** because it first introduced concepts like *Town Planning Schemes*, *Zoning*, and *Statutory Master Plans* into codified Indian law.

## 1.4 Why Did These Acts Begin? — The Triggering Conditions

1. **Plague Epidemic of 1896 (Bombay)** — killed thousands due to overcrowding, narrow streets, and poor sanitation.
2. **Fire Hazards** in dense inner-city bazaars.
3. **Industrial Revolution influx** — rapid, unplanned urban migration.
4. **Public Health Movement** (influenced by Edwin Chadwick, UK).
5. **Pressure from the Royal Commission (1907–09)** on Decentralisation.
6. **Advocacy of Sir Patrick Geddes** — *“Cities in Evolution”* (1915).
7. **Influence of the British Housing & Town Planning Act, 1909** (the *“Addison Act”*).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Pre-Act Era — Problem Definition

Before 1888, Indian municipalities operated under the **Bombay Municipal Act 1888** which gave only *general* powers of public health, conservancy, and road maintenance. There was **no statutory framework for comprehensive land-use planning**. The city fabric grew under:

- *Laissez-faire* private development
- Arbitrary *Floor Space Index (FSI)* — effectively unbounded
- No concept of *setbacks, height limits, or plot coverage rules*

## 2.2 The Three Evolutionary Phases of Indian Town Planning Legislation

### Phase I — The Improvement Trust Phase (1888–1914)
- **Mechanism:** Special-purpose statutory bodies (*Improvement Trusts*) created by **separate local Acts**.
- **Function:** Acquire land in declared *“slum areas”* and redevelop with wider roads, open spaces, and sanitary infrastructure.
- **Powers:** Compulsory land acquisition, demolition of unfit buildings, raising loans, and levying betterment charges.
- **Limitation:** *Reactive, not proactive* — addressed decay, not future growth.

### Phase II — The Town Planning Scheme Phase (1915–1939)
- **Mechanism:** The **1915 Bombay Act** introduced the revolutionary concept of the *“Town Planning Scheme”* under **Sections 314–325** of the **Bombay District Municipal Act, 1901 (as amended)**.
- **Function:** Statutory preparation of a *layout plan* showing roads, plots, open spaces, and reservations; financed by **reconstituted plots** (the *pooling* or *readjustment of land boundaries* method).
- **Key Innovation:** Owners surrender land for roads/public use but receive developed plots back in the same area, with **50% of the appreciation in land value** returned to the trust as *increment contribution*.

### Phase III — The Master Plan / Zonal Plan Phase (Post-Independence)
- **Mechanism:** Introduction of regional and metropolitan plans through the **Town & Country Planning Acts** of various states (e.g., Maharashtra 1955, Karnataka 1961, Kerala — in process).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes the **high-yield legal formulas and economic mechanisms** introduced in the 1915 Bombay Act that KTU examiners love to test.

| Concept | Expression / Definition | Unit / Value | Application |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------|-------------|
| **Original Plot Area (OPA)** | $OPA$ | sq.m | Land owned by owner before scheme |
| **Final Plot Area (FPA)** | $FPA = OPA - \text{deduction for roads \& amenities}$ | sq.m | Land returned to owner |
| **Deduction Ratio** | $DR = \dfrac{\text{Area surrendered}}{\text{Original Plot Area}}$ | dimensionless | Determines owner's contribution |
| **Increment Contribution (IC)** | $IC = 0.50 \times (FV - OV)$ | ₹ (Rupees) | 50% of land-value gain due to scheme |
| **Half Cost of Scheme** | $\text{HCS} = 0.50 \times \text{Total Scheme Cost}$ | ₹ | Recoverable from owners via FPA valuation |
| **Contributing Plot Value (CPV)** | $CPV = \text{Original Site Value} + \text{IC} + \text{HCS}$ | ₹ | Price at which FPA is sold to owner |
| **Setback (front)** | $S_f \geq \dfrac{H}{3}$ minimum | metres | Building line distance (typical) |
| **Plot Coverage (PC)** | $PC = \dfrac{\text{Ground Floor Built-up Area}}{\text{Total Plot Area}} \times 100$ | percent (%) | Maximum coverage rule |
| **Floor Space Index (FSI)** | $FSI = \dfrac{\text{Total Floor Area (all floors)}}{\text{Total Plot Area}}$ | dimensionless | Density control (Bombay legacy) |

> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked the *“increment contribution rate”* in a 1915-style Town Planning Scheme, write **50%** — this is the legally codified value in the original Act.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The 1915 Bombay Act's *plot-reconstitution* mechanism is still operational in **Maharashtra's Town Planning Schemes** (over 650 schemes executed to date — more than the rest of India combined). It is used by urban planners worldwide as a **"pro-poor, equitable land readjustment"** tool because:

- No land acquisition compensation disputes
- Owners benefit from value appreciation
- Public infrastructure is financed by owners themselves

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Increment Contribution Mechanism

This is the **single most important KTU question** in this module. The board expects you to derive the *Contributing Plot Value (CPV)* step by step.

> **Given:**
> - Original Plot Area, $OPA = 1{,}000 \text{ m}^2$
> - Original Market Value per sq.m, $OV = 5{,}000 \, \text{₹/m}^2$
> - Final (Reconstituted) Plot Area, $FPA = 700 \text{ m}^2$
> - Final Market Value per sq.m, $FV = 12{,}000 \, \text{₹/m}^2$
> - Total Scheme Cost, $C_{total} = 60 \text{ Lakh ₹}$
> - Total number of plots in the scheme, $N = 50$

**Step 1: Compute the Original Site Value (OSV) of the plot.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OSV &= OPA \times OV \\
    &= 1{,}000 \text{ m}^2 \times 5{,}000 \, \text{₹/m}^2 \\
    &= 50{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \quad \text{(i.e., 50 Lakh ₹)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Final Site Value (FSV) of the reconstituted plot.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FSV &= FPA \times FV \\
    &= 700 \text{ m}^2 \times 12{,}000 \, \text{₹/m}^2 \\
    &= 84{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \quad \text{(i.e., 84 Lakh ₹)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the value increment (unearned gain) due to the scheme.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= FSV - OSV \\
         &= 84 \text{ Lakh} - 50 \text{ Lakh} \\
         &= 34 \text{ Lakh ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the 50% Increment Contribution Rule (Statutory under 1915 Act).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
IC &= 0.50 \times \Delta V \\
   &= 0.50 \times 34 \text{ Lakh} \\
   &= 17 \text{ Lakh ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the owner's share of the Half Cost of Scheme.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{HCS per plot} &= 0.50 \times \dfrac{C_{total}}{N} \\
                     &= 0.50 \times \dfrac{60 \text{ Lakh}}{50} \\
                     &= 0.50 \times 1.2 \text{ Lakh} \\
                     &= 0.60 \text{ Lakh ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the Contributing Plot Value (CPV) at which FPA is sold to the owner.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CPV &= OSV + IC + \text{HCS per plot} \\
    &= 50 \text{ Lakh} + 17 \text{ Lakh} + 0.60 \text{ Lakh} \\
    &= 67.60 \text{ Lakh ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Verify the financial wisdom for the owner.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net wealth to owner} &= FSV - CPV \\
                           &= 84 \text{ Lakh} - 67.60 \text{ Lakh} \\
                           &= 16.40 \text{ Lakh ₹ (gain)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> The owner is **better off by 16.40 Lakh ₹** despite surrendering 300 m² of land, justifying the legitimacy of the scheme.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Pre-Act vs Post-1915 Act

| Parameter | Pre-1915 (Improvement Trust Era) | Post-1915 (T.P. Act Era) |
|-----------|----------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Approach | Reactive (acquire & rebuild slums) | Proactive (plan *before* growth) |
| Planning Instrument | Trust's *Scheme of Improvement* | Statutory *Town Planning Scheme* |
| Land Acquisition | Compulsory, paid at market rate | *Reconstitution* of plots (no full acquisition) |
| Owner Compensation | Cash compensation + solatium | Reconstituted plot at *Contributing Plot Value* |
| Public Cost Recovery | Government loans / taxes | *Increment Contribution* (50%) + *Half Cost* |
| Coverage | Inner-city *slum pockets* | Whole *planning area* (urban + suburban) |
| Plan Statutory Force | Advisory only | **Legally binding** on all owners |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    nodeB --> nodeC["C1 Bombay Improvement Trust 1898"]
```

```mermaid
    nodeQ1["Q1 Original Plot OPA and OV"] --> nodeQ4["Q4 Original Site Value OSV equals OPA times OV"]
    nodeQ2["Q2 Final Plot FPA and FV"] --> nodeQ5["Q5 Final Site Value FSV equals FPA times FV"]

    nodeQ6 --> nodeQ7["Q7 Increment Contribution IC equals 0.5 times delta V"]

    nodeQ7 --> nodeQ9["Q9 Contributing Plot Value CPV equals OSV plus IC plus HCS"]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]
**State any three causes that led to the beginning of Town Planning Acts in India.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Plague epidemic of 1896** in Bombay exposed the insanitary living conditions of overcrowded inner-city areas, forcing the colonial government to legislate urban reform. **(1 mark)**
2. **Rapid, unplanned urban growth** following industrialisation and migration overwhelmed municipal capacities, demanding statutory planning powers. **(1 mark)**
3. **Influence of the British Housing & Town Planning Act, 1909** (the Addison Act) and the **Royal Commission on Decentralisation (1907–09)** directly recommended a statutory framework for India. **(1 mark)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model]
**List the three evolutionary phases of Indian Town Planning Legislation with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Phase I — Improvement Trust Phase (1888–1914):** *Example — Bombay Improvement Trust, 1898.* **(1 mark)**
2. **Phase II — Town Planning Scheme Phase (1915–1939):** *Example — Bombay District Municipal Act (T.P. provisions), 1915.* **(1 mark)**
3. **Phase III — Master Plan Phase (Post-Independence):** *Example — Maharashtra Town & Country Planning Act, 1955.* **(1 mark)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the historical background that led to the enactment of the first Town Planning Act in India. Mention at least four triggering factors. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the procedure for preparation of a Town Planning Scheme under the Bombay Town Planning Act, 1915. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Historical Background (7 marks):**

1. **Pre-1888 Conditions:** Indian cities — particularly Bombay, Calcutta, and Madras — suffered acute overcrowding, narrow winding lanes (e.g., *Kumbharwada* and *Dharavi* precursors in Bombay), absence of drainage, and a complete lack of building bylaws. The Indian Factories Act of 1881 and the British Housing Acts of 1875 & 1890 had not been extended to municipal governance. **(1 mark)**

2. **Plague Epidemic of 1896:** The bubonic plague killed nearly **20,000 people** in Bombay alone. The Plague Commission, in its 1898 report, directly recommended comprehensive physical improvement of inner-city areas through statutory authority. **(1 mark)**

3. **Bombay Improvement Trust Act, 1898:** The first statutory body for urban renewal in India. However, its powers were *limited to declared improvement areas only* — it had no jurisdiction over a *whole city's planned future growth*. **(1 mark)**

4. **Royal Commission on Decentralisation (1907–09):** This all-India commission, chaired by **Lord Montagu**, explicitly recommended that municipal and local bodies be granted statutory town planning powers analogous to those in the UK Addison Act, 1909. **(1 mark)**

5. **Influence of Sir Patrick Geddes:** His seminal work *"Cities in Evolution"* (1915) and his preparation of town plans for Indian cities (e.g., his plan for Indore, 1918) popularised the modern town planning movement in India. **(1 mark)**

6. **Extension of the British Housing & Town Planning Act 1909:** This Act made *Town Planning Schemes* a statutory instrument in England. Indian municipal reformers, led by **Sir George P. Chitale**, advocated its adoption in India. **(1 mark)**

7. **The 1915 Bombay Act:** The Bombay Presidency, being the industrial-financial capital of British India, enacted the first formal *Town Planning Act* as part of the District Municipal Act, with detailed provisions for statutory Town Planning Schemes. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Procedure for Preparation of Town Planning Scheme (7 marks):**

1. **Notification of Planning Area (Sec. 314):** The State Government notifies a defined area as a *Planning Area* in the Official Gazette. **(1 mark)**

2. **Survey and Existing Land Use Study (Sec. 315):** The local authority conducts a cadastral, topographic, and ownership survey. **(1 mark)**

3. **Draft Town Planning Scheme Preparation (Sec. 316):** The local authority (or Trust) prepares a draft scheme showing proposed roads, parks, building plots, public amenities, and reservations. **(1 mark)**

4. **Public Notice & Objections (Sec. 318):** The draft is published, and any owner may file objections within a stipulated period (usually 30 days). A public hearing is conducted. **(1 mark)**

5. **Sanction by State Government (Sec. 320):** After considering objections, the scheme is finalised and *sanctioned* by the State Government — making it a *legally binding instrument*. **(1 mark)**

6. **Reconstitution of Plots (Sec. 322–324):** Plot boundaries are redrawn; each owner surrenders land for roads/amenities but receives a *reconstituted plot* in the same locality. **(1 mark)**

7. **Recovery of Increment Contribution and Half-Cost (Sec. 325):** The owner is charged **50% of the value increment** plus **50% of the scheme cost** at the *Contributing Plot Value* when taking back the reconstituted plot. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students confuse **"Improvement Trust"** (reactive body, 1898) with **"Town Planning Scheme"** (proactive planning instrument, 1915). The board **deducts 1 full mark** for this confusion.
> - The **increment contribution rate is 50%**, *not 33% or 25%*. Memorise it.
> - When asked about the *first Act*, write **"Bombay District Municipal Act (T.P. provisions), 1915"** — not "Bombay Town Planning Act" (which is the colloquial name).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Compare the **Improvement Trust Phase (1888–1914)** and the **Town Planning Scheme Phase (1915–1939)** in terms of scope, land acquisition mechanism, and financial recovery. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A land owner has an original plot of **800 m²** valued at **₹ 4,000/m²**. After implementation of a Town Planning Scheme under the 1915 Act, he is reconstituted with **600 m²** valued at **₹ 9,000/m²**. The total scheme cost is **₹ 40 Lakh** spread across **40 plots**. Calculate the Increment Contribution, Half Cost per plot, and the Contributing Plot Value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Comparative Analysis (7 marks):**

| Dimension | Improvement Trust Phase | T.P. Scheme Phase |
|-----------|------------------------|-------------------|
| **Scope** | Limited to declared *slum/improvement areas* | Entire *Planning Area* (urban + suburban) |
| **Trigger** | Reactive — fires, plagues, decay | Proactive — *future growth* planning |
| **Land Acquisition** | Compulsory acquisition at market price + solatium | Plot *reconstitution* — no full acquisition |
| **Financial Recovery** | Government loans, taxes, rents | Increment Contribution (50%) + Half-Cost |
| **Plan Force** | Advisory/schematic | **Statutory & Legally Binding** |
| **Owner Benefit** | Cash compensation only | Reconstituted developed plot |
| **Typical Statute** | Local Improvement Acts (e.g., 1898 BIT Act) | Bombay District Municipal Act 1915 |

*(2 marks for the table + 1 mark each for narrative explanation of any 3 distinct differences = 5 marks; balance 2 marks for an introduction and conclusion.)*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Original Site Value (OSV):** **(1 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
OSV &= OPA \times OV = 800 \times 4{,}000 = 32{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Final Site Value (FSV):** **(1 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
FSV &= FPA \times FV = 600 \times 9{,}000 = 54{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Value Increment (ΔV):** **(1 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V &= FSV - OSV = 54 \text{ L} - 32 \text{ L} = 22 \text{ Lakh ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Increment Contribution (IC) at 50%:** **(1 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
IC &= 0.5 \times 22 \text{ L} = 11 \text{ Lakh ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Half-Cost of Scheme per plot (HCS):** **(1 mark)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
HCS &= 0.5 \times \dfrac{40 \text{ Lakh}}{40 \text{ plots}} = 0.5 \times 1 \text{ L} = 0.5 \text{ Lakh ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Contributing Plot Value (CPV):** **(2 marks)**
$$
\begin{aligned}
CPV &= OSV + IC + HCS \\
    &= 32 \text{ L} + 11 \text{ L} + 0.5 \text{ L} \\
    &= 43.5 \text{ Lakh ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** IC = **₹ 11 Lakh**, HCS = **₹ 50,000**, CPV = **₹ 43.5 Lakh**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout for Numerical Questions:**
> - Do **NOT** apply the 50% increment to the *area* — apply it to the *value difference*. This is the most common 2-mark error.
> - Always show the *unit* (Lakh ₹) clearly in the final answer line — examiners grant a 0.5 mark **step-mark** for unit clarity.
> - If $FPA > OPA$ (very rare), the *value increment* is still positive but the deduction ratio is negative — interpret correctly.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Beginning of Town Planning Acts"**

- ✅ The **Bombay Improvement Trust (1898)** was the *first* statutory urban renewal body in India, NOT a Town Planning Act per se.
- ✅ The **1915 Bombay Act** (B.D.M.A. amendment) is the **first formal Town Planning Act in India** — universally cited.
- ✅ Three phases: **Improvement Trust → T.P. Scheme → Master Plan**.
- ✅ Increment Contribution = **50% of (FSV − OSV)** — a hard-coded statutory value.
- ✅ Half-Cost = **50% of total scheme cost**, distributed among all plots.
- ✅ CPV = OSV + IC + HCS — the *price at which the reconstituted plot is sold to the original owner*.
- ✅ **Sir Patrick Geddes** (*Cities in Evolution*, 1915) was the philosophical father; **Sir George P. Chitale** and **Sir Edwin Lutyens** were practical proponents.
- ✅ The **1919 Madras Town Planning Act** was the *first dedicated state Act* (separate legislation, not an amendment).
- ✅ Triggering conditions = Plague (1896) + Industrialisation + Royal Commission 1909 + British Housing Act 1909 + Geddes's influence.
- ✅ KTU keyword for answer-introduction: *"Town Planning Acts in India are rooted in the colonial public-health and improvement-trust movement of the late 19th century, with the 1915 Bombay Act marking the statutory birth of comprehensive town planning legislation in the subcontinent."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of New Towns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of New Towns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **New Town** is a comprehensively planned, self-contained urban settlement that is deliberately created — either by a governmental authority or a development corporation — to accommodate a targeted population, relieve congestion in an existing major city (the "parent" or "mother" city), and to decentralize industrial, residential, and commercial activities into a balanced, sustainable urban unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> A *New Town* is an urban unit planned in advance on a virgin site (or on the periphery of an existing settlement), incorporating a balanced mix of **residential, industrial, recreational, and administrative land uses**, governed by a single statutory development authority, and designed to be economically and socially self-supporting.

The United Kingdom's **New Towns Act of 1946** is historically considered the legislative benchmark worldwide. Under this Act, a *New Town* was defined as a town "designed to secure the proper distribution of population and industry, and to provide for the social and economic well-being of its residents."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a New Town as a "Seeded Apple."**

Imagine a giant, crowded apple tree (the metropolitan city) whose branches are bending and breaking under the weight of too many apples (people and industries). Rather than letting the branches snap, a gardener carefully picks a few healthy apples and **plants them in a freshly prepared patch of soil (greenfield site)** at a planned distance from the parent tree. Each new sapling is then deliberately shaped — given its own roots (employment), its own canopy (housing), and its share of sunlight and water (open spaces, amenities) — so that it grows into a **fully independent apple tree** instead of remaining a weak branch.

That is exactly what a *New Town* is:
- **Picked from** the over-loaded parent city (decentralization / decentralization of population).
- **Planted on** a fresh, untouched site (greenfield development).
- **Deliberately shaped** with a master plan (balanced land-use zoning).
- **Grown to be self-supporting** (economic and social self-sufficiency).

## 1.3 Key Parameters & Standard Metrics

A new town is typically evaluated against the following **standard planning metrics**, which are commonly tested in KTU examinations:

| Metric | Standard Benchmark | Symbol/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Population Size** | 20,000 – 250,000 (small to medium new town) | P (persons) |
| **Population Density (Gross)** | 40 – 60 persons/hectare | ρ_g (p/ha) |
| **Density of Neighborhood Unit** | 100 – 150 dwellings/hectare | ρ_n (dw/ha) |
| **Open Space Standard** | **3–4 hectares per 1,000 population** | m²/person |
| **Green Belt Width** | 8 – 16 km around the town | km |
| **Travel Time / Work-Place Distance** | 30 – 45 minutes maximum | minutes |
| **Land-Use Mix Balance** | Residential : Industrial : Open = 40 : 15 : 25 (approx.) | % of total area |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECET527 M1):**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly links the *Concept of New Towns* to the historical evolution from the **Garden City Movement (Ebenezer Howard, 1898)** to the **British New Towns (1946 onwards)** and the **Indian New Towns (e.g., Chandigarh, Bhubaneswar, Gandhinagar, Navi Mumbai)**.

## 1.4 Distinguishing "New Town" from Related Urban Forms

| Concept | Key Difference from a New Town |
|---|---|
| **Satellite Town** | Depends on the parent city; lacks full self-employment. |
| **Garden City** | A *theoretical model* (Howard, 1898) with population capped at 32,000; new towns implement this idea at a larger scale. |
| **Greenfield City** | Built on virgin land, but may not be *deliberately decentralized* from a parent city. |
| **Smart City** | Driven by **digital/ICT infrastructure** as the primary feature; new towns emphasize **physical and social planning**. |
| **Town Extension** | An organic addition to an existing town, not a fully independent settlement. |

---

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Theoretical Foundations of New Town Planning

The concept of a New Town rests on **three foundational pillars**, each grounded in classical urban planning theory:

### Pillar 1 — The Decentralization Doctrine
- **Why:** Large metropolitan cities suffer from congestion, high land values, slums, and infrastructural overload.
- **How:** New towns act as **counter-magnets** that absorb the "overspill" population and industries from the parent city.
- **Theory Origin:** Patrick Geddes (*Cities in Evolution*, 1915) and the **Greater London Regional Plan (1944)** by Patrick Abercrombie.

### Pillar 2 — The Garden City Principle
- **Why:** Combine the advantages of **urban (employment, services)** and **rural (open space, low density)** living.
- **How:** A concentric layout — Central Park → Town Centre → Residential zones → Industrial zones → Agricultural Green Belt.
- **Theory Origin:** Sir Ebenezer Howard, *To-morrow: A Peaceful Path to Real Reform* (1898).

### Pillar 3 — The Neighborhood Unit Principle
- **Why:** Foster community identity, social cohesion, and walkable access to daily amenities.
- **How:** A self-contained residential cluster (≈ 5,000–10,000 population) with a central school, shops, and parks within a 5-minute walking radius.
- **Theory Origin:** Clarence Perry (1929).

## 2.2 Operational Sequence: How a New Town is Created

The complete life-cycle of a new town, as taught under KTU PECET527 Module 1, follows **seven sequential stages**:

1. **Identification of Need** — Conduct a regional survey; identify congestion in the parent city and select a suitable site (typically 30–50 km away).
2. **Site Selection Criteria** — Proximity to parent city, availability of water, gentle topography, soil stability, transport corridor, and agricultural buffer.
3. **Constitution of a Development Authority** — A statutory body (e.g., *New Town Development Authority*, *City Development Corporation*).
4. **Preparation of Master Plan / Structure Plan** — Land-use zoning, road network, sectorization, density distribution.
5. **Phased Development** — Implemented in **3–5 construction phases** over **20–30 years** to ensure controlled growth.
6. **Population Absorption** — Migration of target population from the parent city via housing, employment, and incentive policies.
7. **Self-Sufficiency & Handover** — Achieves economic, social, and administrative independence.

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Numerical Cheat Sheet

Although *Concept of New Towns* is primarily descriptive, the following **planning formulas** appear in numerical KTU questions:

| # | Formula | Description | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{P \times \text{per capita land req.}}{10000}$ hectares | Convert population into gross land area (1 ha = 10,000 m²) | Calculate total town area |
| 2 | $\rho_g = \dfrac{P}{A}$ (p/ha) | Gross population density | Density verification |
| 3 | $A_{\text{open}} = \dfrac{P \times 4}{1000}$ hectares | Open space = 4 ha per 1,000 persons | Standards check |
| 4 | $D_{\text{neighborhood}} = \dfrac{P_n}{A_n}$ | Density of neighborhood unit | Sub-area design |
| 5 | $W_{\text{belt}} = k \cdot \sqrt{A}$ | Green belt width (k = empirical constant) | Buffer design |
| 6 | $\text{Work-Place ratio} = \dfrac{E_{\text{local}}}{P_{\text{working}}}$ | Local employment index; ≥ 0.7 = self-sufficient | Self-sufficiency test |
| 7 | $T_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{d}{v_{\text{avg}}}$ | Maximum commuting time | d = distance, v = average speed |
| 8 | $\text{Housing deficit} = P_{\text{design}} - P_{\text{existing}}$ | Population absorption target | Phasing math |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** For absolute-value-style expressions inside tables, use `\vert x \vert` (NOT the `|` symbol), to comply with markdown table parsing.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Planning

- **Urban Engineers** use new town metrics to size water supply, sewerage, and road networks.
- **Transportation Planners** design radial and ring road systems based on the *sectoral* or *polycentric* new-town template.
- **Environmental Engineers** apply the *green-belt* and *open-space* standards to compute **storm-water retention**, **air-quality buffers**, and **carbon-sink capacity**.
- **Civil Engineers** use density and population formulas to determine structural loads, building footprints, and utility line lengths.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> **Problem:** A new town is proposed for a target population of **1,20,000 persons**. The per-capita gross land requirement is **80 m²**. Calculate (a) Total land area, (b) Open space required at the standard of **4 ha per 1,000 persons**, and (c) Verify whether the gross density lies within the KTU benchmark of **40–60 p/ha**.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**(a) Total Land Area**

$$
A = \frac{P \times \text{per-capita area}}{10{,}000}
$$

Substituting $P = 1{,}20{,}000$ and per-capita area $= 80 \text{ m}^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{1{,}20{,}000 \times 80}{10{,}000} \\
  &= \frac{96{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} \\
  &= 960 \text{ hectares}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* We convert m² to hectares by dividing by 10,000 (since 1 ha = 10,000 m²). The town therefore occupies **960 ha** of developable land.

**(b) Open Space Required**

$$
A_{\text{open}} = \frac{P \times 4}{1{,}000}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{open}} &= \frac{1{,}20{,}000 \times 4}{1{,}000} \\
                &= \frac{4{,}80{,}000}{1{,}000} \\
                &= 480 \text{ hectares}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* This is **50 %** of the total town area (480 / 960 = 0.5) — extremely high and indicates the assumption must be re-checked. The actual typical open-space standard for a balanced new town is **3–4 ha per 1,000 persons** applied to **the gross area**, not the population; in practice planners apply this to the residential catchment. KTU students should clearly state assumptions.

**(c) Gross Density Check**

$$
\rho_g = \frac{P}{A}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_g &= \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{960} \\
       &= 125 \text{ persons/hectare}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* Since 125 p/ha **exceeds** the KTU benchmark of **40–60 p/ha**, the design is **not compliant** as a low-density new town. To meet the benchmark, the gross area must be increased:

$$
A_{\text{required}} = \frac{1{,}20{,}000}{50} = 2{,}400 \text{ hectares}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Master Plan Land-Use Split (KTU Template)

For a typical new town of **1,20,000 persons on 2,400 ha**, the KTU master-plan split is:

| Land-Use Category | % of Total | Area (ha) | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Residential | 40 % | 960 | Net housing density ~ 50 p/ha |
| Industrial | 15 % | 360 | Self-employment for ~ 30 % of workforce |
| Commercial | 5 % | 120 | CBD and sub-centres |
| Recreational / Open Space | 25 % | 600 | Parks, playgrounds, greenways |
| Transportation (Roads, Parking) | 10 % | 240 | Hierarchical road network |
| Public & Semi-Public (schools, hospitals) | 5 % | 120 | Social infrastructure |
| **Total** | **100 %** | **2,400** | — |

> *Derivation Note:* The residential percentage (40 %) is calculated as:
> $\dfrac{\rho_{\text{residential}}}{\rho_g} \approx \dfrac{50}{125}$, then adjusted upward to 40 % to account for roads, utilities, and community facilities inside residential sectors.

## 3.3 Self-Sufficiency Test (Work-Place Balance Ratio)

A new town is deemed **economically self-supporting** when at least 70 % of its working population is employed locally:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{WPR} &= \frac{E_{\text{local}}}{P_{\text{working}}} \\
\text{If } E_{\text{local}} &= 28{,}000 \\
\text{and } P_{\text{working}} &= 40{,}000 \\
\text{then } \text{WPR} &= \frac{28{,}000}{40{,}000} = 0.70 = 70\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Logic:* 70 % WPR is the KTU and UN-Habitat minimum threshold. Below 70 %, the town becomes a *dormitory* (bedroom) settlement and fails the new-town definition.

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three Generations of New Town Theory (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Garden City 1898 Howard"] --> node2["Neighborhood Unit 1929 Perry"]
    node2 --> node3["British New Towns 1946 New Towns Act"]
    node3 --> node4["Indian New Towns 1950s Chandigarh Gandhinagar"]
    node4 --> node5["Smart New Towns 2010s Amaravati GIFT City"]
```

> *Reading:* This flow traces the **chronological evolution** of the New Town concept, beginning with Howard's theoretical model and culminating in India's contemporary techno-planned smart new towns.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a New Town (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph parentCity["Parent City Overflow Source"]
        pc1["Congested Population"]
        pc2["Decentralizing Industries"]
    end

    subgraph newTown["New Town System"]
        d1["Statutory Development Authority"]
        d2["Master Plan & Sector Zoning"]
        d3["Residential Sectors"]
        d4["Industrial Zone"]
        d5["Green Belt & Open Spaces"]
        d6["Transport Network"]
    end

    subgraph outputs["Outputs to Citizens"]
        o1["Balanced Land Use"]
        o2["Economic Self Sufficiency"]
        o3["High Quality of Life"]
    end

    pc1 --> d1
    pc2 --> d1
    d1 --> d2
    d2 --> d3
    d2 --> d4
    d2 --> d5
    d2 --> d6
    d3 --> o1
    d4 --> o2
    d5 --> o3
    d6 --> o1
```

## 4.3 Phased Development of a New Town (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    phase1["Phase 1 Site Acquisition and Infrastructure 0 to 5 yr"] --> phase2["Phase 2 First Residential Sectors 5 to 10 yr"]
    phase2 --> phase3["Phase 3 Industrial Anchor Units 10 to 15 yr"]
    phase3 --> phase4["Phase 4 Commercial and CBD Growth 15 to 20 yr"]
    phase4 --> phase5["Phase 5 Full Self Sufficiency 20 to 30 yr"]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Land-Use Zoning Pattern (Concentric-Sector Hybrid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    centre["CBD and Administrative Core"]
    ring1["Inner Residential Ring High Density"]
    ring2["Outer Residential Sectors Low Density"]
    ring3["Industrial Belt along Transport Corridor"]
    ring4["Green Belt Agricultural Buffer"]
    centre --> ring1
    ring1 --> ring2
    ring2 --> ring3
    ring3 --> ring4
```

> *Reading:* The diagram maps the **classical Howard-Abbot** concentric model combined with the **sectoral** logic used in Chandigarh and the British Mark-1 new towns.

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Define a "New Town" and state any four of its essential characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *New Town* is a comprehensively planned urban settlement developed on a selected site, typically to relieve congestion in a major parent city, and designed to be **economically and socially self-sufficient**.

Four essential characteristics:
1. **Pre-planned development** with a single statutory authority.
2. **Balanced land-use mix** (residential, industrial, recreational).
3. **Adequate open spaces** at 3–4 ha per 1,000 persons.
4. **Self-contained employment** to minimise commuting to the parent city.

*(Marking tip: 1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 for characteristics.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Distinguish between a New Town and a Satellite Town.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | New Town | Satellite Town |
|---|---|---|
| **Self-sufficiency** | Fully self-supporting (≥ 70 % local employment) | Largely dependent on parent city |
| **Distance from parent** | 30–80 km | 10–30 km |
| **Authority** | Statutory Development Corporation | Often under municipal authority |
| **Master plan** | Independently prepared | Adheres to parent-city regional plan |

*(1 mark per differentiating line × 3 = 3 marks.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *(Recommended Choice for high-yield preparation)*

> **Q.A.** [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]
> **(a)** Explain the historical evolution of the **New Town concept** with special reference to Ebenezer Howard's *Garden City* theory. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss the **principles, objectives, and site-selection criteria** for the planning of a new town. Illustrate your answer with an **Indian example**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (a) (7 marks)

**1. Pre-Howard Urban Crisis (19th Century England) (1 mark)**
Industrial Revolution caused slum growth in London, Manchester, and Birmingham. Overcrowding, disease (cholera, 1848), and lack of sanitation triggered the need for planned settlements.

**2. Ebenezer Howard's Garden City (1898) (2 marks)**
- Published *To-morrow: A Peaceful Path to Real Reform*.
- Proposed **three magnets — Town, Country, Town-Country** — to combine urban and rural advantages.
- Recommended a population of **32,000** on **2,400 ha** with a **central park of 60 ha**, surrounded by an agricultural **green belt of 3,200 ha**.
- First implemented as **Letchworth (1903)** and **Welwyn (1920)**.

**3. Influence on 20th Century Planning (2 marks)**
- **Raymond Unwin** translated Howard's diagrams into detailed town plans.
- The **Greater London Plan (1944)** by Patrick Abercrombie proposed **ringed new towns** around London.
- This directly led to the **UK New Towns Act, 1946**, which designated **14 first-generation new towns** (Stevenage, Crawley, Harlow, etc.).

**4. Indian Adaptation (2 marks)**
Post-independence India used the new-town idea for **Chandigarh (Le Corbusier, 1951)**, **Bhubaneswar (Otto Königsberger, 1948)**, **Gandhinagar (H.K. Mewada, 1960s)**, and **Navi Mumbai (CIDCO, 1972)**.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A (b) (7 marks)

**1. Principles of New Town Planning (2 marks)**
- *Decentralization* from parent city.
- *Balanced land-use zoning*.
- *Neighborhood unit* as the basic social cell.
- *Green belt* as a permanent boundary.
- *Sectoral* or *concentric* growth pattern.

**2. Objectives (2 marks)**
- Relieve congestion of the parent city.
- Provide planned employment and housing.
- Promote regional balanced development.
- Improve quality of life through open spaces.

**3. Site-Selection Criteria (2 marks)**
- 30–50 km from parent city along a major transport corridor.
- Gentle topography (< 10 % slope).
- Adequate water supply and drainage.
- Stable soil for foundations.
- Agricultural buffer for green belt.
- Avoidance of flood-prone and ecologically sensitive zones.

**4. Indian Example — Chandigarh (1 mark)**
Designed by Le Corbusier as the new capital of Punjab and Haryana, on a **grid-iron sectoral plan** of 56 sectors, with explicit segregation of **commercial (sector 17)**, **industrial**, and **residential** uses, accommodating a planned population of **5,00,000**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Choice Alternative*

> **Q.B.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]
> **(a)** Describe the **role of a Development Authority** in the creation and management of a New Town. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Write notes on **(i) Green Belt, (ii) Neighborhood Unit, and (iii) Self-Sufficiency Index** as applied to New Town planning. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Q.B (a) (7 marks)

1. **Constitution (1 mark):** A *New Town Development Authority* is a statutory body created by a State Act (e.g., *New Town Development Authority Act*).
2. **Functions (3 marks):**
   - Acquires land under the *Land Acquisition Act*.
   - Prepares the *Master Plan / Structure Plan*.
   - Develops and maintains infrastructure (roads, water, sewerage, power).
   - Allots residential, commercial, and industrial plots.
3. **Funding (1 mark):** Through government grants, market borrowings, and land-value capture.
4. **Examples in India (2 marks):**
   - **Noida Authority** (New Okhla Industrial Development Authority, 1976) for the NCR region.
   - **CIDCO** for Navi Mumbai.
   - **NTDA** for Amaravati (2015).

#### Model Solution — Q.B (b) (7 marks)

**(i) Green Belt (2 marks)**
- A ring of open/agricultural land permanently reserved around a new town to **limit urban sprawl**, **provide recreation**, and **maintain ecological balance**.
- Width: 8–16 km (UK standard); land use: forestry, farming, water bodies.

**(ii) Neighborhood Unit (2 marks)**
- Proposed by **Clarence Perry (1929)**; population of **5,000–10,000** with a central elementary school, local shops, parks, and places of worship.
- Designed around a 5-minute walking radius (≈ 400 m).

**(iii) Self-Sufficiency Index (3 marks)**
- Defined as:
$$
\text{SI} = \frac{\text{Local Employment}}{\text{Total Working Population}} \times 100
$$
- A new town should achieve **SI ≥ 70 %** to be classified as economically independent.
- Computed by comparing **zoned industrial/commercial jobs** with the **projected working-age population**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "New Town" with "Smart City":** A smart city emphasizes ICT; a new town emphasizes *physical planning* and *decentralization*.
> 2. **Forgetting the numerical thresholds:** When asked about density or open space, students often quote only one number; always state the **range** (e.g., 40–60 p/ha).
> 3. **Missing the historical link:** Questions worth 7 marks often expect a *time-line* (Howard → Perry → Abercrombie → 1946 Act → Indian new towns).
> 4. **Skipping diagrams:** In a 14-mark answer, a *Master Plan sketch* or *Zoning diagram* is mandatory — losing up to 2 marks otherwise.
> 5. **Not naming the Indian example:** Always cite a real new town (Chandigarh, Gandhinagar, Navi Mumbai) with designer and year.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the "Concept of New Towns"**

- ✅ **New Town = Planned + Self-Sufficient + Decentralized** urban unit.
- ✅ Father of the concept: **Sir Ebenezer Howard (1898)** — *Garden City*.
- ✅ First statutory framework: **UK New Towns Act, 1946**.
- ✅ Three theoretical pillars: **Decentralization + Garden City + Neighborhood Unit**.
- ✅ Standard population: **20,000 – 2,50,000**; density: **40–60 p/ha**.
- ✅ Open-space standard: **3–4 ha per 1,000 persons**.
- ✅ Green-belt width: **8–16 km** around the new town.
- ✅ Self-sufficiency threshold: **Work-Place Ratio ≥ 70 %**.
- ✅ Site distance from parent city: **30–80 km** along a transport corridor.
- ✅ Indian new-town pioneers: **Chandigarh (Le Corbusier, 1951)**, **Navi Mumbai (CIDCO, 1972)**, **Gandhinagar (1960s)**, **Noida (1976)**.
- ✅ Phase duration: **20–30 years** in **3–5 construction phases**.
- ✅ Remember the difference: **New Town vs. Satellite Town vs. Smart City**.
- ✅ Always draw a **Master Plan / Zoning diagram** in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Use formulas **$A = P \times \text{pca}/10{,}000$** and **$\rho_g = P/A$** with consistent units (hectares).
- ✅ Quote **range values**, not single numbers, for all planning standards.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

topics.title: Comprehensive Planning of Towns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Comprehensive Planning of Towns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Comprehensive Planning of Towns** is a holistic, multi-dimensional, and integrated process of preparing a long-range physical, social, and economic development plan for an existing town or a proposed new town. It involves the systematic analysis of existing conditions, projection of future needs, formulation of goals, design of land-use patterns, and creation of an actionable implementation framework covering **a planning horizon of typically 20 to 30 years**.

As per standard town planning literature aligned with the KTU 2024 PECET527 syllabus, comprehensive planning refers to a **Master Plan / Development Plan** that integrates all sub-systems of a town — residential, commercial, industrial, recreational, transportation, public utilities, and social infrastructure — into a unified, balanced, and sustainable spatial framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Box:**
> *Comprehensive Town Planning* = A continuous, democratic, and technocratic process aimed at guiding the physical, economic, and social development of a town to achieve efficiency, equity, health, and welfare of its present and future inhabitants, through the preparation and enforcement of a Master Plan.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a family deciding how to spend money, allocate rooms, plan meals, and prepare for their children's future — all at once. They don't just buy a bigger house; they consider income, school locations, neighborhood safety, transport, parks, future savings, and rules for the family.

A **town is like a giant family of buildings, roads, people, and jobs**. *Comprehensive town planning* is the "family meeting with a smart financial advisor + architect + doctor + teacher sitting together" to design a 25-year roadmap for the entire family (town).

### Real-World Analogy: Building a House vs. Designing a Smart City

| Scenario | What Happens | Lesson for Town Planning |
|---|---|---|
| Building a single house randomly | Walls block sunlight, kitchen far from dining, no water drainage | Unplanned towns face congestion, flooding, slums |
| Designing a complete home (rooms, wiring, plumbing, garden) | Everything works together | **Comprehensive plan** = integrated land use + transport + utilities |
| Just buying land and building | No future-proofing | Master Plan reserves land for 2045 needs |

In short: **"Don't just build buildings — plan the city around people's lives."**

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Planning Metrics Used in KTU Examinations**

- **Planning Horizon:** **20–30 years** (typically adopted in Indian Master Plans)
- **Plan Period:** Often divided into **short-term (5 yrs)**, **medium-term (10 yrs)**, and **long-term (20–30 yrs)** phases
- **Projected Population (P_n):** Calculated using arithmetic, geometric, or incremental increase methods
- **Density Standards:** *Gross density* = 100–250 persons/ha; *Net residential density* = 200–400 persons/ha (varies by city tier)
- **Floor Area Ratio (FAR/FSI):** Typically **1.0 to 2.5** for residential in Indian Class II & III towns
- **Plan Implementation Authority:** Development Authority / Municipal Corporation (e.g., **Kerala Town and Country Planning Act, 110 of 2020**)

---

## 1.4 Objectives of Comprehensive Town Planning (KTU Board Favourite)

The fundamental objectives are universally aligned with the **UN-Habitat Sustainable Development Goals (SDG 11)** and Indian planning acts:

1. **Economic Efficiency** — optimal use of land, labour, and capital
2. **Social Equity** — equal access to housing, transport, and amenities
3. **Environmental Sustainability** — protection of water bodies, green cover, and air quality
4. **Public Health & Safety** — disaster-resilient layouts, sanitation
5. **Aesthetic & Cultural Value** — preserving heritage and identity
6. **Democratic Participation** — involving citizens in plan preparation

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Comprehensive Planning

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to understand that comprehensive planning is **not a single document** but a **5-stage cyclical process**. Each pillar answers a specific question.

### Pillar 1 — SURVEY & ANALYSIS ("Where are we now?")
Collects data on:
- **Physical features** — topography, soil, drainage, geology
- **Demographic data** — population, density, age-sex distribution
- **Economic data** — industries, employment, income
- **Social data** — education, health, recreation
- **Built environment** — buildings, roads, utilities
- **Legal & institutional** — ownership, zoning, building bylaws

### Pillar 2 — PROJECTIONS & FORECASTS ("Where are we going?")
Uses mathematical, statistical, and qualitative methods to predict:
- Future population (decadal growth)
- Future land requirements
- Future economic base
- Future traffic and trip generation

### Pillar 3 — GOAL FORMULATION ("Where do we want to be?")
Sets **SMART goals** (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound):
- *Reduce slum population by 50% by 2035*
- *Provide 100% piped water supply*
- *Achieve 30% green cover*

### Pillar 4 — PLAN PREPARATION ("How do we get there?")
Synthesizes all above into:
- Land-use plan (zoning map)
- Transportation plan (road hierarchy + transit)
- Sector plans (housing, industry, recreation)
- Public utility plan (water supply, sewerage, power, drainage)
- Implementation schedule (phasing)

### Pillar 5 — IMPLEMENTATION & REVIEW ("Are we on track?")
- Legal enforcement (zoning regulations, building rules)
- Financing (development charges, municipal bonds)
- Periodic review (every 5 years)
- Citizen feedback mechanisms

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential Formulas for Comprehensive Planning Questions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_n &= P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x} \quad &&\text{(Arithmetic Increase Method)} \\[4pt]
P_n &= P_0 \cdot (1 + r/100)^n \quad &&\text{(Geometric Increase Method)} \\[4pt]
P_n &= P_0 + n \cdot x + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y} \quad &&\text{(Incremental Increase Method)}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $P_n$ | Population after $n$ decades | persons |
| $P_0$ | Base/decade population | persons |
| $n$ | Number of decades | dimensionless |
| $\bar{x}$ | Average decadal increase | persons/decade |
| $r$ | Geometric growth rate | % per decade |
| $\bar{y}$ | Average incremental increase | persons/decade² |

### Other Essential Formulas

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Gross Density} \; D_g &= \frac{\text{Total Population}}{\text{Total Urban Area}} \quad &&\text{[persons/hectare]} \\[4pt]
\text{Net Residential Density} \; D_n &= \frac{\text{Population in Residential Zone}}{\text{Net Residential Area}} \quad &&\text{[persons/ha]} \\[4pt]
\text{FAR / FSI} &= \frac{\text{Total Floor Area of All Floors}}{\text{Plot Area}} \quad &&\text{[dimensionless]} \\[4pt]
\text{Per Capita Land Requirement} &= \frac{\text{Total Land Area}}{\text{Total Population}} \quad &&\text{[m}^2\text{/person]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Standard Norms (Kerala/Indian Context)

| Land Use | % of Total Developed Area | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Residential | **40 – 50%** | Largest single component |
| Commercial | **3 – 5%** | CBD + sub-centres |
| Industrial | **5 – 10%** | Depends on town class |
| Recreational/Open | **10 – 15%** | Parks, playgrounds, gardens |
| Transportation | **10 – 15%** | Roads, terminals |
| Public/Semi-Public | **5 – 8%** | Schools, hospitals, offices |

---

## 2.3 Principles Underlying Comprehensive Planning (KTU Theory)

1. **Principle of Unified Development** — no piecemeal development; whole town treated as a single organism
2. **Principle of Long-Range Vision** — anticipation of 20–30 year horizon
3. **Principle of Flexibility** — provision for revision, contingencies
4. **Principle of Citizen Participation** — democratic legitimacy
5. **Principle of Equity & Social Justice** — no spatial exclusion
6. **Principle of Sustainability** — intergenerational responsibility
7. **Principle of Economic Viability** — phasing matched with finance

---

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Application Area | Role of Comprehensive Plan |
|---|---|
| Smart Cities Mission (India) | Acts as the statutory anchor for all DPRs |
| Disaster Management (Kerala 2018, 2019 floods) | Identifies hazard zones, evacuation corridors |
| Transit-Oriented Development (Kochi Metro) | Reserves TOD corridors in advance |
| Affordable Housing (PMAY) | Allocates EWS/LIG land pockets |
| Coastal Regulation (Kerala CRZ) | Plans setback lines, no-development zones |
| Heritage Conservation (Fort Kochi, Mattancherry) | Demarcates heritage precincts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Population Projection & Land Allocation

This is the **single most important numerical question type** in KTU exams for this module. Let us solve it completely, step by step.

> **[KTU 2024 Style Numerical]**
> A Class III town in Kerala has the following population data (in thousands):
> 1971: 28, 1981: 34, 1991: 41, 2001: 49, 2011: 58
>
> **(a)** Using the **Geometric Increase Method**, project the population for **2031** and **2041**.
> **(b)** If the **net residential density** adopted is **300 persons/hectare** and **residential land** is to be **45% of total developed area**, find the **total developed area required by 2041**.

---

### Step 1 — Identify the Parameters

- Last known population (2011): $P_0 = 58{,}000$
- Target year: 2041
- Number of decades from base year: $n = (2041 - 2011)/10 = 3$
- We must first find the **geometric growth rate $r$** from historical data.

---

### Step 2 — Compute Decade-wise Geometric Growth Rates

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 \;(\text{1971–1981}) &= \left(\frac{34}{28}\right) - 1 = 0.2143 = 21.43\% \\[4pt]
r_2 \;(\text{1981–1991}) &= \left(\frac{41}{34}\right) - 1 = 0.2059 = 20.59\% \\[4pt]
r_3 \;(\text{1991–2001}) &= \left(\frac{49}{41}\right) - 1 = 0.1951 = 19.51\% \\[4pt]
r_4 \;(\text{2001–2011}) &= \left(\frac{58}{49}\right) - 1 = 0.1837 = 18.37\%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Average Geometric Growth Rate (Most Recent Decade is Used by KTU)

Standard KTU convention: **Use the average of the last 3–4 decades**, OR specifically use the **most recent growth rate**. We use the average for higher accuracy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{r} &= \frac{r_1 + r_2 + r_3 + r_4}{4} \\[4pt]
\bar{r} &= \frac{21.43 + 20.59 + 19.51 + 18.37}{4} \\[4pt]
\bar{r} &= \frac{79.90}{4} = 19.975\% \approx 20\% \text{ per decade}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 4 — Project Population for 2031 ($n=2$ decades after 2011)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2031} &= P_0 \cdot (1 + \bar{r})^{n} \\[4pt]
P_{2031} &= 58{,}000 \cdot (1 + 0.20)^{2} \\[4pt]
P_{2031} &= 58{,}000 \cdot (1.20)^{2} \\[4pt]
P_{2031} &= 58{,}000 \cdot 1.44 \\[4pt]
P_{2031} &= 83{,}520 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 5 — Project Population for 2041 ($n=3$ decades after 2011)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2041} &= 58{,}000 \cdot (1 + 0.20)^{3} \\[4pt]
P_{2041} &= 58{,}000 \cdot (1.20)^{3} \\[4pt]
P_{2041} &= 58{,}000 \cdot 1.728 \\[4pt]
P_{2041} &= 100{,}224 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 6 — Compute Net Residential Land Required (2041)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net Residential Land} &= \frac{\text{Projected Population}}{\text{Net Residential Density}} \\[4pt]
\text{Net Residential Land} &= \frac{100{,}224}{300} \\[4pt]
\text{Net Residential Land} &= 334.08 \text{ hectares}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 7 — Compute Total Developed Area (Reverse Calculation)

Given: Residential = 45% of Total Developed Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Developed Area} &= \frac{\text{Net Residential Land}}{0.45} \\[4pt]
\text{Total Developed Area} &= \frac{334.08}{0.45} \\[4pt]
\text{Total Developed Area} &= 742.4 \text{ hectares}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 8 — Analytical Matrix — Full Land-Use Allocation (2041)

Using the standard KTU norms in Section 2.2, distribute the **742.4 hectares** as follows:

| Land Use Zone | % Adopted | Area (hectares) | Functional Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Residential | 45% | 334.1 | Housing for 100,224 persons |
| Commercial | 5% | 37.1 | CBD, markets, offices |
| Industrial | 8% | 59.4 | Light & service industries |
| Recreational/Open | 12% | 89.1 | Parks, playgrounds, maidan |
| Transportation | 15% | 111.4 | Roads, bus stand, parking |
| Public/Semi-Public | 7% | 51.9 | Schools, hospitals, govt |
| Utilities & Misc. | 8% | 59.4 | Water, sewerage, power, solid waste |
| **TOTAL** | **100%** | **742.4** | **Developed Urban Footprint** |

> [!NOTE]
> *Verification: Sum of percentages = 45 + 5 + 8 + 12 + 15 + 7 + 8 = 100% ✓*
> *Sum of areas: 334.1 + 37.1 + 59.4 + 89.1 + 111.4 + 51.9 + 59.4 = 742.4 ha ✓*

---

## 3.2 Comparison of Population Projection Methods (Analytical Matrix)

| Method | Formula | Best Used For | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic Increase | $P_n = P_0 + n\bar{x}$ | Old, **saturated** towns with constant growth | Overestimates for fast-growing towns |
| Geometric Increase | $P_n = P_0(1+r)^n$ | Young, **rapidly growing** towns | Assumes constant % growth (rare) |
| Incremental Increase | $P_n = P_0 + nx + \frac{n(n+1)}{2}\bar{y}$ | Towns with **variable increments** | Complex; data hungry |
| Exponential | $P_n = P_0 e^{rn}$ | Continuous-growth model | Less common in KTU syllabus |
| Logistic / S-curve | $P_n = \frac{K}{1 + ae^{-bn}}$ | **Carrying capacity** based | Requires saturation data |

---

## 3.3 Why is the Geometric Method Most Common in KTU Exams?

- It uses **percentages**, which is intuitive for Indian towns
- Historical census data is easily available
- Matches the **compounded nature of urban growth**
- Simple to compute and verify
- Universally recommended by the **Town & Country Planning Organisation (TCPO)** for towns under 1 million population

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Architecture of the Comprehensive Planning Process

This block diagram shows the **5-stage master workflow** of comprehensive town planning. Memorize this — it is the single most important figure for 14-mark KTU theory questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: SURVEY AND ANALYSIS] --> B[Stage 2: PROJECTIONS AND FORECASTS]
    B --> C[Stage 3: GOAL FORMULATION]
    C --> D[Stage 4: PLAN PREPARATION]
    D --> E[Stage 5: IMPLEMENTATION AND REVIEW]
    E --> F[Periodic Review Every 5 Years]
    F --> A

    A --> A1[Physical Topography]
    A --> A2[Demographic Census]
    A --> A3[Economic Base Study]
    A --> A4[Social Infrastructure Audit]
    A --> A5[Legal and Land Ownership]

    B --> B1[Population Projection]
    B --> B2[Land Requirement Forecast]
    B --> B3[Traffic Demand Forecast]
    B --> B4[Economic Growth Forecast]

    D --> D1[Land Use Plan]
    D --> D2[Transportation Plan]
    D --> D3[Sectoral Plans Housing Industry]
    D --> D4[Utility Plans Water Sewerage Power]
    D --> D5[Phasing Schedule]

    E --> E1[Zoning Regulations]
    E --> E2[Building Bye Laws]
    E --> E3[Development Charges]
    E --> E4[Public Participation]
    E --> E5[Monitoring and Evaluation]
```

---

## 4.2 Hierarchical Plan Structure (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This shows the **hierarchy of planning documents** in India, which is a frequent 7-mark question in KTU.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[National Level: Five Year Plans] --> L2[Regional Plan: State Level]
    L2 --> L3[Sub Regional Plan: District Level]
    L3 --> L4[Master Plan: Town Level 20 to 30 Years]
    L4 --> L5[Zonal Development Plan: 5 to 10 Years]
    L5 --> L6[Site Layout Plan: Sector Level]
    L6 --> L7[Building Plan: Plot Level]
```

**Read this hierarchy top-down** as: National Policy → Regional → Master Plan → Zonal Plan → Layout → Building Plan.

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow of a Master Plan Document

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Existing Situation Analysis] --> P2[SWOT Analysis]
    P2 --> P3[Vision Statement 2045]
    P3 --> P4[Goals and Objectives]
    P4 --> P5[Strategy Formulation]
    P5 --> P6[Draft Master Plan]
    P6 --> P7[Public Consultation]
    P7 --> P8[Revisions]
    P8 --> P9[Statutory Approval]
    P9 --> P10[Notified Master Plan]
    P10 --> P11[Implementation Phase]
    P11 --> P12[Mid Term Review 10 Years]
    P12 --> P13[Plan Renewal]
```

---

## 4.4 Comprehensive Planning Input–Process–Output (IPO) Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INP[INPUTS: Data Surveys Demographics Economy Environment Legal] --> PROC[PROCESS: Analysis Projection Goal Setting Design Phasing]
    PROC --> OUT[OUTPUTS: Master Plan Maps Zoning Regulations Sector Plans Budget]
    OUT --> FEED[FEEDBACK: Monitoring Evaluation Review Citizens Court]
    FEED --> INP
```

This IPO loop is the **academic definition of comprehensive planning as a continuous process**, not a one-time activity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define Comprehensive Planning of Towns. List any four of its objectives.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Comprehensive Town Planning* is an integrated, long-range process of preparing a Master Plan that guides the physical, economic, and social development of a town over a 20–30 year horizon. *(1 mark)*

**Four objectives:** *(2 marks — 0.5 each)*
1. To achieve **economic efficiency** in land use and infrastructure
2. To ensure **social equity** through equal access to housing and amenities
3. To promote **environmental sustainability** and protect natural resources
4. To enhance **public health, safety, and welfare** of present and future residents

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the five stages of comprehensive town planning. State the typical planning horizon.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The five stages are: *(2.5 marks — 0.5 each)*
1. Survey and Analysis
2. Projections and Forecasts
3. Goal Formulation
4. Plan Preparation
5. Implementation and Review

**Planning Horizon:** **20 to 30 years** (typically adopted for Indian Master Plans, with a mid-term review at 10 years) *(0.5 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION 1**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **objectives and principles** of comprehensive town planning. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **survey and analysis stage** of master plan preparation with its major components. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Objectives & Principles (7 marks):**

**Objectives** *(3.5 marks)*
- To guide orderly, balanced physical growth of the town
- To integrate land use, transport, and utilities
- To ensure economic efficiency and employment generation
- To promote social equity and affordable housing
- To safeguard environment, heritage, and public health
- To enable disaster resilience and climate adaptation
- To involve citizens in democratic decision-making

**Principles** *(3.5 marks)*
- **Unified Development** — no piecemeal sub-area plans
- **Long-Range Vision** — anticipate 20–30 year needs
- **Flexibility** — periodic revision provisions
- **Sustainability** — intergenerational equity
- **Economic Feasibility** — phasing matches fiscal capacity
- **Hierarchy** — Regional Plan → Master Plan → Zonal Plan
- **Continuity** — planning is a process, not an event

---

**(b) Survey and Analysis Stage (7 marks):**

Survey & Analysis is the **data collection and diagnosis phase**. It has six sub-components:

| Component | Data Collected | Tools Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical Survey** *(1.5 m)* | Topography, soil, geology, drainage, contours | Total station, GIS, remote sensing |
| **Demographic Survey** *(1.5 m)* | Population, density, age-sex, literacy, migration | Census data, primary field surveys |
| **Economic Survey** *(1 m)* | Industries, employment, income, occupational pattern | Industrial surveys, employment exchange data |
| **Social Survey** *(1 m)* | Education, health, recreation, community life | Questionnaire, FGDs |
| **Built Environment** *(1 m)* | Existing land use, road network, utilities, building stock | GIS mapping, field inventory |
| **Legal & Institutional** *(1 m)* | Ownership, zoning, master plan, building rules | Revenue records, planning acts |

**Key output:** A **base map** and **SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats)** analysis of the town.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION 2**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** A Class III Kerala town had populations of **42,000 (2001)** and **54,600 (2011)**. Using the **Geometric Increase Method**, project the populations for **2021, 2031, and 2041**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** If the **net residential density** adopted is **275 persons/hectare** and residential use constitutes **48%** of the total developed area, determine the **total developed area required by 2041**. Also list the **land-use distribution** as per KTU norms. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Population Projection (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Geometric growth rate from 2001 to 2011:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \left(\frac{P_{2011}}{P_{2001}}\right) - 1 \\[4pt]
r &= \left(\frac{54{,}600}{42{,}000}\right) - 1 \\[4pt]
r &= 1.30 - 1 = 0.30 = 30\% \text{ per decade}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Project 2021 ($n=1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2021} &= 54{,}600 \cdot (1.30)^{1} = 70{,}980
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Project 2031 ($n=2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2031} &= 54{,}600 \cdot (1.30)^{2} = 54{,}600 \cdot 1.69 = 92{,}274
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Project 2041 ($n=3$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{2041} &= 54{,}600 \cdot (1.30)^{3} = 54{,}600 \cdot 2.197 = 1{,}19{,}956.2 \approx 1{,}19{,}956
\end{aligned}
$$

| Year | Population |
|---|---|
| 2021 | 70,980 |
| 2031 | 92,274 |
| 2041 | 1,19,956 |

---

**(b) Land Area Calculation & Distribution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Net residential land required in 2041:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{res} &= \frac{1{,}19{,}956}{275} = 436.20 \text{ hectares}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Total developed area (since residential = 48%):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{total} &= \frac{436.20}{0.48} = 908.75 \approx 909 \text{ hectares}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Land-use distribution (using KTU norms):

| Land Use | % | Area (ha) |
|---|---|---|
| Residential | 48% | 436.2 |
| Commercial | 5% | 45.4 |
| Industrial | 8% | 72.7 |
| Recreational/Open | 12% | 109.0 |
| Transportation | 15% | 136.3 |
| Public/Semi-Public | 7% | 63.6 |
| Utilities & Misc. | 5% | 45.4 |
| **TOTAL** | **100%** | **908.6** |

> *Note: Minor rounding differences in last digit are acceptable.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing $n$**: Students often take $n$ as the number of *years* instead of *decades*. **Remember: in geometric/arithmetic increase, $n$ = number of decades.** *(Loss: 1–2 marks)*
> 2. **Using wrong base year**: Always take the **last known census year** as $P_0$, not the first year of data. *(Loss: 1 mark)*
> 3. **Skipping the growth-rate calculation**: KTU expects you to **derive $r$ or $\bar{x}$ first**, then apply. Writing the final number without intermediate steps = 0.
> 4. **Density–area confusion**: *Net density* uses *residential area*; *Gross density* uses *total urban area*. Mixing them = 2 marks lost.
> 5. **Skipping land-use table**: Even if the area is correct, the **land-use distribution table is mandatory** for full marks in part (b).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin This on Your Wall!)**

✅ Comprehensive planning = **Master Plan preparation** for a **20–30 year horizon**

✅ **Five stages** in order: **Survey & Analysis → Projections → Goals → Plan Preparation → Implementation & Review**

✅ **Geometric method** is the **KTU default** for population projection; formula is $P_n = P_0(1+r)^n$

✅ Always compute $r$ from **last two census decades** unless specified

✅ Land use proportions (must memorize): **Residential ~45%**, **Roads ~15%**, **Open spaces ~12%**, **Industrial ~8%**, **Commercial ~5%**

✅ **Net density** applies to **residential area only**; **Gross density** applies to **total urban area**

✅ Comprehensive plan is a **continuous process**, not a one-time activity — it loops back via periodic review

✅ Hierarchy in India: **National Plan → Regional Plan → Master Plan → Zonal Plan → Layout Plan → Building Plan**

✅ Kerala-specific legislation: **Kerala Town and Country Planning Act, 110 of 2020** (for statutory authority)

✅ Citizens' participation is **mandatory** for democratic legitimacy and plan acceptance

✅ Smart Cities, PMAY, and Kochi Metro are all **anchored on the Master Plan**

✅ A good comprehensive plan integrates **land + transport + utilities + environment + economy + society** as a unified whole

---

**END OF NOTES — COMPREHENSIVE PLANNING OF TOWNS (MODULE 1)**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, PECET527 — Town Planning*
