# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define Town Planning. Why is it considered necessary in modern times?**

**Model Answer:**
Town Planning is the art and science of ordering the land-use, siting, and design of buildings, communication networks, and community facilities in such a way as to ensure the **health, safety, convenience, and welfare of the population**. In modern times, it is considered necessary because:
1. **Rapid urbanization** — UN projects 68% world urban population by 2050.
2. **Scarcity of land** — finite land must be optimally allocated.
3. **Public health** — prevents epidemics through proper sanitation.
4. **Traffic management** — avoids congestion in growing cities.
5. **Environmental sustainability** — preserves ecology and mitigates climate change.
*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for any 2 valid points with metrics.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: List any three necessities of town planning in the context of Indian cities.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Containment of Slum Growth** — Without planning, ~35% of Indian urban population lives in slums.
2. **Heritage Conservation** — Indian cities like Varanasi, Jaipur need regulated development.
3. **Flood & Disaster Management** — Coastal cities like Kochi, Chennai, Mumbai require scientific planning.
4. **Economic Efficiency** — Industrial corridors need zoning to avoid conflicts.
5. **Equitable Service Delivery** — Public amenities must be within 300–500 m of every household.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In "Necessity" questions, students often write *generic points* without **quantification, case studies, or formula linkage**. This fetches only **5–6 marks** out of 14.
> - Always include: **at least 1 numerical example, 1 historical case, and 1 modern reference (SDG/Smart City)**.
> - **Diagrams fetch 1–2 extra marks** — always draw the *7 Pillars* flow or a *zoning diagram*.
> - Do NOT exceed **6 pages** — conciseness with structure is rewarded.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) Discuss in detail the various necessities of town planning in the context of growing urban areas. Support your answer with suitable examples and at least one numerical illustration. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 Mark):**
Town planning is the systematic regulation of land use and infrastructure development to ensure orderly, healthy, and efficient urban growth. With rapid urbanization — India is projected to have **600 million urban dwellers by 2030** — town planning is not optional but **mandatory**.

**2. Necessities (5 Marks) — Cover at least 5 of the 7 pillars:**

- **Public Health Necessity:** Unplanned cities breed epidemics. The **1854 Broad Street Cholera outbreak in London** led Dr. John Snow to link water supply to disease, eventually prompting London's sewerage master plan.
- **Transportation Necessity:** Without road hierarchy, traffic collapses. **Mumbai's Eastern Freeway and Bangalore's Outer Ring Road** are direct responses to planning necessities.
- **Economic Necessity:** **SEZs, IT corridors, and industrial estates** (e.g., Bengaluru Whitefield) require zoning to function efficiently.
- **Environmental Necessity:** **Delhi's air pollution crisis** and **Chennai's 2015 floods** are direct results of unplanned urban sprawl.
- **Social Equity Necessity:** **Ray-Mehta Model (1959)** and **Rajiv Awas Yojana** addressed housing necessity.
- **Aesthetic Necessity:** **Chandigarh (Le Corbusier)** and **Jaipur (Vidyadhar Bhattacharya)** showcase necessity of planned beauty.
- **Disaster Resilience Necessity:** **Bhubaneshwar post-1999 Super Cyclone reconstruction** is a planning necessity response.

**3. Numerical Illustration (1 Mark):**
A town with $P_0 = 50{,}000$ growing at $r = 4\%$ per year with $A = 200$ hectares. At safe density $D = 250$ p/ha, $P_{max} = 50{,}000$. Thus the town has *already* reached its safe limit. **Without vertical development planning (FAR increase, TOD), the city will degrade** — proving planning necessity.

---

**(b) Explain with neat sketches how the necessity of town planning differs for (i) a newly planned city and (ii) an existing old city. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Necessity for a Newly Planned City (3.5 Marks):**
- Greenfield design — **complete freedom** in land use, road network, zoning.
- Necessities include: **futuristic population accommodation, sustainable transport (TOD), green corridors, disaster-resilient layout, mixed-use zoning**.
- **Example: Chandigarh, Gandhinagar, Amaravati, Navi Mumbai, GIFT City (Gujarat).**
- The necessity is *prospective* — designing for the **next 100 years**.

**(ii) Necessity for an Existing Old City (3.5 Marks):**
- **Brownfield constraints** — heritage, dense built fabric, narrow lanes, ownership disputes.
- Necessities include: **adaptive reuse, heritage conservation, traffic decongestion, slum upgradation, retrofitting drainage/water supply, densification via vertical growth, preserving cultural identity**.
- **Example: Delhi (Shahjahanabad), Varanasi, Kochi Fort Kochi, Ahmedabad Walled City, Madurai Meenakshi Temple zone.**
- The necessity is *retrospective + corrective* — solving problems **already created by past neglect**.

**Conclusion (integrated):** Whether greenfield or brownfield, planning is necessary, but the *tools* differ — **greenfield uses master planning, brownfield uses redevelopment, retrofit, and conservation planning**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) "Town planning is a necessity born out of the failures of unplanned urbanization." Critically analyze this statement with reference to the **7 pillars of planning necessity**. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Premise (1 Mark):**
The statement is **correct** — town planning is a *response* to failures, not an arbitrary imposition.

**2. The 7 Pillars as Failures → Solutions (5 Marks):**

| Failure (Pillar) | Historical Example | Planning Response |
|---|---|---|
| Health | Cholera epidemics (19th C.) | Sewage & water supply master plans |
| Traffic | Mumbai 2005 floods, Delhi jams | Ring roads, metro rail, BRT |
| Economy | Mixed industrial-residential conflicts | Zoning ordinances, SEZs |
| Environment | Bengaluru lake loss, Delhi AQI | Wetland protection, green belts |
| Equity | Dharavi, Kolkata bustees | PMAY, Slum Board, in-situ rehabilitation |
| Aesthetics | Unauthorized demolition of heritage | Heritage Acts, UNESCO norms |
| Disaster | Bhuj 2001, Kashmir 2014 | NBC 2016, NDMA guidelines |

**3. Critical Analysis (1 Mark):**
While the statement is true, one must note that town planning is also a **proactive necessity** — e.g., the **Smart Cities Mission (100 cities)** is forward-looking, not just remedial. So planning is both *curative* and *preventive*.

---

**(b) Using a numerical example, demonstrate why town planning becomes a *time-bound necessity* for a growing Indian town. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Problem:** A town has $P_0 = 2{,}00{,}000$ in 2024, growing at $r = 2.5\%$ per annum. Total available land = 800 ha. Safe density = 250 p/ha. **Find the year by which planning intervention is mandatory.**

**Step 1:** Maximum safe population $P_{max} = 250 \times 800 = 2{,}00{,}000$. **Already at the limit in 2024!**

**Step 2:** Suppose the city adopts **vertical planning** (increasing FAR from 1.5 to 3.0), effectively **doubling capacity**: $P_{max,new} = 4{,}00{,}000$.

**Step 3:** Solve for $n$ when $P_n$ reaches this new limit:
$$2{,}00{,}000 \times (1.025)^n = 4{,}00{,}000$$
$$(1.025)^n = 2$$
$$n = \frac{\ln 2}{\ln 1.025} = \frac{0.6931}{0.02469} \approx 28.07 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4:** The planning intervention buys **~28 years** of safe growth. After that, **further planning (peripheral expansion, satellite town development, or transit corridors)** becomes necessary.

**Step 5:** Conclusion — The example proves that **planning is a time-bound, continuous necessity**, not a one-time activity. **Master Plans must be revised every 20–30 years** (as mandated in Kerala TCP Act 2016).

**Diagram (1 mark for any of these):**
- Show a **Land Use Pie Chart** (Residential 40%, Commercial 10%, Industrial 15%, Recreational 15%, Transport 15%, Other 5%).
- Show a **Density Curve** (Population vs Years with and without planning).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "NECESSITY" Module 2**

- **Core Idea:** Town planning is *necessary* because urban growth is inevitable and land is finite.
- **7 Pillars of Necessity:** Health, Traffic, Economy, Environment, Equity, Aesthetics, Disaster Safety.
- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - Geometric Growth: $P_n = P_0 (1 + r)^n$
  - Arithmetic Growth: $P_n = P_0 + n \bar{x}$
  - Density: $D = P/A$
  - FAR Control
- **Mandatory Statistics for Exams:**
  - UN 68% urban by 2050
  - India 600M urban by 2030
  - 9 m²/person open space (WHO)
  - 250 p/ha safe density
- **Case Studies to Quote:** Chandigarh, Varanasi, London Sewerage Plan, Delhi Metro, Bhuj Reconstruction, GIFT City, Kochi Smart City.
- **Laws/Acts to Reference:** Kerala Town & Country Planning Act 2016, 74th Constitutional Amendment, NBC 2016, Model Town & Country Planning Law (MoHUA).
- **Modern Hooks:** SDG 11, Smart Cities Mission, PMAY-U, Swachh Bharat Urban, Climate Resilient City Plans.
- **Always quantify:** A "Necessity" answer without numbers is a *low-mark answer*.
- **Always cite:** At least 1 historical + 1 contemporary example.
- **Always draw:** A diagram (zoning pie chart, flow chart, or master plan sketch) for extra marks.
- **Avoid these common mistakes:**
  - Writing generic sentences like *"cities are growing fast"*.
  - Forgetting to mention the *time-bound* nature of planning necessity.
  - Ignoring the **equity dimension** (slums and affordable housing).
  - Failing to link necessity to **sustainability and climate action**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collection of Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Collection of Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Collection** in town planning is the systematic process of gathering, measuring, recording, and analyzing qualitative and quantitative information related to the physical, social, economic, demographic, and environmental characteristics of a region or settlement, to serve as the empirical foundation for informed planning decisions, policy formulation, and design interventions.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)**, data collection forms the *first and most critical phase* of the **Planning Process**, preceding analysis, synthesis, and the preparation of a development plan/master plan.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECET527 – Module 2):**
> The collection of data for a town planning exercise broadly encompasses:
> 1. **Demographic Data** (Population, density, age-sex distribution)
> 2. **Economic Data** (Occupation, income, employment patterns)
> 3. **Land Use Data** (Existing land use, built form, open spaces)
> 4. **Traffic & Transportation Data** (Volume, mode, network)
> 5. **Housing Data** (Typology, condition, occupancy)
> 6. **Infrastructure & Utility Data** (Water, sewerage, power, drainage)
> 7. **Environmental Data** (Topography, climate, vegetation, water bodies)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. Before prescribing medicine, the doctor must:
- Measure temperature,
- Check blood pressure,
- Ask about symptoms,
- Review past medical history.

**Town planning works exactly the same way.** A town planner is the "doctor" of a city. The "patient" is the town/city itself. Before designing a road, a park, a housing layout, or a drainage system, the planner must **collect data** (symptoms and measurements) to understand the current health of the town.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy – "Building a House":**
> Before an architect designs a house, they need a *site survey* — soil test, boundary measurements, sun path, wind direction, existing trees, neighboring buildings. Without this data, the design is guesswork. Similarly, **no town plan can be scientifically prepared without authentic, up-to-date data**.

---

### 1.3 Why is Data Collection Important? (KTU Board Examiner Perspective)

| Sl. No. | Purpose | KTU Board Perspective |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Base for planning** | Every master plan requires empirical data as its foundation. |
| 2 | **Identifies problems** | Surveys reveal deficiencies — slums, traffic congestion, lack of parks. |
| 3 | **Forecasting** | Past + present data → projection of future needs. |
| 4 | **Justification** | Plans and budgets must be evidence-based. |
| 5 | **Legal/Statutory** | Plans need statutory backing (e.g., under Town & Country Planning Acts). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric (as per URDPFI / NBC 2016):**
> A standard data collection exercise for a master plan typically covers the **Planning Area** (sometimes with an additional **2 km buffer zone**) and must be updated at least every **10–20 years** (depending on the rate of urban growth).

---

### 1.4 Two Fundamental Categories of Data

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box – Two Pillars of Town Planning Data:**
> - **Primary Data** → Data collected *first-hand* by the planner/surveyor for the *first time* (e.g., a household survey, a traffic count at an intersection).
> - **Secondary Data** → Data already collected and published by *other agencies* (e.g., Census of India, Census of Housing, Town Planning Office records, statistical handbooks).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Data Required for Town Planning

Town planning data can be classified along multiple axes. The KTU board typically tests the following classification:

#### A. Based on Source

1. **Primary Data (First-Hand Data)**
   - Collected directly through field surveys.
   - Examples: Questionnaire surveys, traffic volume counts, housing condition surveys, land use surveys, topographic surveys.
   - *Advantage:* Specific, accurate, current.
   - *Disadvantage:* Costly, time-consuming, requires trained enumerators.

2. **Secondary Data (Second-Hand Data)**
   - Sourced from published/recorded documents.
   - Examples: Census of India, NSSO surveys, Town Planning Department records, Municipal budgets, Five-Year Plans.
   - *Advantage:* Cheap, readily available.
   - *Disadvantage:* May be outdated, may not match planning unit boundaries.

#### B. Based on Nature

1. **Quantitative Data** – Numerical (e.g., population = 5,00,000; traffic = 4500 PCU/hr).
2. **Qualitative Data** – Descriptive (e.g., quality of life, satisfaction level, perception of safety).

#### C. Based on Time Dimension

1. **Cross-Sectional Data** – Snapshot at a single point in time.
2. **Time-Series Data** – Collected over multiple time periods (useful for forecasting).

---

### 2.2 Sources of Data (Detailed — High KTU Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.1: Comprehensive Sources of Town Planning Data**

| Sl. No. | Data Type | Primary Source(s) | Secondary Source(s) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Population & Demographics | Door-to-door census, sample household survey | Census of India, District Census Handbook, Town Planning Department |
| 2 | Economic / Occupation | Field survey, employment survey | Employment Exchange records, Industries Department, Economic Census |
| 3 | Land Use | Field survey, GPS/GIS mapping, aerial photography | Master Plan records, Revenue maps, Cadastral maps, Municipality records |
| 4 | Housing | Housing condition survey, sample dwelling survey | Census of Housing (NBO), Building permissions, Slum surveys |
| 5 | Traffic & Transportation | Manual/Automatic traffic counts, O-D surveys, speed-delay surveys | RTO records, PWD/NHAI data, traffic police records |
| 6 | Water Supply | Field survey of households, source inventory | PHED, KWA, Municipal Engineering Department |
| 7 | Sewerage & Drainage | Network mapping, inspection manholes | Municipal/Public Health Engineering records |
| 8 | Topography & Physical Features | Total Station, DGPS, Drone survey, LiDAR | Survey of India (SoI) toposheets, GSI, NRSC Bhuvan |
| 9 | Climate & Environment | Field weather stations, micro-climate sensors | IMD (India Meteorological Department), State Pollution Control Board |
| 10 | Social / Community Facilities | Inventory of schools, hospitals, parks | Education/Health Department records, ULBs |

---

### 2.3 Methods of Data Collection (Step-by-Step Logic)

#### Method 1: **Field Survey (Primary)**

**Step 1:** Define the **Planning Area** boundary.
**Step 2:** Divide the area into **traffic analysis zones (TAZs)** or **census enumeration blocks**.
**Step 3:** Design a **structured questionnaire / schedule**.
**Step 4:** Train **enumerators / investigators**.
**Step 5:** Conduct **pilot survey** (test on 5–10% sample).
**Step 6:** Final survey execution — door-to-door or at fixed points.
**Step 7:** **Quality check** (supervisor re-checks 10% of schedules).
**Step 8:** **Data entry, coding, tabulation**.

#### Method 2: **Questionnaire Method**

Two sub-types:

- **Open-ended questionnaire** – Respondent answers freely (rich qualitative data).
- **Closed-ended / Structured questionnaire** – Pre-defined options (easy to tabulate, KTU-favored).

> [!NOTE]
> **Sampling Note (for KTU 2024):**
> A typical Master Plan survey may use **stratified random sampling** — dividing the planning area into strata (zones), then randomly sampling households within each zone. Sample size is often **5–10% of households** (for city-level plans).

#### Method 3: **Observation Method**

The planner physically observes and records — e.g., counting vehicles at a junction, observing land use, building heights, pedestrian movement.

#### Method 4: **Remote Sensing & GIS Method**

Satellite imagery (e.g., from **Cartosat, Resourcesat, Sentinel-2, Landsat**) and **GIS (Geographic Information System)** platforms (e.g., QGIS, ArcGIS) are used to map:
- Existing land use,
- Built-up area,
- Open spaces,
- Vegetation,
- Water bodies.

> **Spatial Resolution Reference:**
> - **Cartosat-1 (Cartosat-2 Series):** ~1 m panchromatic — ideal for urban land use.
> - **Sentinel-2:** 10 m multispectral — for large-area land cover.
> - **Google Earth Pro:** Free, high-resolution for preliminary surveys.

#### Method 5: **Secondary Data Compilation**

Census of India, District Census Handbooks, Town Planning Department records, Municipal budgets, Five-Year Plan documents, DPRs, NSSO reports.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Demographic Projections)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 2.2: Master Formula Sheet for Population Forecasting**
> (Critical for Town Planning data analysis — KTU-favorite topic)

| Method | Formula | Variables | Best Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Arithmetic Increase** | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \overline{x}$ | $P_n$ = future pop, $P_0$ = base pop, $n$ = decades, $\overline{x}$ = avg decadal increment | Old, **saturated/small towns** |
| **Geometric Increase** | $P_n = P_0 \cdot (1 + r)^n$ | $r$ = geometric growth rate | **Young, rapidly growing** cities |
| **Exponential Increase** | $P_n = P_0 \cdot e^{rn}$ | $e$ = Euler's number $\approx 2.718$ | Very fast growing cities, mathematical continuity |
| **Incremental Increase** | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \overline{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \overline{y}$ | $\overline{y}$ = avg of incremental increase | **Most realistic** for medium towns |
| **Ratio Method** | $P_n = P_0 \cdot \frac{T_n}{T_0}$ | $T_n$, $T_0$ = total population of larger region at future/base period | Linked regional growth |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Warning:** In the Geometric Increase method, students often confuse the compounding period. If the growth rate $r$ is **per decade**, then $n$ = number of decades (not years). Always check units!

---

### 2.5 Sampling Techniques in Town Planning Surveys

**Probability Sampling:**
- **Simple Random Sampling** — every unit has equal chance.
- **Stratified Sampling** — area divided into homogeneous strata; preferred for town planning.
- **Systematic Sampling** — every $k^{th}$ unit selected.

**Non-Probability Sampling:**
- **Purposive Sampling** — expert selects "typical" cases.
- **Snowball Sampling** — respondents refer other respondents (useful for slum/homeless surveys).

> **Standard Reference Sample Sizes (Master Plan Surveys):**
> - Detailed urban survey: **100%** (in core areas for land use).
> - Socio-economic survey: **10%** sample.
> - Traffic O-D survey: **5–10%** sample.
> - Pilot survey: **5%** of main survey size.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Census 2011 / 2021** data forms the demographic backbone of every Indian Master Plan.
- **Bhuvan (NRSC)** provides free satellite imagery for land use mapping.
- **Google Mobility Data** and **OpenStreetMap (OSM)** are modern open-data sources.
- **Smart City Mission** uses IoT-based real-time data collection.
- **Kerala Town & Country Planning Act** mandates data collection for preparation of District Plans and Local Plans.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Geometric Increase Method (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem Statement:**
The population of a town in **2011** was **80,000**. The decadal growth rates for 1981–1991, 1991–2001, and 2001–2011 were observed as **25%, 30%, and 35%** respectively. Estimate the population in **2031** using the Geometric Increase Method. Also find the **average geometric growth rate**.

---

#### Step 1: Calculate the average geometric growth rate ($\bar{r}$)

The geometric mean of growth rates is computed as:

$$
\bar{r}_{gm} = \sqrt[n]{r_1 \cdot r_2 \cdot r_3 \cdots r_n}
$$

where $n$ = number of decades.

Substituting the values:

$$
\bar{r}_{gm} = \sqrt[3]{0.25 \times 0.30 \times 0.35}
$$

Let us compute the product step by step:

$$
0.25 \times 0.30 = 0.075
$$

$$
0.075 \times 0.35 = 0.02625
$$

Now take the cube root:

$$
\bar{r}_{gm} = (0.02625)^{1/3}
$$

$$
\bar{r}_{gm} = 0.29710 \approx 29.71\%
$$

> **Result:** Average geometric growth rate $\bar{r} \approx 29.71\%$ per decade.

---

#### Step 2: Apply the Geometric Increase formula

The standard formula is:

$$
P_n = P_0 \cdot (1 + r)^n
$$

Where:
- $P_0 = 80{,}000$ (population in 2011),
- $r = 0.2971$ (average decadal growth rate),
- $n = 2$ (from 2011 to 2031 = 2 decades).

Substituting:

$$
P_{2031} = 80{,}000 \times (1 + 0.2971)^{2}
$$

$$
P_{2031} = 80{,}000 \times (1.2971)^{2}
$$

$$
(1.2971)^{2} = 1.68247
$$

$$
P_{2031} = 80{,}000 \times 1.68247
$$

$$
P_{2031} = 134{,}597.6 \approx 1{,}34{,}598
$$

> **Final Result:** Estimated population in 2031 ≈ **1,34,598 persons**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Arithmetic Increase Method (Incremental)

**Problem Statement:**
A town had populations of:
- 1961: 25,000
- 1971: 32,000
- 1981: 40,000
- 1991: 50,000
- 2001: 62,000

Estimate the population in **2031** using the **Incremental Increase Method**.

---

#### Step 1: Calculate the decadal increment ($\overline{x}$)

$$
\overline{x} = \frac{32{,}000 - 25{,}000}{4} = \frac{7{,}000}{4} = 1{,}750
$$

Wait — using the full span (1961–2001 = 4 decades):

$$
\overline{x} = \frac{62{,}000 - 25{,}000}{4} = \frac{37{,}000}{4} = 9{,}250
$$

#### Step 2: Calculate the incremental increase ($\overline{y}$)

The increments are:
- 1961→1971: $7{,}000$
- 1971→1981: $8{,}000$
- 1981→1991: $10{,}000$
- 1991→2001: $12{,}000$

The increments' increments (differences):
- $8{,}000 - 7{,}000 = 1{,}000$
- $10{,}000 - 8{,}000 = 2{,}000$
- $12{,}000 - 10{,}000 = 2{,}000$

$$
\overline{y} = \frac{1{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 2{,}000}{3} = \frac{5{,}000}{3} \approx 1{,}666.67
$$

---

#### Step 3: Apply the Incremental Increase Formula

$$
P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \overline{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \overline{y}
$$

Here $n = 3$ (2001→2031 = 3 decades), $P_0 = 62{,}000$.

$$
P_{2031} = 62{,}000 + 3 \times 9{,}250 + \frac{3 \times 4}{2} \times 1{,}666.67
$$

$$
P_{2031} = 62{,}000 + 27{,}750 + 6 \times 1{,}666.67
$$

$$
P_{2031} = 62{,}000 + 27{,}750 + 10{,}000.02
$$

$$
P_{2031} \approx 99{,}750
$$

> **Final Result:** Estimated population in 2031 ≈ **99,750 persons**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Forecasting Methods

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table 3.1: Comparative Analysis of Population Forecasting Methods (KTU-Favorite Table)**

| Method | Mathematical Nature | Underlying Assumption | Data Required | Accuracy | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | Linear | Constant increase per decade | 2 or more past populations | Low | Old/saturated towns |
| Geometric | Compound | Constant growth rate | Past growth rates | High for young cities | Fast-growing cities |
| Exponential | Continuous compound | Continuous constant growth | Continuous growth rate | High (continuous) | Long-term projections |
| Incremental Increase | Quadratic | Both $\overline{x}$ and $\overline{y}$ constant | Multiple past decades | Moderate-High | Most realistic, KTU-favorite |
| Ratio/Corporative | Proportional | Linked regional growth | Region + city data | Variable | Regional plans |
| Logistic (S-curve) | Non-linear | Saturation/K carrying capacity | Carrying capacity estimate | Very high | Long-term saturation analysis |

---

### 3.4 KTU-Type Analytical Question: Sampling Size Determination

> **Question:** A city has 5,00,000 households. A planner wishes to conduct a 5% stratified random sample. If the city is divided into **5 zones** with household counts of 1,20,000, 1,10,000, 90,000, 1,00,000, and 80,000 respectively, determine the sample size per zone.

**Solution:**

Total sample size = $5\% \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 25{,}000$ households.

Sample per zone (proportional allocation):

$$
n_i = N_i \times \frac{n}{N}
$$

| Zone | $N_i$ (Households) | Proportion | $n_i$ (Sample) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1,20,000 | 0.24 | 6,000 |
| 2 | 1,10,000 | 0.22 | 5,500 |
| 3 | 90,000 | 0.18 | 4,500 |
| 4 | 1,00,000 | 0.20 | 5,000 |
| 5 | 80,000 | 0.16 | 4,000 |
| **Total** | **5,00,000** | **1.00** | **25,000** |

> **Result:** Zone-wise sample sizes: 6000, 5500, 4500, 5000, 4000 (proportional).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Data Collection Workflow in Town Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Planning Area & Scope] --> B[Define Data Requirements]
    B --> C{Select Data Type}
    C -->|Primary Data| D[Design Questionnaires / Schedules]
    C -->|Secondary Data| E[Compile from Existing Sources]
    D --> F[Train Enumerators / Field Staff]
    F --> G[Pilot Survey]
    G --> H[Full-Scale Field Survey]
    E --> I[Source Verification & Cross-Check]
    H --> J[Data Validation & Quality Check]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Data Coding & Tabulation]
    K --> L[Statistical Analysis]
    L --> M[Population Forecasting & GIS Mapping]
    M --> N[Preparation of Base Map & Existing Land Use Map]
    N --> O[Master Plan Formulation]
```

---

### 4.2 Architecture: Sources & Methods of Data Collection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Data Collection in Town Planning] --> B[Primary Sources]
    A --> C[Secondary Sources]
    A --> D[Modern Technological Sources]

    B --> B1[Door-to-Door Household Survey]
    B --> B2[Traffic Volume Count]
    B --> B3[Land Use Field Survey]
    B --> B4[Housing Condition Survey]
    B --> B5[Slum Survey]

    C --> C1[Census of India]
    C --> C2[District Census Handbook]
    C --> C3[Municipal Records]
    C --> C4[NSSO Reports]
    C --> C5[Town Planning Dept Archives]

    D --> D1[Remote Sensing - NRSC Bhuvan]
    D --> D2[GIS - QGIS ArcGIS]
    D --> D3[Drone Aerial Survey]
    D --> D4[GPS DGPS Surveys]
    D --> D5[IoT and Smart City Sensors]
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Types of Data Classified for Town Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Town Planning Data] --> Nature{Nature}
    Nature -->|Numerical| Q[Quantitative Data]
    Nature -->|Descriptive| QL[Qualitative Data]

    Q --> QD1[Population Counts]
    Q --> QD2[Traffic Volume in PCU]
    Q --> QD3[Land Area in Hectares]
    Q --> QD4[Income in Rupees]

    QL --> QL1[Quality of Life Index]
    QL --> QL2[Community Satisfaction Surveys]
    QL --> QL3[Visual Perception Studies]

    Start --> Source{Source}
    Source -->|First-hand| P[Primary]
    Source -->|Published| S[Secondary]

    Start --> Time{Time Dimension}
    Time -->|Single Point| CS[Cross-Sectional]
    Time -->|Multiple Periods| TS[Time Series]
```

---

### 4.4 Process Flow: Population Forecasting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Past Census Data] --> B[Compute Decadal Growth]
    B --> C{Select Method}
    C -->|Linear Growth| D1[Arithmetic Increase]
    C -->|Constant Rate| D2[Geometric Increase]
    C -->|Variable Increment| D3[Incremental Increase]
    C -->|Continuous Growth| D4[Exponential Increase]
    D1 --> E[Apply Formula]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Compute Future Population]
    F --> G[Validate with Sanity Checks]
    G --> H[Use in Master Plan Projections]
```

---

### 4.5 Hierarchical Block: Sampling Strategy Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Study Population] --> B{Sampling Type}
    B -->|Probability Sampling| C1[Simple Random]
    B -->|Probability Sampling| C2[Stratified Random]
    B -->|Probability Sampling| C3[Systematic]
    B -->|Non-Probability| D1[Purposive]
    B -->|Non-Probability| D2[Snowball]

    C1 --> E1[Best for Homogeneous Area]
    C2 --> E2[Best for Heterogeneous Town - KTU Favorite]
    C3 --> E3[Best for Systematic List]
    D1 --> E4[Best for Expert-Led Selection]
    D2 --> E5[Best for Hidden Populations - Slums]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Define "Data Collection" in town planning. List any four types of data required for the preparation of a Master Plan.**

**Model Answer (15 lines):**
Data collection is the systematic process of gathering quantitative and qualitative information about a town to form the empirical basis of planning. It is the first stage of the planning process and precedes analysis, forecasting, and plan preparation.
Four types of data required for a Master Plan:
1. **Demographic data** – population, density, age-sex distribution, literacy.
2. **Land use data** – existing land use pattern, area under each use, vacant land.
3. **Traffic and transportation data** – traffic volume, origin-destination, mode share, road network.
4. **Housing data** – housing typology, condition, occupancy, density, slum inventory.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Differentiate between primary data and secondary data with examples relevant to town planning.**

**Model Answer:**
**Primary data** is collected *first-hand* by the planner through field surveys for a specific planning purpose. It is original, current, and tailored to the planning area, but is costly and time-consuming. *Example:* A door-to-door household survey conducted by the town planning department to assess housing conditions in a ward.
**Secondary data** is data already collected and published by other agencies for different purposes. It is cheap, easily available, but may be outdated or at a different geographic scale. *Example:* Census of India data, District Census Handbook, Municipal budget documents, or NSSO reports.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern, Module 2]**
> **(a)** Explain in detail the different methods of collecting primary data for a town planning survey. (7 Marks)
> **(b)** A city had a population of 1,20,000 in 2001 and 1,68,000 in 2011. Estimate the population in 2041 using (i) Arithmetic, and (ii) Geometric Increase methods. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a): Methods of Collecting Primary Data (7 Marks)**

Primary data collection in town planning involves field-based, first-hand surveys. The main methods are:

1. **Door-to-Door Household Survey**
   The most comprehensive method. Trained enumerators visit every selected household (or sample) to collect data on family size, occupation, income, housing condition, migration, and access to services. A structured questionnaire/schedule is used. Best suited for demographic and socio-economic data.

2. **Land Use Survey**
   A field survey to map and classify the existing land use of every plot in the planning area. Categories typically include residential, commercial, industrial, public/semi-public, recreational, transportation, agricultural, and water bodies. Output is the *Existing Land Use Map (ELU)*.

3. **Traffic and Transportation Survey**
   Includes:
   - *Traffic Volume Count* (manual or automatic counters at intersections).
   - *Origin-Destination (O-D) Survey* (home-interview or roadside interview method).
   - *Speed and Delay Survey* (floating car method).
   - *Parking Survey.*
   - *Pedestrian Count.*

4. **Housing Condition Survey**
   Visual inspection of building conditions — structure, roof, walls, floor, age, maintenance. Classifies dwellings as *Good / Fair / Poor / Dilapidated*.

5. **Slum Survey**
   Special survey of notified and non-notified slums, mapping hutment locations, household numbers, facilities, tenure.

6. **Observation Method**
   The planner directly observes land use activities, building heights, signage, pedestrian movement, encroachments.

7. **Pilot Survey**
   A small-scale test survey (usually 5% of the main sample) to refine the questionnaire, train enumerators, and identify logistical issues.

> All surveys must be supported by **GIS-based mapping**, **photographic documentation**, and **GPS coordinates** for plots and facilities.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b): Population Forecast (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $P_{2001} = 1{,}20{,}000$
- $P_{2011} = 1{,}68{,}000$
- Find $P_{2041}$ (i.e., $n = 4$ decades from 2001 to 2041)

---

**(i) Arithmetic Increase Method**

Decadal increase:

$$
\overline{x} = P_{2011} - P_{2001} = 1{,}68{,}000 - 1{,}20{,}000 = 48{,}000
$$

Future population:

$$
P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \overline{x}
$$

$$
P_{2041} = 1{,}20{,}000 + 4 \times 48{,}000
$$

$$
P_{2041} = 1{,}20{,}000 + 1{,}92{,}000 = 3{,}12{,}000
$$

> **Result (Arithmetic):** $P_{2041} = 3{,}12{,}000$

---

**(ii) Geometric Increase Method**

Decadal growth rate:

$$
r = \frac{P_{2011}}{P_{2001}} - 1 = \frac{1{,}68{,}000}{1{,}20{,}000} - 1 = 1.4 - 1 = 0.40
$$

So $r = 40\%$ per decade.

Future population:

$$
P_n = P_0 \cdot (1 + r)^n
$$

$$
P_{2041} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times (1.40)^{4}
$$

Let us compute step by step:

$$
(1.40)^{2} = 1.96
$$

$$
(1.40)^{4} = (1.96)^{2} = 3.8416
$$

$$
P_{2041} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 3.8416
$$

$$
P_{2041} = 4{,}60{,}992
$$

> **Result (Geometric):** $P_{2041} \approx 4{,}60{,}992$

---

**Comparative Comment:** The Geometric method gives a higher population (4,60,992) compared to the Arithmetic method (3,12,000). This is because the Geometric method compounds growth, while the Arithmetic method assumes linear growth. For a young, growing city, the Geometric method is more realistic.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** Discuss in detail the various sources of demographic and economic data for a town planning survey. (7 Marks)
> **(b)** The populations of a town for the last five decades are: 1961 – 25,000; 1971 – 30,000; 1981 – 36,000; 1991 – 43,000; 2001 – 51,000. Estimate the population in **2031** using the **Incremental Increase Method**. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a): Sources of Demographic and Economic Data (7 Marks)**

**Sources of Demographic Data:**
1. **Census of India** – the primary source, conducted every 10 years, providing population, density, literacy, sex ratio, age distribution, migration, religion, language.
2. **District Census Handbook (DCH)** – town/village-level disaggregated census data.
3. **Town Planning Department records** – local population data from previous plans.
4. **Registrar of Births and Deaths** – annual vital statistics, birth/death rates, natural growth rate.
5. **Sample Registration System (SRS)** – provides fertility and mortality indicators.
6. **Field surveys** – door-to-door household surveys by the planning agency.
7. **National Family Health Survey (NFHS)** – demographic and health data.

**Sources of Economic Data:**
1. **Economic Census** – conducted periodically, listing non-agricultural economic activities.
2. **Census of India** – provides working population, main vs. marginal workers, occupational categories.
3. **Industries Department / DIC (District Industries Centre)** – data on registered industries, MSME units.
4. **Employment Exchange records** – registered unemployed, skill levels.
5. **Municipal / ULB records** – property tax, trade licenses, business registrations.
6. **NSSO (National Sample Survey Office)** – consumption expenditure, employment, household income.
7. **Banking and financial institutions** – credit data, savings, industrial finance.
8. **Field surveys** – sample household income and occupation surveys.

> For Master Plan preparation, **triangulation** (using two or more sources) is recommended to ensure reliability.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b): Incremental Increase Method (7 Marks)**

**Given Data:**

| Census Year | Population | Decadal Increase | Incremental Increase |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1961 | 25,000 | — | — |
| 1971 | 30,000 | 5,000 | — |
| 1981 | 36,000 | 6,000 | 1,000 |
| 1991 | 43,000 | 7,000 | 1,000 |
| 2001 | 51,000 | 8,000 | 1,000 |

---

**Step 1: Average decadal increase ($\overline{x}$)**

$$
\overline{x} = \frac{51{,}000 - 25{,}000}{4} = \frac{26{,}000}{4} = 6{,}500
$$

---

**Step 2: Average incremental increase ($\overline{y}$)**

$$
\overline{y} = \frac{1{,}000 + 1{,}000 + 1{,}000}{3} = \frac{3{,}000}{3} = 1{,}000
$$

---

**Step 3: Apply the Incremental Increase Formula**

$$
P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \overline{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \overline{y}
$$

Here $P_0 = 51{,}000$ (2001), and $n = 3$ decades (2001 → 2031).

$$
P_{2031} = 51{,}000 + 3 \times 6{,}500 + \frac{3 \times 4}{2} \times 1{,}000
$$

$$
P_{2031} = 51{,}000 + 19{,}500 + 6 \times 1{,}000
$$

$$
P_{2031} = 51{,}000 + 19{,}500 + 6{,}000
$$

$$
P_{2031} = 76{,}500
$$

> **Result:** Estimated population in 2031 ≈ **76,500 persons**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch in Geometric Method** — Mixing up *decadal rate* with *annual rate*. If $r$ is given per decade, $n$ must be the number of decades, not years.
> 2. **Sample Size Calculation** — Failing to multiply by the *proportion* for stratified sampling. Always show: $n_i = N_i \times (n / N)$.
> 3. **Missing Practical Justification** — Students give formulas but fail to mention *why* a method is chosen (e.g., "Geometric is used for young, fast-growing cities because growth is compounding, not linear"). Examiners award marks for justification.
> 4. **Forgetting Pilot Survey** — Many answers on data collection skip the *pilot survey* step, which is mandatory in the KTU-recommended workflow.
> 5. **Ignoring Secondary Data Validation** — Secondary data must be cross-verified; just copying census data without noting its limitations loses application-level marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Collection of Data**

- ✅ Data collection is the **first step** of the town planning process.
- ✅ Two main types: **Primary (first-hand)** and **Secondary (published)**.
- ✅ Master Plan data covers **demographic, economic, land use, traffic, housing, infrastructure, environment**.
- ✅ Key primary methods: **door-to-door survey, land use survey, traffic survey, housing survey, observation, pilot survey**.
- ✅ Key secondary sources: **Census of India, District Census Handbook, NSSO, Municipal records, Town Planning archives**.
- ✅ Modern tools: **Remote Sensing (Bhuvan, Cartosat), GIS, GPS/DGPS, Drones, IoT sensors**.
- ✅ Population forecasting methods: **Arithmetic, Geometric, Exponential, Incremental Increase, Ratio/Logistic**.
- ✅ Geometric mean growth rate formula: $\bar{r}_{gm} = (r_1 \cdot r_2 \cdot r_3)^{1/n}$.
- ✅ Standard Master Plan sample: **5–10% household survey**, with **stratified random sampling** as the preferred technique.
- ✅ Always validate secondary data with at least one primary check (triangulation).
- ✅ Data collected must be **mapped (GIS)**, **tabulated**, and **analyzed statistically** before being used for plan formulation.
- ✅ For KTU exams: Always draw a **flowchart or block diagram** of the data collection process — visual answers score higher.
- ✅ State the **sampling technique**, **sample size**, and **limitation** of data — these are favorite 3-mark questions.
- ✅ Use **decimal points** carefully in growth rate calculations (e.g., 25% = 0.25, not 25).
- ✅ **Closing Thought:** *In town planning, the quality of the plan can never exceed the quality of the data on which it is based.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Surveys
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types of Surveys

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Town Planning**, a **Survey** is defined as a systematic, scientific, and methodical process of collecting, recording, analyzing, and presenting data pertaining to the physical, social, economic, and environmental characteristics of an existing town or region, with the ultimate objective of preparing a rational, efficient, and sustainable **Master Plan / Development Plan**.

The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies surveys into multiple specialized categories, each designed to capture a specific dimension of urban data, forming the empirical foundation upon which all planning decisions rest.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Surveys are the *first step* in the planning process. As per the **Town and Country Planning Organisation (TCPO)** guidelines adopted in KTU references, the accuracy and comprehensiveness of survey work directly govern the validity of the resultant Master Plan.

## 1.2 Types of Surveys — Master Classification

The various types of surveys conducted for town planning are broadly classified as follows:

| S. No. | Survey Type | Primary Focus |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Topographical Survey** | Physical relief & features of land |
| 2 | **Aerial / Photographic Survey** | Bird's-eye view mapping using photographs |
| 3 | **Land Use Survey** | Existing utilization pattern of land |
| 4 | **Building Survey** | Condition, age, height, use of buildings |
| 5 | **Demographic / Population Survey** | Population size, density, distribution |
| 6 | **Socio-Economic Survey** | Income, occupation, education, community |
| 7 | **Traffic & Transportation Survey** | Volume, flow, network analysis |
| 8 | **Drainage & Sanitation Survey** | Sewerage, water supply, storm drainage |
| 9 | **Public Health / Vital Statistics Survey** | Mortality, morbidity, epidemics |
| 10 | **Engineering / Utility Services Survey** | Roads, water, power, gas lines |
| 11 | **Environmental Survey** | Pollution levels, green cover, ecology |
| 12 | **Market / Commercial Survey** | Trade, commerce, market centers |

> [!NOTE]
> All surveys are **interlinked** — a Demographic Survey relies on the boundary established by the Topographical Survey, while a Land Use Survey requires base maps generated from the Aerial Survey.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Doctor's Check-up" Model:**
> Imagine a doctor examining a patient before prescribing treatment. The doctor checks:
> - **Blood tests** (Socio-economic survey) — internal condition
> - **X-rays** (Topographical & Aerial survey) — physical structure
> - **Heart rate & ECG** (Traffic & Public Health survey) — vital functioning
> - **Patient history** (Demographic survey) — past records
>
> Similarly, a **Town Planner** conducts multiple "tests" (surveys) on a town to diagnose its problems and prescribe a Master Plan. *No single survey tells the full story; the combination does.*

## 1.4 Necessity of Surveys

The four fundamental necessities of surveys in town planning are:

1. **To study the existing conditions** of the town objectively.
2. **To identify deficiencies** in housing, transport, utilities, and amenities.
3. **To forecast future requirements** based on growth trends.
4. **To evolve a scientific, evidence-based Master Plan** that meets the present and future needs of the community.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of Each Survey Type

### A. Topographical Survey
- **Definition:** A detailed survey of the natural and artificial features of the land surface, including contours, elevations, rivers, hills, valleys, and existing structures.
- **Instruments Used:** Theodolite, Dumpy level, Total Station, GPS, LIDAR.
- **Output:** Contour maps, spot-height plans, digital terrain models (DTM).
- **Significance:** Forms the **base map** for all subsequent surveys; determines the suitability of land for various uses.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Contour intervals of **0.5 m to 1.0 m** are typically adopted for urban areas, while **2.0 m to 5.0 m** are used for regional/rural planning as per Survey of India standards.

### B. Aerial / Photographic Survey
- **Definition:** A survey conducted using aerial photographs taken from aircraft or drones, supplemented by photogrammetric interpretation.
- **Types:**
  - *Vertical photographs* — taken with the camera axis vertical.
  - *Oblique photographs* — taken with the camera axis inclined (high oblique & low oblique).
- **Scales:** Typically 1:10,000 to 1:25,000 for regional planning; 1:2,500 to 1:5,000 for town planning.
- **Advantages:** Rapid coverage of large areas, captures inaccessible terrain, time-stamped data.

### C. Land Use Survey
- **Definition:** An inventory of the existing use of every parcel of land in the town.
- **Classification (as per TCPO / URDPFI guidelines):**
  - Residential, Commercial, Industrial, Public & Semi-Public, Recreational, Transportation, Agricultural, Water Bodies, Vacant Land.
- **Method:** Door-to-door enumeration, GIS mapping, satellite imagery overlay.
- **Output:** A **Land Use Map** with color-coded zones.

### D. Building Survey
- **Definition:** A detailed study of existing buildings, recording age, height, structural condition, occupancy, and use.
- **Parameters Recorded:**
  - Plinth area, number of stories, type of construction (load-bearing/RCC), ownership.
  - Building condition: **Good / Fair / Dilapidated / Ruinous**.
- **Application:** Identifies areas needing redevelopment, conservation, or slum improvement.

### E. Demographic / Population Survey
- **Definition:** Collection of data on population characteristics — size, growth, density, distribution, age-sex composition, literacy.
- **Source:** Census of India (decennial), municipal records, sample surveys.
- **Key Indicators:** Birth rate, death rate, migration rate, density (persons per hectare).

### F. Socio-Economic Survey
- **Definition:** Study of the social and economic structure of the community.
- **Data Collected:** Income levels, occupation, religion, caste, family size, education, housing conditions.
- **Significance:** Reveals disparities, helps in zoning for affordable housing, locating community facilities.

### G. Traffic & Transportation Survey
- **Definition:** Systematic study of vehicular and pedestrian movement patterns.
- **Types:**
  - *Origin-Destination (O-D) survey* — where trips begin and end.
  - *Volume count survey* — number of vehicles at a point.
  - *Speed and delay survey* — travel time analysis.
  - *Parking survey* — demand and supply of parking.
- **Output:** Trip generation rates, modal split, traffic flow diagrams.

### H. Drainage & Sanitation Survey
- **Definition:** Study of the existing sewerage network, storm water drains, water supply lines, and solid waste management systems.
- **Parameters:** Pipe diameter, gradients, manhole locations, treatment plant capacity, coverage area (%).

### I. Public Health / Vital Statistics Survey
- **Definition:** Collection of data on health indicators — incidence of diseases, mortality rates, infant mortality, life expectancy, availability of hospitals and dispensaries.
- **Application:** Siting of health facilities, epidemic control, planning of green spaces.

### J. Engineering / Utility Services Survey
- **Definition:** Inventory of all engineered services — roads, water mains, sewer lines, electric substations, gas pipelines, telecommunication networks.
- **Output:** Composite utility map, helping planners reserve corridors for future utilities.

### K. Environmental Survey
- **Definition:** Assessment of environmental quality — air, water, and noise pollution; tree cover; water bodies; flood-prone zones.
- **Standards:** CPCB / KSPCB norms for pollutants; **PM2.5 < 40 µg/m³**, **PM10 < 60 µg/m³** (24-hour NAAQS).

### L. Market / Commercial Survey
- **Definition:** Study of existing markets — wholesale, retail, weekly, specialized (grain, cloth, cattle).
- **Output:** Identifies gaps, suggests new market zones, redevelops congested ones.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Standards | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Population Density | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | persons / hectare | Demographic Survey |
| Population Forecasting (Geometric) | $P_n = P_0 (1 + r)^n$ | persons | Future population |
| Population Forecasting (Arithmetic) | $P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$ | persons | Short-term forecast |
| Annual Growth Rate | $r = \left(\dfrac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | ratio (decimals) | Demographic Survey |
| Contour Interval | $CI = \dfrac{\text{Relief}}{\text{Number of Contours}}$ | metres | Topographical Survey |
| Scale of Aerial Photo | $S = \dfrac{f}{H}$ | dimensionless | Aerial Survey |
| Traffic Volume | $V = \dfrac{N}{t}$ | vehicles / hour | Traffic Survey |
| Trip Generation Rate | $TGR = \dfrac{T}{P}$ | trips / person / day | Traffic Survey |
| Floor Space Index | $FSI = \dfrac{\text{Total Floor Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$ | dimensionless | Building Survey |
| Building Coverage Ratio | $BCR = \dfrac{\text{Ground Floor Area}}{\text{Plot Area}} \times 100$ | percentage | Building Survey |
| Per Capita Water Supply | $q = \dfrac{Q}{P}$ | litres / person / day | Utility Survey |
| Per Capita Land | $L = \dfrac{A}{P}$ | m² / person | Land Use Survey |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In the KTU Valuation Key, **units carry 0.5 to 1 mark**. Always append units (e.g., persons/ha, l/c/d) to numerical answers. Missing units = lost marks.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Civil Engineering:** Topographical & utility surveys guide the geometric design of roads, drainage networks, and structural foundations.
- **Transportation Engineering:** Traffic surveys determine pavement design life, intersection geometry, and signal phasing.
- **Environmental Engineering:** Health and environmental surveys identify zones requiring sewage treatment plants and pollution control measures.
- **Urban Design & Architecture:** Building and land use surveys inform FAR/FSI regulations, conservation zones, and redevelopment schemes.
- **GIS & Remote Sensing:** Aerial surveys feed into GIS databases used for smart-city initiatives (e.g., Smart City Mission, AMRUT in India).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations of Key Survey Formulas

### 3.1.1 Derivation of Geometric Population Forecast

The geometric progression method assumes a **constant percentage growth rate** $r$ per decade/year.

**Step 1:** Let initial population be $P_0$ at time $t = 0$.
After one decade, population becomes:

$$P_1 = P_0 + r P_0 = P_0(1 + r)$$

**Step 2:** After two decades, applying the same growth rate to $P_1$:

$$P_2 = P_1(1 + r) = P_0(1 + r)^2$$

**Step 3:** Generalizing for $n$ decades:

$$P_n = P_0 (1 + r)^n$$

**Step 4:** Solving for the growth rate $r$ given initial $P_0$, final $P_n$, and period $n$:

$$\dfrac{P_n}{P_0} = (1 + r)^n$$

Taking the $n^{th}$ root:

$$(1 + r) = \left(\dfrac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n}$$

Therefore:

$$r = \left(\dfrac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Geometric method** is preferred for **young, rapidly growing** cities (e.g., Indian metros). The **Arithmetic method** is preferred for **saturated, old** cities (e.g., mature European towns).

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Scale of Aerial Photograph

For a vertical aerial photograph taken from height $H$ above mean sea level (or above ground datum), with a camera lens of focal length $f$:

**Step 1:** By the principle of similar triangles in camera geometry:

$$\dfrac{\text{Photo distance }(ab)}{\text{Ground distance }(AB)} = \dfrac{f}{H}$$

**Step 2:** Define the **Representative Fraction (RF) / Scale** $S$ as:

$$S = \dfrac{ab}{AB}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting:

$$S = \dfrac{f}{H}$$

**Step 4:** For a ground at elevation $h$ above datum, the **effective flying height** is $H - h$:

$$S = \dfrac{f}{H - h}$$

> [!TIP]
> For oblique photographs, the scale varies across the image; the formula becomes:
> $$S = \dfrac{f}{H} \cdot \sec \theta$$
> where $\theta$ is the tilt angle.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of Per Capita Land Requirement

**Step 1:** Let total town area be $A$ (hectares) and total population be $P$.

**Step 2:** Per capita land is:

$$L = \dfrac{A}{P} \;\;\; [\text{m}^2/\text{person}]$$

**Step 3:** Converting hectares to m² (1 ha = 10,000 m²):

$$L = \dfrac{A \times 10{,}000}{P} \;\;\; [\text{m}^2/\text{person}]$$

**Step 4:** The **standard per capita land provision** as per URDPFI guidelines is given in the table below.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

### 3.2.1 Survey Method Comparison

| Survey Type | Instrument / Tool | Output | Coverage Area | Time Required | Relative Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Topographical | Total Station, GPS, LIDAR | Contour map, DTM | Medium–Large | Medium | Medium–High |
| Aerial | Aircraft, Drone, Camera | Vertical/Oblique photos | Very Large | Low | High |
| Land Use | GIS, Field enumeration | Land-use map | Town-wide | High | Low–Medium |
| Building | Tape, Schedule, GIS | Building register | Plot-wise | High | Low |
| Demographic | Census forms, Tablets | Population tables | Town-wide | Medium | Low |
| Socio-Economic | Questionnaire, Interview | Tabulated data | Sample-based | High | Medium |
| Traffic | Counting devices, O-D forms | Flow diagrams | Network | High | Medium |
| Drainage | Manhole inspection, GPR | Sewer map | Network | Medium | Medium |
| Public Health | Hospital records, Field survey | Health index map | Town-wide | Medium | Low |
| Utility | GIS, Field tracing | Composite utility map | Town-wide | Medium | Medium |
| Environmental | Air samplers, Noise meters | Pollution maps | Point-based | Continuous | High |
| Market | Field study, Trade data | Market plan | Town-wide | Medium | Low |

### 3.2.2 Standard Per Capita Land Provisions (URDPFI Guidelines)

| Land Use Category | Per Capita Provision (m²/person) |
|---|---|
| Residential | 12 – 36 |
| Recreational (Open Space) | 4 – 6 |
| Commercial | 1.5 – 2.5 |
| Industrial | 4 – 6 |
| Transportation (Roads) | 10 – 13 |
| Public & Semi-Public | 4 – 6 |
| **Total Recommended** | **35 – 65** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These values form the **basis of density zoning** in Master Plans across India. KTU examination questions frequently use these numbers in numerical problems.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A town had a population of **50,000** in 2000 and **80,000** in 2020. Forecast the population in **2040** using the **Geometric Progression method**, and determine the per capita land if the total town area is **1,200 hectares**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the decadal growth rate $r$.

Here, $P_0 = 50{,}000$ (year 2000), $P_n = 80{,}000$ (year 2020), and $n = 2$ decades.

$$r = \left(\dfrac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1 = \left(\dfrac{80{,}000}{50{,}000}\right)^{1/2} - 1$$

$$r = (1.6)^{0.5} - 1 = 1.2649 - 1 = 0.2649$$

$$r \approx 26.49\% \text{ per decade}$$

**Step 2:** Forecast population in 2040 (i.e., 2 more decades from 2020).

$$P_{2040} = P_{2020} (1 + r)^2 = 80{,}000 \times (1.2649)^2$$

$$P_{2040} = 80{,}000 \times 1.6000 = 1{,}28{,}000 \text{ persons}$$

**Step 3:** Per capita land.

$$L = \dfrac{A \times 10{,}000}{P} = \dfrac{1{,}200 \times 10{,}000}{1{,}28{,}000}$$

$$L = \dfrac{1{,}20{,}00{,}000}{1{,}28{,}000} = 93.75 \;\text{m}^2/\text{person}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is well within the URDPFI recommended range of **35–65 m²/person** for sustainable planning. The town has surplus land and can accommodate the projected growth comfortably.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Surveys

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[TOWN PLANNING SURVEYS] --> B[PHYSICAL SURVEYS]
    A --> C[SOCIAL SURVEYS]
    A --> D[ECONOMIC SURVEYS]
    A --> E[INFRASTRUCTURE SURVEYS]
    A --> F[ENVIRONMENTAL SURVEYS]

    B --> B1[Topographical Survey]
    B --> B2[Aerial Survey]
    B --> B3[Land Use Survey]
    B --> B4[Building Survey]

    C --> C1[Demographic Survey]
    C --> C2[Socio-Economic Survey]
    C --> C3[Public Health Survey]

    D --> D1[Market Survey]
    D --> D2[Commercial Survey]
    D --> D3[Industrial Survey]

    E --> E1[Traffic and Transport Survey]
    E --> E2[Drainage and Sanitation Survey]
    E --> E3[Utility Services Survey]

    F --> F1[Air Quality Survey]
    F --> F2[Noise Pollution Survey]
    F --> F3[Water Quality Survey]
    F --> F4[Green Cover Survey]
```

## 4.2 Sequential Workflow of a Town Planning Survey

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1: Reconnaissance Visit] --> P2[Stage 2: Data Collection]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Field Measurement]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4: Data Tabulation]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5: Analysis and Interpretation]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6: Map Preparation]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 7: Report and Recommendations]
    P7 --> P8[Master Plan Formulation]
```

## 4.3 Interrelationship Between Survey Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Topographical Base Map] --> L[Land Use Map]
    T --> B[Building Register]
    T --> DR[Drainage Map]
    T --> U[Utility Map]

    L --> A[Analysis Stage]
    B --> A
    DR --> A
    U --> A
    DM[Demographic Data] --> A
    SE[Socio-Economic Data] --> A
    TR[Traffic Data] --> A
    H[Health Statistics] --> A

    A --> M[Master Plan]
    A --> Z[Zoning Regulations]
    A --> DP[Development Plan]
```

## 4.4 Aerial Survey Photogrammetry Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Flight Planning] --> F2[Camera Calibration]
    F2 --> F3[Aerial Photography]
    F3 --> F4[Photo Processing]
    F4 --> F5[Stereo Plotting]
    F5 --> F6[Map Compilation]
    F6 --> F7[Ground Control Check]
    F7 --> F8[Final Topo Map Output]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading a Mermaid Diagram:** Each box represents a *process step*; each arrow indicates the *direction of data flow*. In examination answers, a similar hand-drawn flowchart using boxes and arrows fetches **2–3 marks** in descriptive questions on surveys.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
*List any six types of surveys conducted for town planning and state the purpose of each in one line.*

**Model Answer:**

| S. No. | Survey Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Topographical Survey | To map natural and artificial features of land for base-map preparation |
| 2 | Aerial Survey | To obtain a bird's-eye photographic record of large areas quickly |
| 3 | Land Use Survey | To record the existing pattern of land utilization |
| 4 | Demographic Survey | To ascertain the present and projected population |
| 5 | Traffic Survey | To study vehicular and pedestrian movement patterns |
| 6 | Socio-Economic Survey | To assess income, occupation, and community structure |

*(1 mark for list, 1 mark for purposes, 1 mark for neat tabular format — 3 marks total)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "Contour Interval" and state the standard values used in urban and regional planning.*

**Model Answer:**
Contour interval is the vertical distance between two successive contour lines on a topographical map.

$$\text{Contour Interval} = \dfrac{\text{Total Relief of Area}}{\text{Number of Contours Drawn}}$$

**Standard Values:**
- **Urban planning:** 0.5 m to 1.0 m
- **Regional / Rural planning:** 2.0 m to 5.0 m

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark standards — 3 marks total)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and contains **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, typically of **7 marks each**. Internal choice is mandatory.

---

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**(a)** Explain in detail the **Topographical Survey** for town planning. Discuss the instruments used, the data collected, and the method of preparing a contour map. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** Describe the **Aerial Survey** method. Differentiate between vertical and oblique aerial photographs, and explain the formula for scale determination. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Topographical Survey:**

Topographical survey is the most fundamental survey that records the **relief and configuration of the land surface**, including all natural and man-made features. It forms the **base map** on which all other surveys are superimposed.

**Instruments Used:**
1. **Dumpy level** — for measuring differences in elevation.
2. **Theodolite** — for horizontal and vertical angle measurement.
3. **Total Station** — modern instrument combining EDM and angle measurement.
4. **GPS / GNSS receivers** — for fixing horizontal positions.
5. **LIDAR** — Light Detection and Ranging for high-precision 3D mapping.

**Data Collected:**
- Spot heights and benchmarks.
- Location of roads, railways, rivers, lakes, and buildings.
- Contour lines representing equal elevation.
- Existing utility corridors.

**Method of Contour Map Preparation:**

$$\text{Step 1: Establish horizontal control using triangulation / traverse.}$$
$$\text{Step 2: Establish vertical control through levelling (BM at known RL).}$$
$$\text{Step 3: Run radial / grid levelling from instrument station.}$$
\text{Step 4: Plot spot heights on a base sheet to scale.}
\text{Step 5: Interpolate contour lines between spot heights using proportional sections.}
\text{Step 6: Trace final contour map; verify in the field.}

*Mark Distribution (7 marks):* Definition — 1, Instruments — 2, Data — 2, Method — 2.

**Part (b) — Aerial Survey:**

Aerial survey involves taking **photographs of the ground from an aircraft or drone** flying at a specified altitude. It is the fastest method of covering large and inaccessible areas.

**Vertical vs Oblique Photographs:**

| Parameter | Vertical Photograph | Oblique Photograph |
|---|---|---|
| Camera axis | Vertical (perpendicular to ground) | Inclined at angle $\theta$ |
| Coverage | Small square area | Trapezoidal / strip |
| Scale | Uniform throughout | Varies — larger in foreground |
| Use | Cartographic mapping | Reconnaissance, illustrations |
| Horizon | Not visible | Visible in high oblique |
| Scale Formula | $S = \dfrac{f}{H-h}$ | $S = \dfrac{f}{H-h} \cdot \sec \theta$ |

**Scale Derivation (Vertical):** Using similar triangles in the camera geometry, the ratio of photo distance to ground distance equals the ratio of focal length to flying height above ground:

$$S = \dfrac{ab}{AB} = \dfrac{f}{H - h}$$

where $f$ = focal length, $H$ = flying height above datum, $h$ = ground elevation.

**Example:** If $f = 150$ mm and $H - h = 3000$ m, then:

$$S = \dfrac{0.150}{3000} = 1:20{,}000$$

*Mark Distribution (7 marks):* Definition — 1, Types & comparison — 3, Formula derivation — 2, Numerical example — 1.

---

### Question B (Choice 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Explain the **Demographic Survey** in detail. Discuss the various methods of population forecasting with relevant formulas. *(7 marks)*
**(b)** What is a **Traffic Survey**? Explain the different types of traffic surveys and their significance in town planning. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Demographic Survey:**

Demographic survey is the **systematic collection of data relating to population** — its size, composition, distribution, growth, and trends. It is the backbone of demand estimation for housing, water supply, transport, schools, and hospitals.

**Data Collected:**
- Total population, density, age-sex distribution.
- Birth rate, death rate, migration rate.
- Literacy rate, workforce participation.
- Family size and household structure.

**Methods of Population Forecasting:**

**(i) Arithmetic Progression Method:**

$$P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x}$$

where $\bar{x} = \dfrac{P_n - P_0}{n}$ is the average increase per decade.

**(ii) Geometric Progression Method:**

$$P_n = P_0 (1 + r)^n$$

where $r = \left(\dfrac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1$

**(iii) Incremental Increase Method:**

$$P_n = P_0 + n \cdot \bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \cdot \bar{y}$$

where $\bar{y}$ is the average incremental increase per decade.

**Comparison of Methods:**

| Method | Assumption | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| Arithmetic | Constant absolute increase | Old, saturated towns |
| Geometric | Constant percentage increase | Young, growing towns |
| Incremental Increase | Increasing absolute increase | Towns with accelerating growth |

*Mark Distribution (7 marks):* Definition — 1, Data — 1, Methods with formulas — 4, Comparison — 1.

**Part (b) — Traffic Survey:**

A traffic survey is a **systematic study of the movement of vehicles and pedestrians** on the existing road network. It forms the basis for designing the **transportation plan** of a town.

**Types of Traffic Surveys:**

| Survey Type | Data Recorded | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Volume Count | Number of vehicles per hour at a point | Road width, intersection design |
| Origin-Destination (O-D) | Trip start, end, purpose, mode | Trip distribution, network design |
| Speed and Delay | Travel time, running speed, delays | Level of service assessment |
| Parking Survey | Existing & proposed parking demand | Parking facility planning |
| Pedestrian Count | Foot-fall at intersections | Footpath, subway design |
| Accident Survey | Black spots, accident causes | Road safety improvements |

**Significance in Town Planning:**
- Determines **road hierarchy** — arterial, sub-arterial, collector, local.
- Establishes **right-of-way** and lane requirements.
- Identifies need for **bypasses, ring roads, flyovers**.
- Helps locate **public transport terminals** (bus stand, railway station, metro).
- Provides data for **traffic management** and **signal design**.

*Mark Distribution (7 marks):* Definition — 1, Types table — 3, Significance — 3.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing survey types:** Students often mix up *Aerial Survey* (uses photos) with *Land Use Survey* (uses GIS/field). Keep definitions crisp.
> 2. **Skipping units:** Writing "Population density = 100" without specifying *persons/hectare* loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the formula derivation:** In 7-mark questions on Topographical or Aerial survey, a *derivation* of the scale formula is expected, not just the result.
> 4. **Ignoring the interrelationship:** Examiners expect students to show how surveys feed into the Master Plan. A diagram or flowchart adds 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Not mentioning standards:** Always cite **URDPFI / TCPO** standards wherever applicable (per capita land, FSI, contour interval, etc.).
> 6. **Arithmetic vs Geometric confusion:** Use Geometric method for Indian cities (rapid growth); use Arithmetic only for stagnating towns.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Types of Surveys**

- ☐ **12 major survey types** are part of the KTU syllabus — Topographical, Aerial, Land Use, Building, Demographic, Socio-Economic, Traffic, Drainage, Public Health, Utility, Environmental, Market.
- ☐ **Topographical Survey** = the *base survey* — produces the contour map using Dumpy Level / Total Station / LIDAR.
- ☐ **Aerial Survey** scale formula: $S = \dfrac{f}{H-h}$ (vertical) and $S = \dfrac{f}{H-h} \cdot \sec \theta$ (oblique).
- ☐ **Land Use Survey** classifies land into Residential, Commercial, Industrial, Public-Semi Public, Recreational, Transportation, Agricultural, Water Bodies, Vacant.
- ☐ **Building Survey** records age, height, condition (Good / Fair / Dilapidated / Ruinous), use, and FSI / BCR.
- ☐ **Demographic Survey** uses three forecast methods: Arithmetic, Geometric, Incremental Increase.
- ☐ **Geometric forecast:** $P_n = P_0(1+r)^n$ — preferred for growing Indian towns.
- ☐ **Population density:** $\rho = \dfrac{P}{A}$ — units: persons/hectare.
- ☐ **Traffic Survey** types: Volume Count, O-D, Speed & Delay, Parking, Pedestrian, Accident.
- ☐ **Public Health Survey** indicators: IMR, MMR, life expectancy, morbidity rate.
- ☐ **Per capita land** (URDPFI): 35–65 m²/person total, 12–36 for residential.
- ☐ **Per capita water supply** (Indian standard): 135 l/c/d (urban), 70 l/c/d (rural).
- ☐ **Contour interval:** 0.5–1.0 m (urban), 2.0–5.0 m (regional).
- ☐ Always remember to write **units** in numerical answers.
- ☐ Always draw a **flowchart** when asked "list the surveys" — it fetches bonus marks.
- ☐ Surveys are the **first step** of the planning process: Survey → Analysis → Master Plan → Implementation → Review.
- ☐ TCPO = Town and Country Planning Organisation; URDPFI = Urban Development Plans Formulation and Implementation guidelines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Mantra for Module 2:**
> *"A planner who surveys well, plans well. A planner who plans well, builds a livable city."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
