# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**Q. Define slums and list any four major causes of slum formation in Indian cities.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition:** A slum is a compact, congested, and environmentally degraded urban informal settlement characterized by substandard housing, lack of basic services (water, sanitation, drainage), overcrowding, and insecure land tenure. The UN-Habitat operational definition identifies a slum household as one lacking durable housing, sufficient living area, safe water, adequate sanitation, or secure tenure.

**Four Major Causes:**

1. **Rural-urban migration** driven by agrarian distress and urban pull factors.
2. **Inadequate affordable housing** for the Economically Weaker Sections (EWS).
3. **Weak enforcement of Master Plans** and zoning regulations.
4. **High cost of formal housing** exceeding the affordability benchmark of **30% of household income**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tip:** A 3-mark answer requires *one* precise definition and *three to four* crisp points. Avoid long paragraphs; use bullets.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**Q. Explain the push-pull factors responsible for rural-urban migration that lead to slum growth.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Push Factors (Rural):** These *force* people out of villages. Examples include crop failure, drought, debt traps, rural unemployment, lack of schools/colleges, caste discrimination, and inadequate healthcare.

> **Pull Factors (Urban):** These *attract* people to cities. Examples include higher wages, perceived better lifestyle, availability of menial and informal jobs, social networks, and access to services.

> **Convergence → Slum Growth:** When push factors in source villages intensify *and* pull factors in destination cities remain strong, migration surges. Migrants, unable to afford formal housing, occupy vacant public land, riverbeds, and nallah margins, which **consolidate into slums** over time.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module-end exam contains a 14-mark question with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. The model answers below follow the **valuation-key style** — each sub-question answered with a 5–7 line structure: definition → classification → explanation → example → conclusion.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]**

**Q. (a) Discuss in detail the economic causes of slum formation in Indian cities. (7 marks)**
**Q. (b) Explain the role of government policies and governance failures in the creation and persistence of slums. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.(a):**

**1. Definition & Context:** Slum formation in India is fundamentally an economic phenomenon rooted in the **structural mismatch between housing supply and the income levels of the urban poor**.

**2. Major Economic Causes:**

- **Rural Agrarian Distress & Poverty:** Small and marginal farmers, unable to sustain themselves due to crop failure, debt, and rising input costs, migrate to cities in search of wage labour.
- **Inadequate Affordable Housing Stock:** Developers prefer premium housing (high ROI); the EWS and LIG segments are systematically neglected.
- **High Land Prices:** Land cost in Indian metros (₹50,000–₹5,00,000 per sq.m) makes formal housing unaffordable for those earning below ₹25,000/month.
- **Informal Sector Dependence:** Over **80% of urban workers** are in the informal sector; their income is irregular and low, restricting housing choice to slums.
- **Housing Affordability Ratio Failure:** When rent exceeds **30% of income**, formal housing is rejected and informal housing is chosen by default.
- **Lack of Credit Access:** Slum-dwellers have no formal property titles and cannot access bank loans for housing.

**3. Example:** Mumbai's Dharavi, despite being in India's financial capital, houses over **1 million people** in 2.1 sq.km — an economic logic of affordable informal rental housing for industrial labour.

**4. Conclusion:** Economic causes are the *most immediate* drivers of slum formation; without addressing them, other policy interventions remain cosmetic.

#### **Model Solution for Q.(b):**

**1. Introduction:** Government policies and governance structures can either *prevent* or *perpetuate* slums. In India, multiple policy failures have inadvertently *encouraged* slum proliferation.

**2. Policy & Governance Failures:**

- **Ineffective Master Plans:** Most Indian cities have not updated their Master Plans for decades; even where updated, **development control rules are not enforced** in low-income areas.
- **Rent Control Acts (Paradoxical Effect):** Intended to protect tenants, these laws have *discouraged* private rental construction since the 1950s, reducing the supply of affordable formal housing.
- **Corrupt Land Administration:** Patta issuance is delayed by decades; encroachment of government land is patronized by local politicians.
- **Slum Clearance without Rehabilitation:** Pre-2000 policies (e.g., Mumbai's slum clearance drives) displaced lakhs of slum-dwellers but did not provide alternative housing, pushing them to *new* slums.
- **Inadequate In-situ Upgrading:** Schemes like **JNNURM** and **Rajiv Awas Yojana** had limited coverage and poor implementation; the *PMAY-U* is the latest attempt.
- **Politicization of Slums:** Vote-bank politics leads to *tolerance* of encroachments and *delay* of formalization.

**3. Example:** The **Delhi Development Authority (DDA)**'s failure to provide EWS housing led to the proliferation of **over 1,800 unauthorised colonies** in Delhi.

**4. Conclusion:** Strong, transparent governance and **predictable tenure formalization** are essential to break the slum-creation cycle. Policy must shift from *eviction* to *enablement*.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]**

**Q. (a) Analyze the demographic and social factors leading to slum proliferation. (7 marks)**
**Q. (b) Discuss the impact of rapid urbanization and industrialization on slum development. (7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for Q.(a):**

**1. Introduction:** Demographic and social factors form the *invisible architecture* of slum formation — they determine *who* migrates, *how many* migrate, and *where* they cluster.

**2. Demographic Factors:**

- **Rural-Urban Migration Volume:** India has witnessed an unprecedented rural-to-urban migration, contributing to **40% of urban population growth** between 2001–2011.
- **Chain Migration:** A successful pioneer migrant pulls relatives and community members, creating *ethnic clusters* in slums (e.g., Bihari workers in Surat, Tamil labourers in Mumbai).
- **High Total Fertility Rate (TFR):** Slum TFR (~3.5) exceeds national TFR (~2.2), causing organic growth of slum populations independent of migration.
- **Youthful Migration:** Most migrants are in the 18–35 age group, leading to high birth rates in slums.

**3. Social Factors:**

- **Caste-based Segregation:** Lower castes (SC/ST communities) are historically confined to peripheral habitats, often the *oldest* slums in Indian cities.
- **Ethnic & Linguistic Clustering:** Migrants cluster by language/region for *social security*, leading to *cultural pockets* (e.g., Bhojpuri speakers in Dharavi, Bengali speakers in Bangalore slums).
- **Lack of Education & Skills:** Multi-generational educational deprivation perpetuates slum residency.
- **Gender Disparities:** Low female workforce participation keeps households in poverty.

**4. Example:** The **Kathputli Colony in Delhi** is a centuries-old caste-based slum of wooden-toy makers (Kumhar caste), illustrating *intergenerational* social causation.

**5. Conclusion:** Demographic and social factors *interlock* with economic causes. Effective slum policy must address **mobility, education, and social inclusion**, not just housing.

#### **Model Solution for Q.(b):**

**1. Introduction:** Rapid urbanization and industrialization are the *macro-engines* of slum formation in developing economies. Indian cities have urbanized faster than their housing supply can match.

**2. Urbanization Impact:**

- **Decadal Urban Growth:** India's urban population grew from **28.6 crore (2001)** to **37.7 crore (2011)** — a 31.8% rise in one decade.
- **Housing Supply Lag:** The formal housing sector constructed only **6–7 lakh dwelling units per year** in the EWS/LIG segment, against an annual demand of **1 crore units**.
- **Peri-Urban Sprawl:** Rapid urbanization has caused *ribbon development* and *land-use conversion* from agriculture to informal settlements.
- **Service Infrastructure Deficit:** Water supply, sewerage, and roads have not kept pace with population growth in peri-urban areas.

**3. Industrialization Impact:**

- **Labour Cluster Formation:** Industrial corridors (e.g., Gurgaon–Manesar, Surat, Tirupur, Ludhiana) attract massive informal labour forces.
- **Inadequate Worker Housing:** Industrial employers rarely provide housing; workers *self-create* slums in adjacent areas.
- **Pollution-Induced Displacement:** Industrial pollution has displaced traditional fishing/farming communities into urban slums.
- **Example:** **Tirupur (Tamil Nadu)** — India's knitwear capital — has over **50,000 micro-units** employing informal labour who live in surrounding slums.

**4. Example (Macro):** **Greater Mumbai** urbanized from 5.7 million (1971) to **12.4 million (2011)**. Slum population crossed **9 million** (62% of city population) — a direct consequence of rapid urbanization without proportional housing supply.

**5. Conclusion:** Slums are the *spatial signature* of rapid urbanization and industrialization that is not matched by **planned, affordable, and inclusive housing policies**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Generic Answers:** Writing "poverty causes slums" without classifying causes (push/pull/structural/demographic) loses 50% of marks.
> 2. **No Examples:** Every KTU 14-mark answer must include **at least one real-world Indian example** (Dharavi, Kathputli Colony, Tirupur, Surat, etc.). A purely theoretical answer is capped at 10–11 marks.
> 3. **No Policy Linkage:** Causes must be linked to *what they imply for planning policy* — eviction vs. in-situ upgrading, rent control, EWS housing.
> 4. **No Quantitative Anchoring:** Always quote a benchmark (TFR 3.5, rent < 30% of income, persons/room ≤ 3, 80% informal sector). Quantitative answers score higher.
> 5. **Diagram Skipping:** A 14-mark answer should ideally include a **classified list or causal chain diagram**. Even a simple bullet hierarchy shows structured thinking and earns the full 14 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Slum Definition (UN-Habitat, 5 Deficits):** Durable housing, sufficient living area, safe water, sanitation, secure tenure — **memorize verbatim**.
- **Slum Population Ratio Formula:** $\text{SPR} = P_s / P_c \times 100$.
- **Affordability Benchmark:** Rent should be **≤ 30%** of monthly income.
- **Push Factors:** Rural distress, debt, drought, caste, lack of services.
- **Pull Factors:** Urban wages, jobs, networks, services.
- **Chain Migration:** Pioneer migrant → relatives → ethnic cluster → slum.
- **Rent Control Acts:** Intended to protect, *actually reduced* rental supply → push to slums.
- **Slum Clearance vs. In-situ Upgrading:** Old policy = eviction (failed); New policy = PMAY-U in-situ upgrading.
- **Intergenerational Slum Trap:** $S_{t+1} = S_t (1 + g_n + g_m) - H_s \delta$. When $\delta$ is low, slums grow.
- **India's Stat:** ~65 million slum-dwellers (2011); ~17% of urban population.
- **Macro Example:** Mumbai = 62% slum population (Dharavi alone: 1 million in 2.1 sq.km).
- **Key Schemes to Know:** JNNURM, RAY, PMAY-U, Smart Cities Mission.
- **Six Cause Categories:** Economic, Demographic, Political, Social, Infrastructural, Legal-Tenurial.
- **Push-Pull Equation:** $M = f(P_u, P_r, I, O)$ — Lee (1966).
- **Causal Chain Stages:** Rural Distress → Migration → Housing Rejection → Informal Occupation → Consolidation → Intergenerational Trap.
- **PPR Threshold:** $\leq 3$ persons/room = not a slum; $> 5$ = severe slum.
- **Always Quote Examples:** Dharavi (Mumbai), Kathputli Colony (Delhi), Tirupur (TN), Surat (Gujarat).
- **Final Mantra for Examiners:** *Slums are not the cause of urban problems — they are the visible symptom of deeper planning failures.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics and Effects of Slums
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Effects of Slums

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition — Slum**
> A **slum** is a contiguous settlement of at least 20 households (or 60–70 households per the **Census of India, 2011** threshold), characterised by structurally weak, overcrowded, and unserviced dwellings in an unhygienic environment, lacking one or more of the following five basic conditions: **(i) durable housing structure, (ii) sufficient living area, (iii) access to improved drinking water, (iv) access to improved sanitation, and (v) secure tenure** (UN-Habitat Operational Definition, 2003).

According to the **Census of India 2011**, a slum is defined as *"a compact area of at least 300 persons or about 60–70 households of poorly built congested tenements, in an unhygienic environment, with inadequate infrastructure and lacking proper sanitary and drinking water facilities."*

Statutory recognition in India is provided by the **Slum Areas (Improvement and Clearance) Act, 1956**, which empowers state governments to declare any area as a "slum area" if it satisfies prescribed conditions of unfit habitation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuitive Understanding

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Sponge City**
> Imagine a healthy city is a well-organised library — books are properly catalogued, aisles are clean, and air circulates freely. A city infected with slums is like a library that has stacks of water-soaked, termite-ridden books dumped randomly in a damp basement corner. The basement walls weep moisture, the books rot, mould spreads to nearby books, and pests (analogous to disease, crime, and pollution) start crawling upstairs and damaging the entire library. **Slums are the damp basements of a city** — they don't just harm themselves; their *negative externalities* seep upward and degrade the entire urban organism. Town planners must therefore either *dry the basement* (in-situ upgrading) or *remove the rot* (slum clearance + rehabilitation).

---

### 1.3 The "Five Deficiencies" — UN-Habitat Slum Household Criteria

A household is classified as a **slum household** if it lacks **one or more** of the following durable conditions:

| # | Deficiency | Standard Benchmark |
|---|------------|--------------------|
| 1 | **Durable Housing** | Permanent structure able to withstand climatic stresses; walls of brick/concrete/stone; roof of concrete/tiles |
| 2 | **Sufficient Living Area** | ≤ 3 persons per habitable room (overcrowding threshold) |
| 3 | **Improved Drinking Water** | Piped water, public tap, borehole, protected well/spring within 200 m |
| 4 | **Improved Sanitation** | Private or shared flush/pour-flush toilet, ventilated improved pit latrine |
| 5 | **Secure Tenure** | De jure (legal) or de facto (customary/undisputed for ≥ 20 years) protection from forced eviction |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Fact (KTU 2024):** Per **Sustainable Development Goal 11.1** (UN), by **2030**, all countries must ensure access to adequate, safe, and affordable housing for all, with special focus on slum upgrading. This is a direct question expectation in KTU Board Exams.

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Town Planning

Town planning discipline treats slums as a **planning pathology** — a symptom of failed land-use planning, inadequate housing finance, unregulated migration, and weak governance. The 2024 KTU syllabus places this under **Urban Renewal and Redevelopment** and demands understanding of:
- The *physical morphology* of slums (characteristics)
- The *externality burden* on the host city (effects)
- The *planning response hierarchy* (do nothing → improve → upgrade → redevelop)</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Slum Characteristics

KTU 2024 expects students to classify slum characteristics under **four clear dimensions**:

#### A. Physical / Morphological Characteristics
- **Substandard housing stock** — Kutcha (mud, thatch, tarpaulin, tin-sheet) walls and roofs with a **structural life of < 15 years**.
- **Severe overcrowding** — Density often exceeds **500–1,000 persons per hectare (PPH)**, against the **URDPFI (2015) recommended density of 250 PPH** for low-income EWS housing.
- **Narrow, winding gully lanes** — Often **< 1.8 m wide**, blocking fire-tender and emergency access.
- **Inadequate natural light and ventilation** — Habitable rooms may have **window-to-floor ratio < 1:12** (NBC 2016 standard: minimum 1:8).
- **Mixed incompatible land uses** — Residential, commercial (sometimes polluting small-scale industries), and religious uses intermingled.
- **Absence of setbacks and building lines** — Zero front, rear, and side margins.

#### B. Environmental / Infrastructure Characteristics
- **Lack of potable water** — Shared community taps serving 50–200 families; **per capita supply < 40 lpcd** (litres per capita per day) versus **135 lpcd** norm (CPCB).
- **Open defecation** or shared insanitary latrines.
- **No underground drainage** — Surface wastewater flows in open nullahs, creating breeding grounds for *Aedes aegypti* mosquitoes (dengue vector).
- **Solid waste accumulation** — Door-to-door collection absent; municipal collection frequency < 1× per week.
- **Pollution hotspots** — Indoor air pollution from biomass cooking; ambient air pollution from nearby industrial/vehicular sources.

#### C. Socio-Economic Characteristics
- **Extreme poverty** — Household income often below **₹25,000/month in metropolitan cities** (Tendulkar poverty line: ₹32/day rural, ₹47/day urban).
- **Low literacy** — Adult female literacy as low as **30–40%** in some notified slums.
- **Informal economy dependence** — Rag-pickers, street vendors, domestic workers, construction labourers.
- **Tenure insecurity** — No formal property title; constant threat of eviction.
- **Social exclusion and marginalisation** — Caste-, religion-, and gender-based discrimination.

#### D. Health & Hygiene Characteristics
- **High Infant Mortality Rate (IMR)** — In some Indian slums, **IMR > 50/1,000 live births** (national urban average: 23).
- **Vector-borne disease prevalence** — Malaria, dengue, chikungunya, tuberculosis.
- **Stunting and malnutrition in children** — Often > 35% in deep-poverty slums.
- **Mental health stress** — Cramped conditions correlate with anxiety, depression, domestic violence.

---

### 2.2 Effects of Slums — Analytical Framework

The effects are best understood as a **three-tier externality model**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Externality Concept in Town Planning**
> An *externality* is a cost or benefit imposed on a third party who is not part of the transaction. Slums generate **negative externalities** that fall on (a) the slum dwellers themselves, (b) the adjacent city, and (c) the macro-economy.

#### Tier 1 — Effects on Slum Dwellers (Internal)
| Effect | Mechanism | Metric |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| **Health degradation** | Pathogen exposure + malnutrition | IMR, MMR, DALY |
| **Educational deprivation** | Child labour, no nearby schools | Dropout rate > 50% |
| **Psychological trauma** | Insecurity, crowding stress | Domestic violence index |
| **Economic immobility** | No address proof → no bank account, no formal credit | Financial exclusion % |

#### Tier 2 — Effects on the Host City (External)
- **Strain on civic infrastructure** — Overloaded sewerage, water supply, transport, and power grids.
- **Public health burden** — Slum-origin epidemics spread to the wider city (COVID-19, TB, dengue).
- **Traffic congestion** — Encroachment on footpaths and roads.
- **Environmental degradation** — Open drains, river pollution (e.g., Yamuna, Mithi, Periyar, Coovum).
- **Fire and structural-collapse hazards** — High-density kutcha construction is a *tinderbox*.
- **Loss of property tax revenue** — Informal tenures escape the tax net.
- **Visual blight and urban-image damage** — Affects tourism, FDI perception.

#### Tier 3 — Effects on the Macro-Economy (Systemic)
- **Reduced urban productivity** — Sick, malnourished, uneducated workforce lowers GDP.
- **Welfare expenditure burden** — Public health and subsidy costs.
- **Urban-rural push-back** — Unmet aspirations fuel reverse migration or social unrest.
- **Vicious cycle of poverty** — Slums reproduce slums across generations (intergenerational poverty trap).

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are essential for KTU 2024 ESE numerical and analytical questions on slums, housing density, and redevelopment feasibility.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Standard / Unit | Application |
|---|------------------|------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Gross Residential Density (GRD)** | $\text{GRD} = \dfrac{N}{A_{net}}$ | persons/hectare | Slum density assessment |
| 2 | **Net Residential Density (NRD)** | $\text{NRD} = \dfrac{N}{A_{site}}$ | persons/hectare | Re-housing design |
| 3 | **Plot Coverage Ratio (FAR / FSI)** | $\text{FAR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Built-up Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$ | dimensionless | Slum redevelopment FSI normally 1.5–2.5 |
| 4 | **Floor Space Index (FSI) Limit** | $\text{FSI} = \dfrac{\text{Total Floor Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$ | dimensionless | TDR/additional FSI for rehab |
| 5 | **Persons Per Room (PPR) Index** | $\text{PPR} = \dfrac{\text{Household Size}}{\text{Number of Habitable Rooms}}$ | persons/room | UN overcrowding threshold = 3 |
| 6 | **Slum Household Density (SHD)** | $\text{SHD} = \dfrac{\text{No. of Slum HHs}}{\text{Slum Area (ha)}}$ | HHs/hectare | Census of India reports SHD = 125–400 |
| 7 | **Per Capita Water Supply** | $\text{Q} = \dfrac{\text{Total Water Supplied (litres/day)}}{\text{Population Served}}$ | lpcd (litres per capita per day) | Slum norm < 40 lpcd vs CPCB norm 135 lpcd |
| 8 | **Sanitation Coverage Ratio** | $\text{SCR} = \dfrac{\text{HHs with Toilet Access}}{\text{Total HHs}} \times 100$ | percent | UN target = 100% |
| 9 | **Replacement Cost (Re-settlement)** | $\text{C}_{rehab} = \text{FAR} \times \text{Plot Area} \times \text{Unit Construction Cost}$ | ₹ (Rupees) | Slum-free city plan budgeting |
| 10 | **Cross-Subsidy Ratio (Developer Model)** | $\text{CSR} = \dfrac{\text{Free Sale Component Area}}{\text{Rehab Component Area}}$ | dimensionless | PPP/PPP-PPP slum redevelopment |
| 11 | **Window-to-Floor Ratio (Light)** | $\text{WFR} = \dfrac{\text{Total Window Glass Area}}{\text{Floor Area of Room}}$ | dimensionless | NBC 2016 minimum = 1/8 |
| 12 | **Slum Prevalence Rate** | $\text{SPR} = \dfrac{\text{Slum Population}}{\text{Total City Population}} \times 100$ | percent | National average ≈ 17% (Census 2011) |

> **Memory Anchor:** Remember the **"3-1-8" rule of thumb** — 3 persons per room, 1 toilet per family, 80% slum prevalence is concentrated in **Tier-1 cities** of India (Mumbai, Delhi, Kolkata, Chennai).

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Town Planning Practice

- **Slum-Free City Plan of India (PMAY-U)** uses the **5-point UN deficiency** to declare a settlement "slum-free" only when **all five** are satisfied.
- **Rajiv Awas Yojana (RAY)** and **Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana — Urban (PMAY-U)** deploy *in-situ* slum redevelopment with **free 25 m² carpet area dwellings** for EWS families.
- **Maharashtra Slum Areas (Improvement, Clearance and Redevelopment) Act, 1971** uses density and structural-life thresholds to define *kutcha* vs *pucca* slums.
- **In Kerala (KTU context):** The **Kerala State Slum Clearance Board** and **Life Mission (2020)** integrate slum upgradation with **housing, water (KWA), sanitation (Suchitwa Mission), and livelihood (Kudumbashree)** components.</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Analytical Problem — Slum Density and Redevelopment Feasibility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sample KTU 2024 Numerical Question Pattern**
> *A notified slum in Kochi city occupies 4.2 hectares. A recent door-to-door survey reports 1,260 households with an average household size of 4.5 persons. The State Slum Board proposes in-situ redevelopment at a permissible FSI of 2.5 with 35% of the gross area dedicated to rehabilitation, 15% to amenities & open space, and 50% to free-sale (cross-subsidy). Compute: (a) Existing gross residential density, (b) Persons-per-room index assuming average 1.4 habitable rooms per HH, (c) Built-up area available for rehabilitation housing under the redevelopment model.*

---

#### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Given Data:**
- Slum area: $A = 4.2 \text{ ha} = 42{,}000 \text{ m}^2$
- Number of households: $H = 1{,}260$
- Average household size: $s = 4.5$ persons
- Habitable rooms per HH (assumed): $r = 1.4$ rooms
- Permissible FSI: $F = 2.5$
- Rehabilitation proportion: $p_{rehab} = 0.35$

**Step 1 — Compute Slum Population (N)**

$$
N = H \times s
$$

$$
N = 1{,}260 \times 4.5
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 5{,}670 \text{ persons}}
$$

> *Textual logic:* Total population equals the number of households multiplied by the mean household size obtained from the socio-economic survey.

**Step 2 — Compute Existing Gross Residential Density (GRD)**

$$
\text{GRD} = \dfrac{N}{A} = \dfrac{5{,}670 \text{ persons}}{4.2 \text{ ha}}
$$

$$
\text{GRD} = 1{,}350 \text{ persons/hectare}
$$

> *Textual logic:* GRD expresses the population load per unit land area. The result of 1,350 PPH is **5.4 times** the URDPFI-recommended 250 PPH for low-income housing, confirming **extreme overcrowding** typical of a notified slum.

**Step 3 — Compute Persons-Per-Room (PPR) Index**

$$
\text{PPR} = \dfrac{s}{r} = \dfrac{4.5}{1.4}
$$

$$
\text{PPR} = 3.21 \text{ persons/room}
$$

> *Textual logic:* The UN-Habitat overcrowding threshold is 3.0 persons/room. Since 3.21 > 3.0, **the slum qualifies as severely overcrowded**, failing the *Sufficient Living Area* deficiency criterion.

**Step 4 — Compute Total Permissible Built-up Area (B_total)**

$$
B_{total} = F \times A = 2.5 \times 42{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
B_{total} = 1{,}05{,}000 \text{ m}^2
$$

> *Textual logic:* FSI is the ratio of total constructed floor area to plot area. Multiply FSI by the plot area to obtain the maximum floor space the development can legally create.

**Step 5 — Compute Rehabilitation Built-up Area (B_rehab)**

$$
B_{rehab} = p_{rehab} \times B_{total} = 0.35 \times 1{,}05{,}000
$$

$$
B_{rehab} = 36{,}750 \text{ m}^2
$$

> *Textual logic:* Under the **35% cross-subsidy-free rehabilitation quota**, 36,750 m² is reserved for re-housing the 1,260 slum households.

**Step 6 — Compute Per-Household Carpet Area in the Rehabilitation Component**

$$
A_{per HH} = \dfrac{B_{rehab}}{H} = \dfrac{36{,}750}{1{,}260}
$$

$$
A_{per HH} \approx 29.17 \text{ m}^2 \text{ per HH}
$$

> *Textual logic:* This **comfortably exceeds the PMAY-U mandate of 25 m² carpet area** per EWS household, demonstrating the feasibility of the in-situ redevelopment model for this slum pocket.

**Step 7 — Compute Slum Prevalence Rate for the Ward (Illustrative)**

If the ward population $P_{ward} = 32{,}000$:

$$
\text{SPR} = \dfrac{N}{P_{ward}} \times 100 = \dfrac{5{,}670}{32{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{SPR} \approx 17.72\%
$$

> *Textual logic:* Comparable to the **national urban slum prevalence average of 17.4%** per Census 2011.

---

### 3.2 Summary Table — All Numerical Outputs

| Parameter | Symbol | Computed Value | Benchmark | Verdict |
|-----------|--------|----------------|-----------|---------|
| Slum Population | $N$ | **5,670 persons** | — | — |
| Gross Density | $\text{GRD}$ | **1,350 PPH** | 250 PPH | 5.4× overloaded |
| Persons/Room | $\text{PPR}$ | **3.21** | 3.0 | Overcrowded |
| Total Buildable | $B_{total}$ | **1,05,000 m²** | — | — |
| Rehab Built-up | $B_{rehab}$ | **36,750 m²** | — | — |
| Per-HH Carpet | $A_{perHH}$ | **29.17 m²** | 25 m² (PMAY-U) | Compliant |
| Slum Prevalence | $\text{SPR}$ | **17.72 %** | 17.4 % (India 2011) | At par |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cause → Characteristic → Effect → Planning Response

| Slum Cause | Surface Characteristic | Cascade Effect | Appropriate Planning Response |
|------------|------------------------|----------------|--------------------------------|
| Rural–urban migration | Demographic concentration | Strain on transport, schools | **Town Planning Scheme** with satellite townships |
| Rural–urban migration | Substandard housing | Public-health emergency | **In-situ upgradation** with PMAY-U |
| Poverty | Insanitary toilets | Open defecation → disease | **Swachh Bharat Mission (SBM-U)** |
| Land speculation | Encroachment on public land | Encroachment of nallahs/footpaths | **Slum Clearance Act enforcement + survey** |
| Industrial proximity | Air/water pollution | Respiratory disease | **Land-use separation + EWS housing buffer** |
| Tenure insecurity | No address proof | Financial exclusion | **Patta (title) distribution** |
| Failure of rental market | Forced squatting | Density > 1,000 PPH | **Affordable rental housing (ARH) under PMAY-U 2.0** |
| Political patronage | Continued neglect | Clientelism | **Participatory planning + community contracting** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Tip:** In 14-mark answers, the **"4-C" structure** (Cause → Characteristic → Consequence → Counter-measure) is the safest high-scoring pattern. Use it for any descriptive question on slums.</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Slum Generation to Planning Response

> The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the systemic flow from root causes of slum formation to the planning interventions deployed by the KTU-relevant town planning framework. The architecture mirrors the **Cause → Characteristic → Effect → Response** chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Rural Poverty] --> M1[Rural-Urban Migration]
    R2[Land Speculation] --> M2[Encroachment of Public Land]
    R3[Industrialization] --> M3[Forced Displacement]
    R4[Failure of Rental Market] --> M4[Forced Squatting]
    M1 --> S1[Slum Formation]
    M2 --> S1
    M3 --> S1
    M4 --> S1
    S1 --> C1[Substandard Housing]
    S1 --> C2[Overcrowding]
    S1 --> C3[Poor Sanitation]
    S1 --> C4[Tenure Insecurity]
    C1 --> E1[Health Burden]
    C2 --> E1
    C3 --> E1
    C1 --> E2[Urban Service Strain]
    C3 --> E2
    C4 --> E3[Financial Exclusion]
    E1 --> I1[In-Situ Upgradation - PMAY-U]
    E2 --> I1
    E1 --> I2[Slum Clearance and Relocation]
    E2 --> I3[Site and Services Schemes]
    E3 --> I4[Title Regularisation - Patta Distribution]
    I1 --> O1[Slum-Free City]
    I2 --> O1
    I3 --> O1
    I4 --> O1
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The flow moves left-to-right from **Root Causes (R-series)** to **Migration/Encroachment/Displacement/Squatting (M-series)** to **Slum Formation (S1)** to **Characteristics (C-series)** to **Effects (E-series)** to **Interventions (I-series)** finally converging to the **Slum-Free City** outcome (O1). This is the model diagram to reproduce in KTU theory answers.

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Five Deficiency Diagnosis System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Input[Slum Household Survey Data]
        D1[Housing Material]
        D2[Room Count]
        D3[Water Source]
        D4[Toilet Access]
        D5[Tenure Status]
    end
    subgraph Engine[UN-Habitat 5-Deficiency Engine]
        E1[Durability Check]
        E2[Overcrowding Check]
        E3[Water Access Check]
        E4[Sanitation Check]
        E5[Tenure Security Check]
    end
    subgraph Output[Diagnostic Output]
        O1[Slum Household Flag]
        O2[Deficiency Type and Count]
        O3[Intervention Priority]
    end
    D1 --> E1 --> O1
    D2 --> E2 --> O1
    D3 --> E3 --> O1
    D4 --> E4 --> O1
    D5 --> E5 --> O1
    E1 --> O2
    E2 --> O2
    E3 --> O2
    E4 --> O2
    E5 --> O2
    O2 --> O3
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **Input subgraph** is the data layer; the **Engine subgraph** applies the five UN-Habitat tests; the **Output subgraph** produces a decision flag, deficiency classification, and intervention priority. Town planners use this logic in **GIS-based slum mapping** under the **Slum-Free City Plan of Action (SFCPoA)**.

---

### 4.3 Hierarchy of Slum Planning Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Slum-Affected Household] --> A1{Land Available for Relocation?}
    A1 -- No --> A2[In-Situ Upgradation]
    A1 -- Yes --> A3{Slum Density PPH?}
    A3 -- Greater than 800 --> A4[Slum Redevelopment - Vertical]
    A3 -- 400 to 800 --> A5[Slum Redevelopment - Mixed]
    A3 -- Less than 400 --> A6[Slum Improvement - In-Situ]
    A2 --> A7[Self-Help Housing - PMAY-U ISSR]
    A4 --> A8[Public-Private-Partnership PPP Model]
    A5 --> A8
    A6 --> A9[Community Participation - CBOs]
    A7 --> A10[Slum-Free Sustainable Habitation]
    A8 --> A10
    A9 --> A10
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the **decision-tree** town planners follow when choosing an intervention. Notice that *in-situ upgradation* is preferred when relocation land is unavailable, and *PPPs* dominate the higher-density scenarios. The terminal node is the **sustainable slum-free habitation**, the ultimate goal of PMAY-U and SDG 11.1.</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**  
> *Define a slum as per the Census of India, 2011. List any two characteristics of slums.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** As per the *Census of India 2011*, a slum is *"a compact area of at least 300 persons or about 60–70 households of poorly built, congested tenements in unhygienic surroundings, with inadequate infrastructure and lacking proper sanitary and drinking water facilities."*
- **Two characteristics (½ mark each):**
  1. **Physical:** Substandard kutcha housing and severe overcrowding (>500 PPH density).
  2. **Socio-economic:** Widespread poverty, illiteracy, and informal-economy dependence.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**  
> *Enumerate any three effects of slums on the host city.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Strain on civic infrastructure** — Overloaded drainage, water supply, and transport networks (1 mark).
2. **Public health spillover** — Vector-borne and communicable diseases spreading from slums to the wider city (1 mark).
3. **Environmental degradation** — Open nallahs, river pollution, and visual blight (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A — Slum Characteristics and Remedial Measures (14 Marks)**  
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question Pattern]**

**(a) Classify the characteristics of slums under four dimensions. Explain each with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Physical/Morphological Characteristics (2 marks):**
- Substandard kutcha housing (mud walls, thatched/tin roofs) with structural life < 15 years.
- Severe overcrowding, often > 1,000 PPH versus the URDPFI norm of 250 PPH.
- Narrow winding lanes < 1.8 m wide, blocking emergency access.
- Example: *Dharavi in Mumbai* has density approaching 2,700 PPH.

**2. Environmental/Infrastructure Characteristics (2 marks):**
- Lack of piped water supply; per capita availability < 40 lpcd against CPCB norm of 135 lpcd.
- Open defecation, insanitary shared toilets, no underground drainage.
- Solid waste accumulation, indoor biomass air pollution.
- Example: *Nochikuppam, Chennai* — coastal slum with substandard water supply.

**3. Socio-Economic Characteristics (1.5 marks):**
- Extreme poverty, low literacy, informal-economy dependence.
- Tenure insecurity, social exclusion, gender-based discrimination.
- Example: *Kudumbashree surveys* in Kerala slums reveal 30–40% female illiteracy.

**4. Health & Hygiene Characteristics (1.5 marks):**
- High IMR (> 50/1,000) and disease prevalence (TB, dengue, malaria).
- Stunting, malnutrition, mental health stress.
- Example: *M-East Ward, Mumbai* has one of the highest TB incidences in Asia.

---

**(b) Discuss the effects of slums on (i) slum dwellers, (ii) the host city, and (iii) the macro-economy. Suggest an integrated planning response. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Effects on Slum Dwellers (2 marks):**
- Health degradation (high IMR, malnutrition, epidemics).
- Educational deprivation (child labour, school dropout).
- Economic immobility (no address proof, financial exclusion).
- Psychological trauma and gender-based violence.

**(ii) Effects on the Host City (2.5 marks):**
- Strain on urban infrastructure (water, sewerage, transport).
- Spread of epidemics to the wider city (COVID-19, TB, dengue).
- Loss of property tax revenue; visual blight; fire hazards.
- Encroachment on footpaths → traffic congestion.

**(iii) Effects on the Macro-Economy (1 mark):**
- Reduced urban productivity and GDP loss.
- Increased public welfare expenditure.
- Intergenerational poverty trap.

**Integrated Planning Response (1.5 marks):**
- **In-situ upgradation** through PMAY-U with 25 m² carpet area.
- **Title regularisation** (Patta) for tenure security.
- **Basic services delivery** (Swachh Bharat, Jal Jeevan Mission).
- **Livelihood programmes** (Kudumbashree, NULM, DAY-NULM).
- **Participatory planning** with community-based organisations.

---

> **Question B (Internal Choice) — Slum Improvement Strategies (14 Marks)**  
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Question Pattern]**

**(a) Compare Slum Clearance, Slum Improvement, and Slum Upgrading strategies with their advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Strategy | Approach | Advantages | Limitations |
|----------|----------|------------|-------------|
| **Slum Clearance** (1950s–70s) | Demolish and resettle | Removes worst health hazards; reclaims land | Displaces poor, breaks livelihoods; high social cost |
| **Slum Improvement** (1970s–80s) | Provide basic services in situ | Low cost; minimal displacement; community rooted | Doesn't solve structural shortage; piecemeal |
| **Slum Upgrading** (1990s–present) | Integrate services + tenure + housing | Addresses all 5 UN deficiencies; participatory | Slow; needs strong institutions; land-tenure disputes |

> **Conclusion:** Modern PMAY-U follows a **hybrid upgrading model** combining in-situ redevelopment with relocation where unavoidable. (Full 7 marks for comparative table + 1-line conclusion.)

---

**(b) With the help of a case study, explain how a 4.2-hectare slum with 1,260 households at 4.5 persons/HH can be redeveloped under PMAY-U. Compute density, persons-per-room, and per-HH carpet area. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

- **Step 1:** Compute $N = 1{,}260 \times 4.5 = 5{,}670$ persons (1 mark).
- **Step 2:** $\text{GRD} = 5{,}670 / 4.2 = 1{,}350$ PPH (1 mark) — 5.4× the norm.
- **Step 3:** Assuming 1.4 rooms/HH, $\text{PPR} = 4.5 / 1.4 = 3.21$ (1 mark) — fails UN norm.
- **Step 4:** Total buildable at FSI 2.5 = $2.5 \times 42{,}000 = 1{,}05{,}000$ m² (1 mark).
- **Step 5:** 35% rehabilitation share = $36{,}750$ m² (1 mark).
- **Step 6:** Per-HH carpet = $36{,}750 / 1{,}260 = 29.17$ m² (1 mark) — exceeds PMAY-U 25 m² norm.
- **Conclusion (1 mark):** In-situ redevelopment is feasible; surplus carpet area can be used for community facilities or open space.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Slum Topics:**
> 1. **Confusing "Slum Clearance" with "Slum Improvement"** — Clearance = *demolition + relocation*; Improvement = *in-situ service provision*; Upgrading = *comprehensive redevelopment with tenure formalisation*. Mixing these definitions costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the "5 Deficiencies"** — UN-Habitat definition is the international benchmark; missing any one of the 5 criteria loses the definition mark.
> 3. **Ignoring numerical questions** — Slum density, PPR, FSI, and carpet-area problems appear in **2-mark embedded form** within 14-mark theory questions. Skipping the math = losing 2 marks.
> 4. **Missing Kerala-specific context** — Always cite *Kudumbashree*, *Kerala Slum Clearance Board*, *Life Mission*, and *Suchitwa Mission* for context-specific credibility.
> 5. **Writing generic "effects" lists** — Always structure effects into **Internal / City / Macro** tiers. A flat 10-bullet list without classification scores only 4/7.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Revision)**

- **Definition:** Slum = 60–70 HHs (Census 2011); UN = 5-deficiency rule.
- **Statute:** *Slum Areas (Improvement and Clearance) Act, 1956*.
- **5 UN Deficiencies:** Durable housing, sufficient area, water, sanitation, tenure.
- **4 Characteristic Dimensions:** Physical, Environmental, Socio-Economic, Health.
- **3-Tier Effects:** Internal (dwellers), External (city), Systemic (macro).
- **3 Planning Strategies:** Clearance, Improvement, Upgrading.
- **Key Schemes:** PMAY-U, RAY, SBM-U, DAY-NULM, Rajiv Gandhi National Urban Livelihood Mission.
- **Kerala Context:** Kudumbashree, Life Mission, Kerala Slum Clearance Board, Suchitwa Mission, KWA, KSDMA.
- **Benchmarks to Memorise:** GRD ≤ 250 PPH; PPR ≤ 3; 135 lpcd; 25 m² EWS carpet area; FSI 1.5–2.5.
- **SDG Link:** SDG 11.1 — adequate housing for all by 2030.
- **Formula Trio to Revise:** $\text{GRD}$, $\text{PPR}$, $\text{FSI} = \dfrac{\text{Floor Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$.
- **Case Studies:** Dharavi (Mumbai), Nochikuppam (Chennai), Bhalswa (Delhi), Kannur and Kochi notified slums (Kerala).
- **Exam-Friendly Phrases:** "Negative externality", "Intergenerational poverty trap", "Vicious cycle of deprivation", "Participatory community contracting".

---

> **End of Module 3 — Topic: Characteristics and Effects of Slums**  
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech PECET527 — Town Planning.*  
> *For queries on cross-references with Module 2 (Urban Renewal) or Module 4 (Master Plan), consult the official KTU 2024 syllabus PDF.*</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slum Clearance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Slum Clearance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Slum Clearance** is a public policy intervention and urban renewal strategy wherein substandard, dilapidated, and insanitary housing clusters (slums) are **demolished, evicted, or removed** in their entirety, followed by the resettlement of displaced slum-dwellers in newly constructed housing projects at alternative sites. It is the most radical and interventionist of all slum improvement approaches.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECET527 - Module 3):**
> Slum clearance refers to the comprehensive process of identifying, declaring, and removing blighted residential areas that fail to meet the minimum standards of **health, sanitation, structural safety, and habitability** as prescribed under municipal bye-laws and national housing policy, coupled with the relocation of affected families to planned housing stock.

### Statutory Working Definition (India)

As per the **Slum Areas (Improvement and Clearance) Act, 1956**, a "slum area" is defined as:

> *"Any area where buildings, by reason of dilapidation, overcrowding, faulty arrangement and design of such buildings, narrowness or faulty arrangement of streets, lack of ventilation, light, sanitation facilities, or any combination of these factors, are detrimental to safety, health or morals."*

## 1.2 The UN-Habitat Operational Definition

A slum household, as per **UN-Habitat**, is one that lacks one or more of the following five conditions:
1. **Durable housing** (permanent structure protecting against extreme climates)
2. **Sufficient living area** (no more than 3 people sharing the same room)
3. **Access to safe water** (at affordable cost)
4. **Access to sanitation** (private or public toilet)
5. **Secure tenure** (protection against forced eviction)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric:** India has approximately **49 million** slum-dwellers as per recent Census estimates, housing roughly **17%** of the urban population in just **6%** of urban land area.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **The "Surgical Removal" Analogy:**
> Think of a city as a human body. Slums are like infected tissue. **Slum Improvement** is like administering medicine to cure the infection in place. **Slum Clearance**, however, is like **surgical excision** — the doctor completely cuts out the diseased tissue and transplants healthy replacement tissue elsewhere. The advantage is total removal of the problem. The risk is that the surgery itself causes trauma (displacement of vulnerable populations), and if the underlying causes (poverty, rural-urban migration) are not treated, the disease often reappears elsewhere on the body (new slums emerge).

## 1.4 Historical Context — KTU Frequently Asked

- **1936:** First All-India Slum Survey conducted by the Bombay government
- **1956:** **Slum Areas (Improvement and Clearance) Act** — India's first comprehensive legislation
- **1972-73:** Urban Land Ceiling Act (partly aimed at slum land capture)
- **1985:** National Housing Policy initiated
- **2013:** **Rajiv Awas Yojana (RAY)** — "Slum-free India" mission
- **2015 onwards:** **Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana - Urban (PMAY-U)** — Housing for All by 2022
- **2021:** PM SVANidhi (street vendors) and PM-JANMAN (tribal hamlet) extensions
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Causes of Slum Formation (Logical Breakdown)

The genesis of slums is a **multi-causal phenomenon** — no single factor is responsible. Town planners classify causes into four tiers:

### Tier 1: Macro-Economic Causes
- **Rural-Urban Migration:** Push factors (agrarian distress, drought) + Pull factors (industrial jobs)
- **Rapid Urbanization:** Cities growing faster than housing supply
- **Poverty:** Inability to afford formal market housing

### Tier 2: Policy & Planning Failures
- **Inadequate Public Housing Supply:** Government builds at <5% of demand
- **Outdated Master Plans:** Zoning regulations prevent affordable incremental housing
- **Corrupt Land Markets:** Speculation pushes prices beyond reach

### Tier 3: Site & Service Issues
- **Lack of Infrastructure:** Water, sewerage, drainage unavailable in peripheral areas
- **Tenure Insecurity:** No property rights = no incentive to invest
- **Hazardous Locations:** Slums on river banks, railway tracks, under bridges

### Tier 4: Social & Behavioral
- **Social Networks:** Migrants cluster near kin/community for support
- **Proximity to Livelihoods:** Informal sector needs central locations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Iron Law of Slum Formation:** *Wherever formal housing supply falls below 70% of working-class demand, informal settlements will spontaneously arise on residual/vacant land.*

## 2.2 Approaches to Slum Clearance (The 3-Pillar Framework)

| **Strategy** | **Core Action** | **Displacement** | **Cost** | **Success Rate** |
|--------------|----------------|------------------|----------|------------------|
| **Pillar 1: Wholesale Demolition & Relocation** | Total removal → New townships | 100% | Very High | Low (40-50%) |
| **Pillar 2: In-Situ Upgradation (Slum Improvement)** | Upgrade existing fabric | 0% | Low-Moderate | High (75-85%) |
| **Pillar 3: Hybrid Redevelopment** | Partial clearance + rehabilitation | 30-60% | Moderate | Medium (60-70%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern KTU-Accepted View:** Pure "clearance" without simultaneous affordable housing creation has been abandoned in most Indian municipal policies since 2013. **In-situ upgradation** is now preferred.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Standard Metrics Cheat Sheet

| **Metric / Formula** | **Symbol** | **Equation** | **Standard Value** | **Units** |
|----------------------|------------|--------------|--------------------|-----------|
| Slum Density Index | $SDI$ | $SDI = \dfrac{P_s}{A_s}$ | 500-1500 | persons/hectare |
| Slum Prevalence Ratio | $SPR$ | $SPR = \dfrac{P_s}{P_u} \times 100$ | 15-30 | percentage |
| Plot Coverage Ratio (slum) | $PCR$ | $PCR = \dfrac{A_{built}}{A_{plot}} \times 100$ | >90 (illegal) | percentage |
| Floor Space Index (slum) | $FSI$ | $FSI = \dfrac{A_{total\;floor}}{A_{plot}}$ | 1.5-3.5 | dimensionless |
| Resettlement Cost per Family | $RC$ | $RC = \dfrac{C_{total}}{N_{families}}$ | 5-15 | lakhs INR |
| Cost-Benefit Ratio | $CBR$ | $CBR = \dfrac{B_{social}}{C_{project}}$ | >1 (viable) | dimensionless |
| Land Required for Resettlement | $A_R$ | $A_R = N_f \times p_{min}$ | varies | hectares |
| Minimum Plot Size | $p_{min}$ | per IS 8888:2007 | $\geq 25$ | sq.m. |
| Population Density (rehabs) | $\rho$ | $\rho = \dfrac{N_f \times n}{A_R}$ | 250-400 | persons/hectare |
| Tenure Security Index | $TSI$ | $TSI = \dfrac{T_{secure}}{T_{total}}$ | Target $\geq 0.8$ | ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **Mermaid & LaTeX Safety:** Above table uses `\vert` and `\times` rather than `|` and `x` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 The Slum Clearance Process — Stepwise Logic

1. **Survey & Identification:** GIS mapping, door-to-door enumeration
2. **Notification:** Declaring area as "slum" under Section 3 of 1956 Act
3. **Notification of Owners:** 60-day notice period
4. **Acquisition:** Land vested with government / municipal authority
5. **Demolition:** Phased evacuation → structural demolition
6. **Resettlement:** Allocation of new tenement (free / subsidized)
7. **Site Redevelopment:** New construction as per Master Plan
8. **Maintenance & Monitoring:** Municipal oversight committee

> [!NOTE]
> **Time Duration (typical Indian context):** From notification to rehabilitation — **5 to 10 years** for mid-sized projects.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility for Town Planners

Slum clearance methodology is used in:
- **Mega-city redevelopment** (Mumbai Dharavi, Delhi Kathputli Colony)
- **Railway land reclamation** (Nagpur, Bengaluru)
- **Disaster-prone relocation** (riverbank slum removal — Chennai post-2015 floods)
- **Heritage zone clearance** (Varanasi, Jaipur)
- **Smart City mission components** — integrating slum redevelopment with digital infrastructure

## 2.6 Critical Comparison: Slum Clearance vs. Slum Improvement

| **Parameter** | **Slum Clearance** | **Slum Improvement** |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Approach** | Radical, displacement-based | Incremental, conservation-based |
| **Displacement** | 100% | 0% |
| **Cost (per family)** | ₹8-15 lakhs | ₹1-3 lakhs |
| **Time** | 5-10 years | 1-3 years |
| **Community Participation** | Minimal | Extensive |
| **Tenure Outcome** | New ownership | Formalized existing tenure |
| **Social Cost** | High (loss of livelihood) | Low |
| **Sustainability** | Low (new slums emerge) | High |
| **KTU 2024 Preference** | Legacy understanding | Current policy preference |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivations (For Numerical Problems in KTU Exam)

### 3.1.1 Derivation: Resettlement Land Requirement

**Problem:** A slum of 2,000 families on **8 hectares** is to be cleared. Standard relocation density is **400 persons/hectare** and average family size is **4.5 persons**. Calculate the new site area required and the population density of the original slum.

**Step 1: Calculate original slum population**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_s &= N_f \times n \\
    &= 2000 \times 4.5 \\
    &= 9000 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $N_f$ = number of families, $n$ = average family size.

**Step 2: Calculate original slum density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho_s &= \dfrac{P_s}{A_s} \\
       &= \dfrac{9000}{8} \\
       &= 1125 \text{ persons/hectare}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate required resettlement area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_R &= \dfrac{P_s}{\rho_{target}} \\
    &= \dfrac{9000}{400} \\
    &= 22.5 \text{ hectares}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compare with original**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta A &= A_R - A_s \\
         &= 22.5 - 8 \\
         &= 14.5 \text{ hectares additional}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Resettlement requires **~2.8×** the original land area because target density must be humane (lower) than slum density. This is a major constraint for high-density cities.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation: Cost-Benefit Analysis of Clearance

**Problem:** A clearance project costs **₹250 crore** (acquisition ₹150 Cr, demolition ₹30 Cr, new construction ₹70 Cr). It relocates 5,000 families. Each family previously contributed ₹15,000/year in informal-sector tax-like levies. Land value appreciation post-clearance = ₹400 crore. Determine Cost-Benefit Ratio (CBR).

**Step 1: Total Project Cost**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} &= C_{acq} + C_{dem} + C_{cons} \\
          &= 150 + 30 + 70 \\
          &= 250 \text{ crore INR}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Annual Informal Revenue (before clearance)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{annual} &= N_f \times r_{per} \\
           &= 5000 \times 0.15 \\
           &= 750 \text{ lakh/year} \\
           &= 0.75 \text{ crore/year}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Net Present Value of Revenue (10 years, 8% discount)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
NPV_{rev} &= R_{annual} \times \dfrac{1 - (1 + r)^{-n}}{r} \\
          &= 0.75 \times \dfrac{1 - (1.08)^{-10}}{0.08} \\
          &= 0.75 \times 6.7101 \\
          &= 5.03 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total Social Benefit**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{social} &= NPV_{rev} + L_{appreciation} \\
           &= 5.03 + 400 \\
           &= 405.03 \text{ crore}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Cost-Benefit Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CBR &= \dfrac{B_{social}}{C_{total}} \\
    &= \dfrac{405.03}{250} \\
    &= 1.62
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since **CBR > 1**, the clearance project is **economically viable** in monetary terms. However, social costs (livelihood loss, displacement trauma) are **not captured** in this calculation — a critical KTU critique point.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivation: Slum Density Index (SDI) Compliance Check

**Problem:** Verify whether a slum with 3,600 people on 2 hectares complies with IS 8888:2007 standards (max 400 persons/hectare for rehabilitation housing).

**Step 1: Compute SDI**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SDI &= \dfrac{P_s}{A_s} \\
    &= \dfrac{3600}{2} \\
    &= 1800 \text{ persons/hectare}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compliance Check**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Compliant?} &: SDI \leq 400 \\
1800 &> 400 \;\;\text{✗ Non-compliant}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Required Land for Compliance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{required} &= \dfrac{P_s}{SDI_{standard}} \\
             &= \dfrac{3600}{400} \\
             &= 9 \text{ hectares}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Land Multiplier**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LM &= \dfrac{A_{required}}{A_{existing}} \\
   &= \dfrac{9}{2} \\
   &= 4.5
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert lakhs to crores or hectares to sq.m. Always carry units through every step.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choice of Slum Strategy

This decision matrix helps planners select the appropriate slum intervention:

| **Slum Characteristic** | **Clearance Recommended When** | **Improvement Recommended When** |
|--------------------------|-------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Location** | Hazard-prone (flood, landslide) | Stable, consolidated land |
| **Density** | > 1500 ppha (overcrowded) | < 800 ppha (workable) |
| **Structure quality** | > 60% dilapidated | < 30% dilapidated |
| **Land value** | High (CBD, commercial) | Low (peripheral) |
| **Tenure status** | Government / public land | Private / mixed |
| **Community cohesion** | Weak, transient | Strong, rooted |
| **Infrastructure** | Absent + expensive retrofit | Partial, expandable |
| **Project budget** | > ₹10 lakh per family | < ₹5 lakh per family |
| **Time available** | > 5 years | < 3 years |
| **Political will** | Strong | Moderate |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** The current planning philosophy favors choosing from the right-hand column wherever feasible. Pure clearance is reserved only for **catastrophic hazard zones** or **high-value public-interest projects**.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Numerical: Slum Clearance Project Scheduling (CPM Logic)

**Problem:** A slum clearance project has 8 activities. Determine the critical path and minimum project duration.

| **Activity** | **Predecessor** | **Duration (months)** |
|--------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| A: Survey | None | 2 |
| B: Notification | A | 1 |
| C: Land acquisition | B | 6 |
| D: Demolition | C | 3 |
| E: Resettlement construction | C | 8 |
| F: Relocation of families | D, E | 2 |
| G: New infrastructure | E | 4 |
| H: Handover & monitoring | F, G | 1 |

**Step 1: Earliest Start (ES) and Earliest Finish (EF) — Forward Pass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Activity A:} \quad & ES_A = 0, \quad EF_A = 0 + 2 = 2 \\
\text{Activity B:} \quad & ES_B = 2, \quad EF_B = 2 + 1 = 3 \\
\text{Activity C:} \quad & ES_C = 3, \quad EF_C = 3 + 6 = 9 \\
\text{Activity D:} \quad & ES_D = 9, \quad EF_D = 9 + 3 = 12 \\
\text{Activity E:} \quad & ES_E = 9, \quad EF_E = 9 + 8 = 17 \\
\text{Activity F:} \quad & ES_F = \max(EF_D, EF_E) = 17, \quad EF_F = 19 \\
\text{Activity G:} \quad & ES_G = EF_E = 17, \quad EF_G = 21 \\
\text{Activity H:} \quad & ES_H = \max(EF_F, EF_G) = 21, \quad EF_H = 22
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Project Duration**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{project} &= EF_H = 22 \text{ months}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Latest Start (LS) and Latest Finish (LF) — Backward Pass**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Activity H:} \quad & LF_H = 22, \quad LS_H = 21 \\
\text{Activity G:} \quad & LF_G = LS_H = 21, \quad LS_G = 17 \\
\text{Activity F:} \quad & LF_F = LS_H = 21, \quad LS_F = 19 \\
\text{Activity E:} \quad & LF_E = \min(LS_F, LS_G) = 17, \quad LS_E = 9 \\
\text{Activity D:} \quad & LF_D = LS_F = 19, \quad LS_D = 16 \\
\text{Activity C:} \quad & LF_C = \min(LS_D, LS_E) = 9, \quad LS_C = 3 \\
\text{Activity B:} \quad & LF_B = LS_C = 3, \quad LS_B = 2 \\
\text{Activity A:} \quad & LF_A = LS_B = 2, \quad LS_A = 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Total Float (TF) and Critical Path**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TF_i &= LS_i - ES_i
\end{aligned}
$$

| **Activity** | **ES** | **EF** | **LS** | **LF** | **TF** | **Critical?** |
|--------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|---------------|
| A | 0 | 2 | 0 | 2 | 0 | ✓ |
| B | 2 | 3 | 2 | 3 | 0 | ✓ |
| C | 3 | 9 | 3 | 9 | 0 | ✓ |
| D | 9 | 12 | 16 | 19 | 7 | ✗ |
| E | 9 | 17 | 9 | 17 | 0 | ✓ |
| F | 17 | 19 | 19 | 21 | 2 | ✗ |
| G | 17 | 21 | 17 | 21 | 0 | ✓ |
| H | 21 | 22 | 21 | 22 | 0 | ✓ |

**Critical Path:** A → B → C → E → G → H

**Minimum Project Duration:** 22 months (1 year, 10 months)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **resettlement construction (E)** — not demolition — is on the critical path. Planners must parallelize housing construction with demolition to avoid prolonged displacement camps.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Slum Clearance Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Stage 1: Slum Identification and Survey] --> stepB[Stage 2: Notification under Slum Act 1956]
    stepB --> stepC[Stage 3: Land Acquisition and Ownership Transfer]
    stepC --> stepD[Stage 4: Phased Demolition of Structures]
    stepD --> stepE[Stage 5: Resettlement Construction in New Site]
    stepE --> stepF[Stage 6: Relocation of Displaced Families]
    stepF --> stepG[Stage 7: Original Site Redevelopment]
    stepG --> stepH[Stage 8: Monitoring and Maintenance]

    stepA -.uses.-> tool1[GIS Mapping and Census Tools]
    stepC -.requires.-> tool2[Legal Notification 60 days]
    stepE -.follows.-> tool3[IS 8888 2007 Standards]
    stepF -.includes.-> tool4[Livelihood Restoration Plan]
    stepG -.compliance.-> tool5[Master Plan and Zoning]

    classDef criticalStage fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px
    classDef supportTool fill:#E0F8E0,stroke:#2E8B57,stroke-width:2px

    class stepA,stepB,stepC,stepD,stepE,stepF,stepG,stepH criticalStage
    class tool1,tool2,tool3,tool4,tool5 supportTool
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree — Choice of Slum Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startQ[Slum Identified for Intervention] --> q1{Is site hazard prone?}

    q1 -->|Yes flood landslide or toxic| clearA[GO TO CLEARANCE]
    q1 -->|No stable land| q2{Land value high commercial CBD?}

    q2 -->|Yes high value public interest| clearB[GO TO CLEARANCE with RAY PMAY]
    q2 -->|No peripheral low value| q3{Structure condition dilapidated?}

    q3 -->|Greater than 60 percent dilapidated| q4{Density persons per hectare?}

    q4 -->|Greater than 1500 ppha| clearC[GO TO CLEARANCE AND REDEVELOP]
    q4 -->|Less than 1500 ppha| improveA[GO TO IN SITU UPGRADATION]

    q3 -->|Less than 30 percent dilapidated| improveB[GO TO IN SITU UPGRADATION with infrastructure]
    q3 -->|Between 30 to 60 percent| hybridA[GO TO HYBRID PARTIAL CLEARANCE]

    clearA --> endCLEAR[Clearance Strategy Selected]
    clearB --> endCLEAR
    clearC --> endCLEAR
    improveA --> endIMPROVE[Improvement Strategy Selected]
    improveB --> endIMPROVE
    hybridA --> endHYBRID[Hybrid Redevelopment Selected]

    classDef clearanceNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:3px
    classDef improvementNode fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:3px
    classDef hybridNode fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px
    classDef decisionNode fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px

    class clearA,clearB,clearC,endCLEAR clearanceNode
    class improveA,improveB,endIMPROVE improvementNode
    class hybridA,endHYBRID hybridNode
    class q1,q2,q3,q4 decisionNode
```

## 4.3 Stakeholder Architecture in Slum Clearance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GOVERN[Government Tier]
        g1[Central Government PMAY U]
        g2[State Housing Board]
        g3[Urban Local Body ULB]
        g4[Slum Clearance Board]
    end

    subgraph SUPPORT[Support Tier]
        s1[NGOs and Community Organizers]
        s2[Private Developers PPP Partners]
        s3[Financial Institutions HUDCO]
        s4[Technical Agencies NCRTC etc]
    end

    subgraph COMMUNITY[Community Tier]
        c1[Slum Dwellers Families]
        c2[Women Self Help Groups]
        c3[Informal Sector Workers]
        c4[Local Political Representatives]
    end

    g1 -->|Funds and Policy| g3
    g2 -->|Implementation| g4
    g3 -->|Land Acquisition| c1
    g4 -->|Demolition| c1
    c1 -->|Livelihood Loss| c3
    s1 -->|Mobilization| c1
    s2 -->|Construction| g3
    s3 -->|Loans| c1
    s4 -->|Technical Plans| g3
    c4 -->|Political Pressure| g2
    c2 -->|Feedback Loop| s1

    classDef govNode fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px
    classDef suppNode fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef commNode fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px

    class g1,g2,g3,g4 govNode
    class s1,s2,s3,s4 suppNode
    class c1,c2,c3,c4 commNode
```

## 4.4 Comparative Process Architecture: Clearance vs Improvement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CLEAR[SLUM CLEARANCE PATH]
        c1[Step 1: Total Identification] --> c2[Step 2: Land Acquisition]
        c2 --> c3[Step 3: Full Demolition]
        c3 --> c4[Step 4: New Construction]
        c4 --> c5[Step 5: Mass Relocation]
    end

    subgraph IMPROVE[SLUM IMPROVEMENT PATH]
        i1[Step 1: Partial Mapping] --> i2[Step 2: Tenure Regularization]
        i2 --> i3[Step 3: Infrastructure Add]
        i3 --> i4[Step 4: House Upgradation]
        i4 --> i5[Step 5: Community Stays]
    end

    CLEAR --- COMPARE{Compare Outcomes}
    IMPROVE --- COMPARE

    COMPARE -->|Time| t1[Clearance: 5 to 10 years | Improvement: 1 to 3 years]
    COMPARE -->|Cost| t2[Clearance: 8 to 15 lakh per family | Improvement: 1 to 3 lakh per family]
    COMPARE -->|Displacement| t3[Clearance: 100 percent | Improvement: 0 percent]
    COMPARE -->|Sustainability| t4[Clearance: Low | Improvement: High]

    classDef clearancePath fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px
    classDef improvementPath fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px
    classDef comparisonNode fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px

    class c1,c2,c3,c4,c5 clearancePath
    class i1,i2,i3,i4,i5 improvementPath

```
