# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model))]**
**"Briefly explain the salient features of the UDPFI Guidelines, 1996."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The UDPFI (Urban Development Plans Formulation and Implementation) Guidelines were issued by the **Ministry of Urban Affairs & Employment, Government of India in 1996**, drafted by the **Town & Country Planning Organisation (TCPO)**. Salient features include:

1. **Hierarchical Plan Structure:** A *three-tier hierarchy* (Regional Plan → Sub-Regional Plan → Local Area Plan) for metropolitan cities and a *two-tier hierarchy* (Development Plan → Local Area Plan) for medium and small towns.
2. **Standardised Plan Period:** *20–25 years* for the Development Plan and *5 years* for the Local Area Plan.
3. **Uniform Planning Norms:** Standardised densities, FAR, road widths, open-space standards, and amenity provisions.
4. **Codified Public-Participation Process:** Mandatory exhibition, objection, and hearing stages.
5. **Implementation Mechanism:** Enforceable **Development Promotion Regulations (DPR)** for all development control.
6. **Standardised Plan Contents:** A uniform 14–15 chapter template for all Development Plans across India.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**"Distinguish between a Development Plan and a Local Area Plan under UDPFI."** *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Development Plan | Local Area Plan |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Plan Period** | 20–25 years | 5 years |
| **Scale / Coverage** | Whole town / city | A ward, zone, or neighbourhood |
| **Level of Detail** | Broad, perspective-level | Detailed, action-level |
| **Statutory Weight** | Forms the parent plan | Statutory child plan under the DP |
| **Primary Output** | Land-use zoning, broad infrastructure | Detailed layout, building regulations |
| **Review Cycle** | Mid-term review at 10 years | Mandatory review every 5 years |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (Module-Internal Choice, 14 Marks)

> The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** within each module. Two fully-worked alternatives are presented below. Either attempt.

### **Question A: 14 Marks** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 (Model)]**

**(a) Explain the hierarchy of urban plans recommended by the UDPFI Guidelines, 1996 for metropolitan cities. Discuss the contents of a Development Plan with a neat block diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i) — Hierarchy for Metropolitan Cities:**

UDPFI 1996 recommends a **three-tier planning hierarchy** for metropolitan cities (population > 1 million) to handle the regional, sub-regional, and local dimensions of urban growth:

1. **Regional Plan (Tier 1):**
   - Covers the **metropolitan region** — typically the urban agglomeration plus its hinterland.
   - Plan period: **20–25 years**.
   - Focuses on regional issues: inter-city transport corridors, regional water sources, satellite-town strategy, ecologically sensitive zones.
   - Prepared by the **State Town Planning Department** in consultation with the Metropolitan Development Authority.

2. **Sub-Regional Plan / Development Plan (Perspective) (Tier 2):**
   - Covers the **local planning area** of the city (municipal limits + urbanisable periphery).
   - Plan period: **20–25 years**.
   - Provides the **land-use plan, infrastructure strategy, housing policy, and the Development Promotion Regulations (DPR)**.
   - It is the *principal statutory document* of the city.

3. **Local Area Plan / Zonal Plan / Detailed Town Plan (Tier 3):**
   - Covers a **zone, ward, sector, or a specific neighbourhood** within the city.
   - Plan period: **5 years**.
   - Contains *detailed layouts*, *plot-level regulations*, *local circulation*, and *amenity placements*.
   - Acts as the *action arm* of the Development Plan.

**Part (a)(ii) — Contents of a Development Plan (with block diagram):**

The contents are presented as a logical 5-stage block architecture (see Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram). The key chapters are:

- **Stage 1 — Inception:** Background, regional context, administrative setup, review of the previous plan.
- **Stage 2 — Surveys & Analysis:** Demographic, economic, physical (land-use), environmental, infrastructure, traffic, and heritage surveys.
- **Stage 3 — Synthesis & Strategy:** SWOT analysis, population & economic projections, problem identification, vision, goals, objectives.
- **Stage 4 — Plans & Proposals:** Land-use plan, infrastructure plans (transport, water, sewerage, power), social-amenity plan, housing & slum plan, environment & disaster plan, heritage plan.
- **Stage 5 — Implementation:** Development Promotion Regulations (DPR), phasing schedule, financing plan, monitoring & review mechanism, public-participation report.

**(b) A town recorded the following census populations: 1981 — 1,00,000; 1991 — 1,20,000; 2001 — 1,44,000; 2011 — 1,72,800. Using the Incremental Increase Method, project the population for 2041 and 2051. Comment on the suitability of the method under UDPFI planning. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the decadal increments:**

| Decade | Population | Decadal Increment $\Delta$ | Increment of Increment $\Delta\Delta$ |
|--------|------------|----------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 1981 | 1,00,000 | — | — |
| 1991 | 1,20,000 | 20,000 | — |
| 2001 | 1,44,000 | 24,000 | 4,000 |
| 2011 | 1,72,800 | 28,800 | 4,800 |

**Step 2 — Compute the average decadal increment $\bar{x}$:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{20{,}000 + 24{,}000 + 28{,}800}{3} = \frac{72{,}800}{3} \approx 24{,}267 \text{ persons/decade}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average increment of increment $\bar{y}$:**

$$\bar{y} = \frac{4{,}000 + 4{,}800}{2} = 4{,}400 \text{ persons/decade}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Incremental Increase formula for 2041 ($n = 3$ decades from 2011):**

$$P_{2041} = P_0 + n \bar{x} + \frac{n(n+1)}{2} \bar{y}$$

$$P_{2041} = 1{,}72{,}800 + 3 \times 24{,}267 + \frac{3 \times 4}{2} \times 4{,}400$$

$$P_{2041} = 1{,}72{,}800 + 72{,}800 + 6 \times 4{,}400$$

$$P_{2041} = 1{,}72{,}800 + 72{,}800 + 26{,}400 = 2{,}72{,}000 \text{ persons}$$

**Step 5 — Project to 2051 ($n = 4$):**

$$P_{2051} = 1{,}72{,}800 + 4 \times 24{,}267 + \frac{4 \times 5}{2} \times 4{,}400$$

$$P_{2051} = 1{,}72{,}800 + 97{,}067 + 10 \times 4{,}400$$

$$P_{2051} = 1{,}72{,}800 + 97{,}067 + 44{,}000 = 3{,}13{,}867 \approx 3{,}13{,}870 \text{ persons}$$

**Step 6 — Suitability under UDPFI:**
The Incremental Increase Method is the **most recommended projection method** for Indian towns in UDPFI plan preparation because:
- It captures both a **constant growth component** ($\bar{x}$) and an **accelerating growth component** ($\bar{y}$) — ideal for a town in its mid-growth phase.
- It avoids the *overestimation* of the Geometric Method (which assumes runaway growth) and the *underestimation* of the Arithmetic Method (which ignores acceleration).
- The method is endorsed by **TCPO** and is routinely used by State Town Planning Departments for 20–25 year Development Plans.

---

### **Question B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a) Discuss the role of the Development Promotion Regulations (DPR) in implementing a UDPFI-compliant plan. List any six building regulations typically contained in a DPR. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Role of DPR in UDPFI Implementation:**

The **Development Promotion Regulations (DPR)** is the **legal and statutory instrument** that translates the spatial vision of the Development Plan into enforceable on-ground rules. Its key roles are:

1. **Land-Use Zoning:** Defines *permissible, conditional, and prohibited* uses for each zone (Residential, Commercial, Industrial, Recreational, etc.).
2. **Building Control:** Regulates *height, setbacks, FAR/FSI, ground coverage, basement usage*.
3. **Subdivision & Layout Rules:** Specifies *minimum plot size, road widths in layouts, plot-frontage, and layout-approval procedures*.
4. **Development Trigger Mechanism:** Approvals linked to availability of infrastructure (the **"infrastructure-first" principle** of UDPFI).
5. **Heritage & Environment Overlay:** Special controls for heritage precincts, ecologically sensitive zones, and disaster-prone areas.
6. **Public-Interest Safeguards:** Reservation of land for amenities (parks, schools, hospitals), EWS housing quotas, and parking standards.
7. **Statutory Enforceability:** Provides the *legal basis* on which the Planning Authority can *approve, modify, or reject* any development proposal.

**Six Typical Building Regulations in a DPR:**

| # | Regulation | UDPFI Typical Value |
|---|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Maximum Ground Coverage** | 33 – 50 % (varies with plot size & zone) |
| 2 | **Maximum FAR / FSI** | 1.0 – 2.5 (up to 4.0 in metros) |
| 3 | **Front Setback** | 3 – 6 m (depends on road width) |
| 4 | **Side & Rear Setback** | 1.5 – 3 m each |
| 5 | **Maximum Building Height** | 15 m (no high-rise) to 70 m (with NOC) |
| 6 | **Mandatory Open Space** | 10 – 15 % of plot area |

**(b) The census populations of a city are: 1971 — 50,000; 1981 — 65,000; 1991 — 84,500; 2001 — 1,09,850; 2011 — 1,42,805. Project the population for 2041 using (i) Arithmetic Method and (ii) Geometric Method. State which method is more suitable for a UDPFI Development Plan and justify. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the data:**

| Decade | Population | Increment $\Delta$ | Growth Rate $r$ (%) |
|--------|------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| 1971 | 50,000 | — | — |
| 1981 | 65,000 | 15,000 | 30.00 |
| 1991 | 84,500 | 19,500 | 30.00 |
| 2001 | 1,09,850 | 25,350 | 30.00 |
| 2011 | 1,42,805 | 32,955 | 30.00 |

**Observation:** The growth rate is **constant at 30 % per decade** — this signals a classic geometric-growth town.

**(i) Arithmetic Method:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{15{,}000 + 19{,}500 + 25{,}350 + 32{,}955}{4} = \frac{92{,}805}{4} = 23{,}201 \text{ persons/decade}$$

Project to **2041** ($n = 3$):

$$P_{2041} = 1{,}42{,}805 + 3 \times 23{,}201 = 1{,}42{,}805 + 69{,}604 = 2{,}12{,}409$$

**(ii) Geometric Method:**

$$r = \left[ \left( \frac{1{,}42{,}805}{50{,}000} \right)^{1/4} - 1 \right] \times 100 = \left[ (2.8561)^{0.25} - 1 \right] \times 100$$

$$r = \left[ 1.3000 - 1 \right] \times 100 = 30.00 \text{ % per decade}$$

Project to **2041** ($n = 3$):

$$P_{2041} = 1{,}42{,}805 \times (1.30)^3 = 1{,}42{,}805 \times 2.197 = 3{,}13{,}803$$

**Suitability:**
The **Geometric Method is more suitable** because the historical data shows a **constant percentage growth rate of 30 % per decade** — the precise assumption of the geometric model. The arithmetic projection (2,12,409) *systematically underestimates* the true momentum of a fast-growing town. Under UDPFI, **under-projection is more dangerous than over-projection** — it leads to *undersized* infrastructure (water, roads, sewers) that cannot be retrofitted economically. Therefore, for this city, the Geometric projection of ≈ **3.14 lakh** must form the basis of the Development Plan, although planners may also consider the *Incremental Increase* projection as a cross-check.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in UDPFI Questions (KTU Board Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Master Plan" with "Development Plan":** UDPFI abandoned the term "Master Plan" — the correct term is *Development Plan* (or Sub-Regional Plan for metros). Writing "Master Plan" alone loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Forgetting the Public-Participation Step:** Almost every UDPFI question carries a sub-part on the *plan-preparation process*. Students routinely skip the *exhibition and objection stage* — this is a guaranteed **2-mark loss**.
> 3. **Population-Projection Formatting:** Examiners expect a **tabular layout of past populations and increments** before applying the formula. Jumping straight to the formula costs **1 mark**.
> 4. **Ignoring the Standard Error / Cross-Check:** UDPFI planning standards recommend projecting by **at least two methods** and comparing. Failing to compare loses the *analytical-depth* marks.
> 5. **Mixing UDPFI (1996) with URDPFI (2014):** URDPFI 2014 is the *capital-investment* guideline; UDPFI 1996 is the *plan-formulation* guideline. Do not interchange.
> 6. **Plan Periods:** *20–25 years* for DP and *5 years* for LAP — stating "10 years" or "15 years" is a factual error costing **0.5–1 mark**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — UDPFI Guidelines (Module 4)**
>
> - **Full Form:** *Urban Development Plans Formulation and Implementation Guidelines.*
> - **Year of Issue:** **1996**; **Issued by:** Ministry of Urban Affairs & Employment, GoI; **Drafted by:** **TCPO**.
> - **Plan Period — Development Plan:** **20–25 years**. **Plan Period — Local Area Plan:** **5 years**.
> - **Hierarchy — Metro Cities (Pop > 1 M):** *Regional → Sub-Regional → Local Area Plan* (3-tier).
> - **Hierarchy — Medium / Small Towns:** *Development Plan → Local Area Plan* (2-tier).
> - **DP Contents:** 14–15 standard chapters; includes land-use, infrastructure, transport, housing, environment, **DPR**, and phasing.
> - **DPR = Statutory Implementation Engine:** Contains zoning, FAR, setbacks, height, parking, subdivision rules, heritage overlays.
> - **Public Participation is Mandatory:** 5 stages — Inception → Draft Exhibition → Objection Hearing → Sanction → Periodic Review.
> - **Net Residential Density Norms:** 75–150 pph (low) / 150–250 pph (medium) / 250–400 pph (high).
> - **FAR / FSI Range:** 1.0–2.5 typical; up to 4.0 in metros.
> - **Road Width Hierarchy (UDPFI):** Local 6 m → Collector 9 m → Sub-Arterial 12 m → Arterial 18–24 m.
> - **Open Space Norm:** 2.5–3.0 sq.m / person; Neighbourhood Park: 0.4–0.5 ha / 1000 pop.
> - **Population Projection Formulas (must memorise):**
>   * Arithmetic: $P_n = P_0 + n \bar{x}$
>   * Geometric: $P_n = P_0 (1 + r/100)^n$
>   * Incremental Increase: $P_n = P_0 + n \bar{x} + \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} \bar{y}$
> - **Preferred Method for Indian Towns:** *Incremental Increase* (mid-growth); *Geometric* for fast-growing metros; *Arithmetic* for old, saturated towns.
> - **Statutory Backing in Kerala:** Kerala Town & Country Planning Act, **2016**.
> - **Companion Documents:** URDPFI (2014), Model Building Bye-Laws (2016), Smart Cities Mission.
> - **KTU Golden Tip:** Always answer UDPFI questions by stating **(i) the hierarchy, (ii) the plan period, (iii) the DPR mechanism, and (iv) the public-participation process** — these four pillars fetch full marks in any 7- or 14-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Disaster Mitigation and Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Disaster Mitigation and Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Disaster (KTU 2024 Definition):** A disaster is a serious disruption of the functioning of a community or a society involving widespread human, material, economic or environmental losses and impacts, which exceeds the ability of the affected community or society to cope using its own resources.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hazard, Vulnerability, and Risk — The Three Pillars of Disaster Studies:**
> - **Hazard:** A dangerous event, phenomenon, substance, human activity or condition that may cause loss of life, injury, property damage, social and economic disruption or environmental degradation.
> - **Vulnerability:** The characteristics and circumstances of a community, system or asset that make it susceptible to the damaging effects of a hazard.
> - **Risk:** The probability of harmful consequences resulting from interactions between hazards, vulnerability and capacity.

The fundamental relationship governing disaster risk is expressed as:

$$\text{Risk} = f(\text{Hazard}, \text{Vulnerability}, \text{Capacity})$$

Or more explicitly in the standard UNDRR (United Nations Office for Disaster Risk Reduction) formulation:

$$R = \frac{H \times V}{C}$$

Where $R$ = Risk, $H$ = Hazard, $V$ = Vulnerability, and $C$ = Coping Capacity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Bathtub" Model of Disaster:**
> Imagine a bathtub with water flowing in from a tap (representing **Hazard**) and water flowing out through a drain (representing **Capacity / Coping Ability**). If the tap flows in faster than the drain can empty (high Hazard, low Capacity), and the tub has many cracks (representing **Vulnerability**), the bathtub will overflow — that overflow IS the disaster. Town planning essentially works to: (1) reduce the tap flow (mitigate hazard), (2) patch the cracks (reduce vulnerability), and (3) widen the drain (build capacity).

## 1.3 Types of Disasters — Classification

| S.No. | Category | Sub-Types | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Natural Disasters (Hydro-meteorological)** | Water & Weather related | Floods, Cyclones, Droughts, Cloudbursts, Heatwaves |
| 2 | **Natural Disasters (Geophysical)** | Earth & Tectonic | Earthquakes, Tsunamis, Landslides, Volcanic Eruptions |
| 3 | **Biological Disasters** | Living organism origin | Epidemics, Pandemics (COVID-19), Locust attacks |
| 4 | **Man-Made / Anthropogenic** | Industrial / Technological | Chemical spills, Nuclear accidents, Structural collapse, Air crashes |
| 5 | **Man-Made / Anthropogenic** | Sociological | Terrorism, Riots, War, Stampedes, Fire accidents |
| 6 | **Complex / Hybrid** | Cascading | Climate-induced migration, Industrial fire → Toxic release |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India's Vulnerability Context:** India is among the world's most disaster-prone countries, with **27 out of 35 states/UTs** classified as multi-hazard prone. The Kerala State (where this KTU syllabus is taught) is particularly vulnerable to **floods, landslides, coastal erosion, and lightning** (per Kerala State Disaster Management Authority — KSDMA data).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Disaster Management Cycle — Operational Framework

Disaster management is a continuous, cyclical process comprising **four interconnected phases**. Town planners operate primarily in Phases 1 and 2, but their decisions impact all four.

### Phase 1: Mitigation (Pre-Disaster — Long Term)
- **Goal:** Reduce or eliminate the long-term risk to human life and property.
- **Planning Tools:** Land-use zoning, Building codes, Hazard-resistant design, Retrofitting, Watershed management, Mangrove restoration, Setback regulations.
- **Planner's Role:** Maximum — this is where town planning exerts the strongest influence.

### Phase 2: Preparedness (Pre-Disaster — Short Term)
- **Goal:** Plan and prepare how to respond when disaster strikes.
- **Planning Tools:** Emergency Operations Plan (EOP), Early Warning Systems (EWS), Evacuation routes, Shelters, Mock drills, Training, Stockpiling relief materials.
- **Planner's Role:** High — designing escape corridors, open-space network, and shelter hierarchy in city plans.

### Phase 3: Response (During Disaster)
- **Goal:** Save lives, reduce suffering, protect property.
- **Activities:** Search & Rescue (SAR), Medical Triage, Evacuation, Emergency sheltering, Law & order, Damage assessment.
- **Planner's Role:** Low (operational, not planning), but the *preparedness* planning enables response.

### Phase 4: Recovery & Reconstruction (Post-Disaster)
- **Goal:** Restore and improve the community to pre-disaster (or better) conditions.
- **Activities:** Debris removal, Temporary housing, Permanent reconstruction ("Build Back Better"), Livelihood restoration, Psychological rehabilitation.
- **Planner's Role:** Critical — Post-disaster reconstruction is one of the most important *applied* town planning exercises (e.g., post-2001 Bhuj earthquake, post-2018 Kerala floods, post-2004 Tsunami reconstruction in Tamil Nadu).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Concept / Formula | Mathematical Expression / Standard | Unit / Boundary Condition | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Disaster Risk Function | $R = f(H, V, C)$ | Dimensionless index | Risk Assessment |
| 2 | UNDRR Risk Equation | $R = \dfrac{H \times V}{C}$ | Dimensionless | Standard UN/ISDR method |
| 3 | Population Vulnerability Index | $PVI = \dfrac{P_{exposed}}{P_{total}} \times S_f$ | Index (0 to 1) | Where $S_f$ = Susceptibility factor |
| 4 | Hazard Magnitude (Flood) | $H_{flood} = \int_0^{T} Q(t)\, dt - C_{channel}$ | Cumecs $\cdot$ hours | Hydrological design |
| 5 | Richter Magnitude (Earthquake) | $M = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A}{A_0}\right)$ | Unitless log scale | Seismic zonation |
| 6 | Peak Ground Acceleration (PGA) | $a_{max} = g \times Z$ | $g$ (9.81 m/s²) | IS 1893 Zonal factor $Z$ (0.10 to 0.40 for India) |
| 7 | Saffir-Simpson Scale (Cyclone) | $C = \left(\dfrac{v_{max} - v_0}{v_{ref}}\right)^{1.5}$ | Category 1 to 5 | Wind velocity-based |
| 8 | Evacuation Time Estimate | $T_{evac} = \dfrac{P \times T_{load}}{N_{lanes} \times C_{lane}}$ | Minutes/Hours | $P$ = Population, $T_{load}$ = loading time, $C_{lane}$ = lane capacity |
| 9 | Shelter Demand | $S_{req} = \dfrac{P_{displaced} \times A_{per\,capita}}{A_{unit}}$ | Number of shelters | $A_{per\,capita} \approx 3.5$ m²/person (Sphere Standards) |
| 10 | Return Period (Probability) | $P(\text{exceedance in } T \text{ yrs}) = 1 - (1 - p)^T$ | Probability 0 to 1 | Flood/cyclone design |
| 11 | Vulnerability Score (Composite) | $V_{score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot v_i$ | Index 0 to 100 | $w_i$ = weight, $v_i$ = sub-vulnerability |
| 12 | Building Damage Index | $BDI = \dfrac{D_{observed}}{D_{max\,possible}}$ | Index 0 to 1 | Post-disaster assessment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sphere Standards (Humanitarian Charter):** Minimum covered living area per person in emergency shelters is **3.5 m²**, minimum **20 litres of water per person per day**, and maximum **20 persons per tap stand**. These are the most-cited KTU examiner-expected numbers.

## 2.3 Institutional Framework — India (KTU High-Weightage)

> [!NOTE]
> **Statutory Backing:** The **Disaster Management Act, 2005 (DM Act 2005)** is the parent legislation in India. It mandates a three-tier institutional structure:

### Three-Tier Structure

**Tier 1 — National Level:**
- **National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA)** — Chaired by the **Prime Minister**. Apex body for policy, guidelines, and approval of National Plan.
- **National Executive Committee (NEC)** — Chaired by the **Union Home Secretary**. Implements NDMA policies.
- **National Disaster Response Force (NDRF)** — Specialized force for response, raised in 2006, currently 16 battalions (~18,000 personnel).

**Tier 2 — State Level:**
- **State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA)** — Chaired by the **Chief Minister**. For Kerala, this is **KSDMA** (Kerala State Disaster Management Authority).
- **State Executive Committee (SEC)** — Chaired by the **Chief Secretary**.
- **State Disaster Response Force (SDRF)** — State counterpart of NDRF.

**Tier 3 — District Level:**
- **District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA)** — Chaired by the **District Collector / District Magistrate**. The most operationally important tier for town planners.
- Responsible for district-level disaster plan, hazard mapping, and coordination.

> [!NOTE]
> **Local Self Government (Panchayati Raj) Inclusion:** Section 41 of the DM Act 2005 mandates that every **District, Municipality, and Panchayat** shall have a Disaster Management Plan. The 73rd and 74th Constitutional Amendments enable **local-level planning**, which is the direct interface with town planning practice.

## 2.4 Role of Town Planning in Disaster Mitigation

Town planning reduces disaster vulnerability through **structural** and **non-structural** measures:

| Category | Tools Used | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Land Use Planning** | Zoning, Master Plan, Spatial allocation | Restricting construction in flood plains, coastal regulation zones (CRZ) |
| **Density Regulation** | FAR/FSI, Setbacks, Building Height | Lower density in hazard-prone zones |
| **Infrastructure Design** | Road widths, Drainage, Bridges | Wider roads = evacuation corridors; adequate drainage = flood control |
| **Building Regulations** | NBC 2016, IS 456, IS 1893, IS 4326 | Earthquake-resistant RCC, fire-resistant materials |
| **Open Space Network** | Parks, Playgrounds, Open spaces | Doubles as emergency shelters & assembly points |
| **Hazard Mapping** | GIS-based overlay analysis | Overlaying flood layer + settlement layer to identify risk |
| **Relocation Schemes** | Slum clearance, Resettlement colonies | Post-disaster permanent rehabilitation |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Disaster Risk Equation (UNDRR / ISDR Standard)

The conceptual derivation of the risk function proceeds from the definition of risk as the expected loss:

**Step 1 — Define Expected Loss:**
Risk is mathematically defined as the expected value of loss due to a hazard event.

$$R = E[\text{Loss}]$$

**Step 2 — Express Loss in Terms of Hazard Exposure:**
Expected loss equals the product of the hazard intensity, the elements exposed, and their vulnerability:

$$R = H \times E \times V$$

Where $H$ = Hazard intensity (probability of occurrence), $E$ = Exposure (value of elements at risk), $V$ = Vulnerability (fraction of value lost, 0 to 1).

**Step 3 — Introduce Capacity as a Denominator:**
The UNDRR-2009 framework introduced capacity (C) as a divisor, recognizing that an affected community with higher capacity suffers lower net risk:

$$R = \frac{H \times E \times V}{C}$$

**Step 4 — Standardize for Town Planning Applications:**
For relative risk ranking (most common in KTU-level problems), exposure is normalized to a unit area and capacity normalized to a 0–1 index:

$$R_{rel} = \frac{H \times V}{C}$$

**Step 5 — Composite Index Form (Kerala KSDMA Practice):**
KSDMA typically computes risk on a 0–100 scale using weighted indicators:

$$R_{index} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot h_i + \sum_{j=1}^{m} w_j \cdot v_j - \sum_{k=1}^{p} w_k \cdot c_k$$

**Interpretation:** Higher $R_{index}$ → higher priority for mitigation investment. Town planners use this index to rank wards/zones for intervention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A coastal ward has Hazard score 8/10, Vulnerability 7/10, and Capacity 4/10. Compute relative risk:
>
> $R_{rel} = \dfrac{8 \times 7}{4} = \dfrac{56}{4} = 14.0$
>
> A hill-slope ward has Hazard 6/10, Vulnerability 8/10, Capacity 5/10:
>
> $R_{rel} = \dfrac{6 \times 8}{5} = \dfrac{48}{5} = 9.6$
>
> **Conclusion:** The coastal ward is at higher risk despite having a lower vulnerability, because of its weak coping capacity. This is the typical **Kerala flood-affected coastal panchayat** scenario.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Evacuation Time Estimation

**Step 1 — Total Evacuation Demand:**
Total time to evacuate a hazard zone depends on the population to be moved and the capacity of evacuation routes.

$$T_{total} = T_{detect} + T_{decide} + T_{warn} + T_{evac} + T_{clear}$$

**Step 2 — Travel Time on Routes:**
Travel time for a single lane segment of length $L$ at speed $v$:

$$T_{travel} = \frac{L}{v}$$

**Step 3 — Lane Saturation Flow:**
Maximum number of vehicles that can pass a point per hour on one lane (Highway Capacity Manual):

$$C_{lane} \approx 1800 \text{ PCU/hr (passenger car units)}$$

**Step 4 — Total Evacuation Time for a Zone:**
With $P$ persons to evacuate, average occupancy $O$ per vehicle, and $N_{lanes}$ lanes available:

$$T_{evac} = \frac{P}{O \times N_{lanes} \times C_{lane}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Warning Lead Time Constraint:**
Evacuation must complete within the warning lead time $T_{lead}$ (time between warning and hazard impact):

$$\frac{P}{O \times N_{lanes} \times C_{lane}} \leq T_{lead}$$

**Re-arranged to find required number of lanes:**

$$N_{lanes} \geq \frac{P}{O \times C_{lane} \times T_{lead}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example:**
A coastal town must evacuate 50,000 people before a cyclone makes landfall in 8 hours. Assume 3 persons/vehicle, 1800 PCU/hr per lane, and the road has 2 effective lanes.

**Compute minimum lanes required:**

$$N_{lanes} \geq \frac{50000}{3 \times 1800 \times 8} = \frac{50000}{43200} \approx 1.16$$

Since 1.16 < 2 existing lanes, evacuation is feasible with 2 lanes. **Town planning implication:** Reserve ≥ 2 arterial roads connecting each hazard zone to safe assembly areas in the master plan.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shelter Demand Calculation (Sphere Standards)

**Step 1 — Total Displaced Population Estimate:**
Displaced population is the exposed population minus those who self-shelter with relatives/host families (typically 60–70% self-shelter in Kerala context):

$$P_{displaced} = P_{exposed} \times (1 - \alpha)$$

Where $\alpha$ = self-shelter rate (typically 0.30 → 0.70 in rural; 0.20 in urban high-rise).

**Step 2 — Total Covered Area Required:**
Sphere humanitarian standard is **3.5 m² per person** covered living area:

$$A_{required} = P_{displaced} \times 3.5 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Number of Shelters Required:**
Assuming each shelter unit (community hall, school, prefabricated unit) serves capacity $A_{unit}$:

$$N_{shelters} = \frac{A_{required}}{A_{unit}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Example (Kerala Floods 2018, hypothetical ward):**
Exposed population = 10,000; self-shelter rate = 0.30; shelter unit capacity = 500 persons.

$$P_{displaced} = 10000 \times (1 - 0.30) = 7000 \text{ persons}$$

$$A_{required} = 7000 \times 3.5 = 24500 \text{ m}^2$$

$$N_{shelters} = \frac{24500}{500} = 49 \text{ shelters needed}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Town Planning Implication:** Every master plan must pre-identify **at least 1 shelter per 5,000 population in hazard-prone zones** and ensure they are accessible within **1.5 km walking distance** (UNDRR standard).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Return Period and Design Probability

**Step 1 — Annual Exceedance Probability:**
If a flood of magnitude $M$ has an annual exceedance probability $p$, then the probability it does NOT occur in any given year is $(1-p)$.

**Step 2 — Probability of Occurrence in T Years:**
The probability that the event occurs *at least once* in T years is:

$$P_T = 1 - (1 - p)^T$$

**Step 3 — Design Return Period:**
The reciprocal of the annual probability gives the return period:

$$T_R = \frac{1}{p}$$

**Step 4 — Example — 100-year Flood Design:**
For a 100-year return period flood, $p = 0.01$ (1% annual chance).
Probability of being equalled or exceeded in 30 years (typical planning horizon):

$$P_{30} = 1 - (1 - 0.01)^{30} = 1 - (0.99)^{30} = 1 - 0.7397 = 0.2603$$

So there is approximately a **26% chance** that a 100-year flood will occur at least once during a 30-year mortgage / planning horizon — a non-trivial risk that town planners must communicate to policymakers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "100-year Flood" is Misleading:** A "100-year flood" does NOT mean it occurs once every 100 years. It means there is a 1% chance of occurrence in ANY given year. This is the most-tested KTU conceptual trap.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Disaster Management Cycle (Mermaid Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NODE_MIT] -->|Long-term Risk Reduction| B[NODE_PREP]
    B -->|Warning, Planning, Drills| C[NODE_RESP]
    C -->|Search, Rescue, Medical Aid| D[NODE_RECOV]
    D -->|Build Back Better| A
    B -.->|Activates| C
    C -.->|Damage Data Feeds| D
    A -.->|Updates Plans| B
    F[NODE_HAZ] -->|Triggers| C
    G[NODE_VUL] -->|Amplifies| C
    H[NODE_CAP] -->|Reduces Impact| C
```

**Reading the diagram:** The four core phases (Mitigation → Preparedness → Response → Recovery) form a continuous cycle. The three risk factors (Hazard, Vulnerability, Capacity) feed into the Response phase when a disaster is triggered. The dashed feedback lines indicate that lessons from one phase inform the next iteration of planning.

## 4.2 Three-Tier Institutional Architecture (India — DM Act 2005)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[NODE_NDMA] --> N2[NODE_NEC]
    N2 --> N3[NODE_NDRF]
    N1 -.->|Guidelines| S1[NODE_SDMA]
    S1 --> S2[NODE_SEC]
    S2 --> S3[NODE_SDRF]
    S1 -.->|State Plan| D1[NODE_DDMA]
    D1 --> D2[NODE_DM_PLAN]
    D1 --> D3[NODE_DRF]
    D1 --> L1[NODE_LOCAL_PANCH]
    D1 --> L2[NODE_LOCAL_MUN]
    L1 --> L2
```

**Reading the diagram:** NDMA (PM-chaired) at the apex, SDMA (CM-chaired) at state level, DDMA (District Collector-chaired) at district level. The dotted arrows represent policy/guideline flow, solid arrows represent command/control flow. The bottom tier interfaces directly with local self-government institutions.

## 4.3 Town Planning Tools Mapped to Disaster Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[NODE_ZONING] --> M1[NODE_MIT]
    T2[NODE_BUILDING_CODE] --> M1
    T3[NODE_EWS] --> M2[NODE_PREP]
    T4[NODE_EVAC_PLAN] --> M2
    T5[NODE_SHELTER_NET] --> M2
    T6[NODE_SAR_OPS] --> M3[NODE_RESP]
    T7[NODE_TRIAGE] --> M3
    T8[NODE_RECONSTRUCT] --> M4[NODE_RECOV]
    T9[NODE_BBB] --> M4
    M1 --> M2
    M2 --> M3
    M3 --> M4
    M4 -.->|Lessons Learned| M1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Nine town planning tools (left column) are distributed across the four disaster management phases (right column). Note that **Zoning and Building Codes are purely pre-disaster (Mitigation) tools**, while **SAR and Triage are pure Response tools** that town planners enable through prior preparedness planning. **"Build Back Better" (BBB)** is the recovery paradigm promoted by UNDRR Sendai Framework (2015–2030).

## 4.4 Disaster Risk Triangle — The Conceptual Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H[NODE_HAZARD_TOP] --> R[NODE_RISK_CENTER]
    V[NODE_VUL_LEFT] --> R
    C[NODE_CAP_RIGHT] --> R
    R --> I[NODE_IMPACT_BOTTOM]
    style H fill:#FFB6B6
    style V fill:#FFD580
    style C fill:#B6E2B6
    style R fill:#D6B6FF
```

**Reading the diagram:** When the three factors — Hazard, Vulnerability, and Capacity — converge at a point in time and space, **Risk** materializes, leading to **Impact** (the disaster event). The colors represent: red = hazard, orange = vulnerability, green = capacity (protective), purple = risk synthesis. Town planning reduces the area of the orange-red triangle and enlarges the green capacity area.
