# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Q: Define 'Vulnerability' and 'Capacity' in the context of disaster management. Why is the concept of 'capacity' important for town planners?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Vulnerability** refers to the characteristics and circumstances of a community, system, or asset that make it susceptible to the damaging effects of a hazard (1 mark). It is determined by physical, social, economic, and environmental factors — for example, poorly constructed buildings in seismic zones or slum settlements in floodplains (1 mark).

> **Capacity** (or Coping Capacity) refers to the combination of strengths, attributes, and resources available to manage and reduce disaster risks (0.5 marks). For town planners, capacity is critical because it directly determines the *Net Risk* experienced by a community. Two areas with identical hazard and vulnerability levels can experience vastly different disaster outcomes if their capacities differ (0.5 marks). Town planning builds capacity through resilient infrastructure, open spaces, shelters, evacuation routes, and trained community institutions.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample]**
**Q: Differentiate between 'Hazard' and 'Disaster' with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> A **Hazard** is a potentially damaging physical event, phenomenon, or human activity that may cause loss of life or property (1 mark). A **Disaster** is the actual realization of a hazard event causing widespread disruption exceeding the community's coping capacity (1 mark).

> **Example of Hazard without Disaster:** A magnitude 3 earthquake in an unpopulated desert region is a hazard but causes no disaster. A 50 mm/hr rainfall in a city with well-designed drainage is a hazard but no disaster (1 mark).

> **Example of Disaster:** The 2018 Kerala floods (Hazard: extreme rainfall > 42% excess; Disaster: 483 deaths, ₹40,000 crore loss, 5.4 million displaced). The 2001 Bhuj earthquake (Hazard: M 7.7 seismic event; Disaster: 20,000+ deaths, 400,000 homes destroyed) (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam — University Question Bank, KTU Model Paper 2024]**

> **Q: Disaster Management in India has evolved from a relief-centric approach to a comprehensive, proactive framework following the Disaster Management Act, 2005. With reference to this evolution, answer the following:**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **institutional framework** established by the Disaster Management Act, 2005, describing the roles of NDMA, SDMA, and DDMA at national, state, and district levels. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **role of town planning** as a proactive tool for disaster mitigation. Support your answer with at least **four specific town planning instruments** and their application in reducing disaster risk. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**  
The Disaster Management Act, 2005 (DM Act, 2005) was enacted on **23 December 2005** in the aftermath of the 2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami (26 December 2004), the 2001 Bhuj earthquake, and recurrent floods. It shifted India's approach from **post-disaster relief** to **pre-disaster preparedness, prevention, and mitigation**. The Act created a **three-tier statutory institutional framework**.

**National Level (2 marks):**  
- **National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA):** Apex body chaired by the **Prime Minister**, with up to 9 members. Responsible for laying down **policies, guidelines, and plans** for disaster management; approving the **National Disaster Management Plan**; and coordinating with state authorities.
- **National Executive Committee (NEC):** Chaired by the **Union Home Secretary**, assists NDMA in implementation, prepares the national plan, and coordinates with state executive committees.
- **National Disaster Response Force (NDRF):** Specialized response force raised in **2006**, currently **16 battalions** with ~18,000 trained personnel. The only dedicated disaster response force in the world.

**State Level (2 marks):**  
- **State Disaster Management Authority (SDMA):** Chaired by the **Chief Minister** (with the concerned Minister as Vice-Chair). For Kerala, this is the **Kerala State Disaster Management Authority (KSDMA)**, established in 2007. Responsibilities include formulating the state plan, ensuring capacity building, and issuing guidelines.
- **State Executive Committee (SEC):** Chaired by the **Chief Secretary**, assists SDMA in implementation.
- **State Disaster Response Force (SDRF):** State-level counterpart of NDRF.

**District Level (1.5 marks):**  
- **District Disaster Management Authority (DDMA):** Chaired by the **District Collector/Magistrate** with an elected representative as co-chair. Most operationally important tier. Responsible for: district disaster management plan, hazard mapping, early warning dissemination, coordinating local authorities, and ensuring community preparedness. Section 41 of the Act mandates every municipality and panchayat to have its own Disaster Management Plan.

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**  
The three-tier structure ensures that disaster management is **decentralized, accountable, and integrated** with local governance — a paradigm shift from the earlier centralized relief model.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**  
Town planning is the most powerful **pre-disaster mitigation** tool because it influences the spatial arrangement of settlements, infrastructure, and population — the very factors that determine vulnerability. The **Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015–2030)** explicitly emphasizes land-use planning as a key priority.

**Instrument 1 — Land-Use Zoning (1.5 marks):**  
Zoning segregates incompatible land uses and prohibits construction in high-hazard zones. **Example:** The **Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification 2019** under the Environment Protection Act prohibits development in CRZ-I and CRZ-II areas (the most sensitive coastal strips). In Kerala, CRZ restricts construction within **200 m of the High Tide Line** in CRZ-III. This directly reduces flood and cyclone exposure.

**Instrument 2 — Building Codes and Structural Standards (1.5 marks):**  
National Building Code (NBC) 2016, IS 1893 (seismic), IS 4326 (earthquake-resistant construction), IS 456 (RCC), and Kerala Municipal Building Rules govern structural safety. **Example:** India is divided into **4 seismic zones (II, III, IV, V)** with zone factors $Z$ of 0.10, 0.16, 0.24, and 0.36 respectively. Kerala falls in **Zone III** (moderate risk, $Z = 0.16$). Town plans must ensure all new construction complies with these.

**Instrument 3 — Open Space and Shelter Network Planning (1.5 marks):**  
Master plans reserve parks, playgrounds, and community grounds that double as **emergency assembly points and shelters**. URDPFI guidelines suggest **1 hectare of open space per 1,000 population** in urban areas. Post-2018 Kerala floods, the state mandated that every local body identify **multi-purpose cyclone/flood shelters** in hazard zones.

**Instrument 4 — Evacuation Route Planning and Road Network Design (1.5 marks):**  
Wider arterial roads (minimum **12 m ROW in dense areas, 18–24 m in high-rise zones**) act as evacuation corridors. GIS-based evacuation modeling identifies dead-ends, narrow lanes, and bridge bottlenecks to be widened. **Example:** The **Kerala State Urban Transport Policy** mandates that no residential zone should be more than **1.5 km from a primary evacuation route** and that at least **2 independent evacuation routes** must be available per hazard zone.

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**  
Town planning's greatest strength lies in its **proactive, preventive, and regulatory** nature — it prevents disaster risk from materializing in the first place, rather than merely responding after the fact.

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam — University Question Bank, Module 4]**

> **Q: With reference to the Disaster Management Cycle, answer the following:**
>
> **(a)** Describe the **four phases of the Disaster Management Cycle** with specific town planning interventions for each phase. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **'Build Back Better' (BBB)** as a recovery strategy. Describe how it was applied (or could have been better applied) in the **2018 Kerala Floods** reconstruction, with at least three specific town planning recommendations. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Introduction (1 mark):**  
The Disaster Management Cycle represents the **continuous, iterative process** of managing disasters across their pre-impact, impact, and post-impact phases. It consists of four interlinked phases: **Mitigation, Preparedness, Response, and Recovery**. Town planning is most influential in the **Mitigation** and **Preparedness** phases but also plays a decisive role in **Recovery** through reconstruction planning.

**Phase 1 — Mitigation (2 marks):**  
**Definition:** Long-term measures to reduce or eliminate the risk of disasters.  
**Town Planning Interventions:**  
- Master Plan and Zoning Regulations restricting construction in hazard-prone areas (floodplains, landslide-prone slopes, CRZ zones).  
- Enforcement of building codes and structural standards (NBC 2016, IS 1893, IS 4326).  
- Watershed management, mangrove restoration, and creation of **retention ponds and bioswales** in drainage plans.  
- **Retrofitting** of vulnerable existing structures (e.g., heritage buildings in old town areas).

**Phase 2 — Preparedness (2 marks):**  
**Definition:** Activities undertaken in advance to ensure effective response when a disaster strikes.  
**Town Planning Interventions:**  
- Pre-identification of **multi-purpose shelters** (schools, community halls) and their inclusion in the master plan.  
- Designation of **evacuation routes** with appropriate signage and capacity.  
- **Early Warning Systems (EWS)** integrated with land-use information — for example, Kerala's **Flood Forecasting and Early Warning Centre (FFWC)** at CWC links IMD rainfall forecasts to GIS-based flood inundation maps that identify which wards will be affected.  
- Mock drills and community awareness programs coordinated through the town planning office.

**Phase 3 — Response (1 mark):**  
**Definition:** Immediate actions during and immediately after a disaster to save lives and reduce suffering.  
**Town Planning Role:** Limited direct operational role, but planning enables response — pre-identified shelters, evacuation routes, and trained DDMA members activate the plan. Town planners may assist with **damage assessment surveys** for the post-disaster needs assessment (PDNA).

**Phase 4 — Recovery and Reconstruction (1 mark):**  
**Definition:** Actions taken to restore and improve the community after a disaster.  
**Town Planning Interventions:**  
- Reconstruction plans that incorporate **'Build Back Better'** principles.  
- **Land pooling and readjustment** to reorganize damaged areas with better layouts.  
- Permanent relocation of settlements from high-hazard zones (e.g., the post-2004 Tsunami relocation in Tamil Nadu, where fishing hamlets were shifted 1–2 km inland with planned infrastructure).  
- Updated Master Plans that incorporate lessons learned.

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**  
The cycle is iterative — lessons from each disaster (and its recovery) feed back into enhanced mitigation and preparedness planning for the next event. This is the essence of **resilient planning**.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Introduction — Concept of Build Back Better (1.5 marks):**  
**Build Back Better (BBB)** is a post-disaster recovery paradigm that goes beyond mere restoration of pre-disaster conditions. The concept was formalized in the **UN Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (2015–2030)** and the **UNDRR 2017 BBB Guide**. It has three pillars: (i) **Disaster Risk Reduction** (reduce future risk), (ii) **Sustainable Development** (build back in a way that is socially, economically, and environmentally sustainable), and (iii) **Resilience** (the rebuilt system can withstand future shocks). The Kerala context provides an ideal case study.

**Context — 2018 Kerala Floods (1 mark):**  
The **August 2018 Kerala floods** were the worst in nearly a century, with rainfall **42% above normal** in the season, **483 deaths**, **5.4 million displaced**, and damages exceeding **₹40,000 crore**. Over **20,000 km²** (≈ 13,558 villages) were affected. The **Kerala Rebuild Kerala Initiative (RKI)** was launched with World Bank assistance to coordinate recovery.

**Recommendation 1 — Hazard-Sensitive Relocation (1.5 marks):**  
**Issue:** Many affected families were resettled in the same hazard zones, or relocated to peripheral areas lacking basic services.  
**BBB Recommendation:**  
- Formulate a **Resettlement and Rehabilitation Master Plan** for all relocated families with strict adherence to **land-use planning standards**.  
- All new resettlement colonies must be **outside the 1-in-100-year flood line** and equipped with schools, hospitals, livelihood opportunities, and public transport.  
- Reference: The post-2001 Bhuj earthquake relocation of Anjar and Bhuj towns used this approach successfully with 47 new townships planned.

**Recommendation 2 — Upgraded Building Standards (1.5 marks):**  
**Issue:** Reconstructed houses often used the same vulnerable designs (mud, unreinforced masonry) that had collapsed.  
**BBB Recommendation:**  
- Mandatory adoption of **IS 4326 and IS 13827** (earthquake-resistant) and **state-specific flood-resistant designs** (raised plinths ≥ 1 m above the Highest Flood Level).  
- **Owner-Driven Reconstruction (ODR)** programs with technical assistance, such as the model used in the **Odisha Super Cyclone 1999 recovery**.  
- Use of **disaster-resistant local materials** (laterite blocks treated, bamboo-reinforced panels) for vernacular houses in Wayanad and Idukki.

**Recommendation 3 — Integrated Drainage and Watershed Plans (1 mark):**  
**Issue:** Reconstruction was often piecemeal and did not address the root cause of flooding — encroachments, loss of wetlands, and inadequate drainage.  
**BBB Recommendation:**  
- Update **Kerala's master plans** with **GIS-based flood risk overlays** and a 20% reservation of natural drainage channels as **"Blue-Green Corridors."**  
- Restore **2,653+ identified wetlands** in the state (as per KSDMA's "Wetalnds Atlas" — first of its kind in India) through strict zoning.  
- Strict enforcement of **buffer zones along rivers** (e.g., the Periyar, Pamba, and Bharathapuzha).

**Conclusion (0.5 marks):**  
The 2018 Kerala floods offered an opportunity to fundamentally reshape the state's spatial planning paradigm. While the **Kerala State Disaster Management Plan (2016)** and **Kerala Rebuild Kerala Development Programme** have made progress, the true test of BBB lies in the next disaster — when these planned interventions will either prove their worth or reveal their gaps.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — How Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Hazard" with "Disaster":** A hazard is a potential event; a disaster is its realization. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 2. **Writing "100-year flood" as "occurs once in 100 years":** It is a **1% annual probability**, NOT a guarantee of 100-year spacing. Marks deducted every time.
> 3. **Forgetting the chairperson of NDMA / SDMA / DDMA:** NDMA = **Prime Minister**, SDMA = **Chief Minister**, DDMA = **District Collector/Magistrate**. This is a 1–2 mark question by itself.
> 4. **Not distinguishing "Mitigation" from "Preparedness":** Mitigation = **long-term** (land use, building codes). Preparedness = **short-term** (drills, early warning, evacuation plans).
> 5. **Ignoring the Disaster Management Act year:** Always write **"Disaster Management Act, 2005"** — not "National Disaster Management Act."
> 6. **Skipping Sendai Framework 2015–2030:** It is the most current international policy instrument and frequently asked.
> 7. **In BBB answers, not giving Kerala-specific examples:** Generic answers score 50% marks. Kerala-specific examples (KSDMA, Rebuild Kerala Initiative, Wayanad landslides) score full marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 4: Disaster Mitigation and Management**

- **Core Definitions (memorize verbatim):** Hazard, Vulnerability, Capacity, Risk, Disaster — all five in the standard UNDRR / DM Act 2005 wording.
- **Core Equation:** $R = \dfrac{H \times V}{C}$ — UNDRR risk formula. Higher H, higher V, lower C → higher Risk.
- **DM Act 2005:** Enacted **23 December 2005** (post-2004 Tsunami). Establishes **NDMA (PM), SDMA (CM), DDMA (District Collector)** — three-tier statutory structure.
- **NDMA:** Chaired by **Prime Minister**; 9 members. Apex policy body. Created **National Disaster Management Plan (2016, revised 2019)**.
- **NDRF:** Raised in **2006**; **16 battalions**; specialized response force; the world's only dedicated disaster response force.
- **Disaster Management Cycle:** **Mitigation → Preparedness → Response → Recovery** (continuous loop).
- **Mitigation = Long-term** (zoning, building codes, retrofitting, land-use plans).
- **Preparedness = Short-term** (early warning, evacuation plans, shelters, mock drills, stockpiling).
- **Town Planning Tools:** Zoning, Density regulation (FAR/FSI), Setbacks, Building codes (NBC 2016, IS 1893, IS 4326), Open space network, GIS hazard mapping, Relocation schemes.
- **Seismic Zones of India:** II, III, IV, V — Kerala in **Zone III** (Z = 0.16). Other states: Delhi/Guwahati Zone IV, Kashmir Zone V, Bangalore Zone II.
- **CRZ Notification 2019:** Prohibits development in **CRZ-I and CRZ-II**. 200 m buffer from High Tide Line in CRZ-III.
- **Sphere Standards:** **3.5 m² covered area per person** in emergency shelters; **20 L water per person per day**; **20 persons per tap stand**.
- **Evacuation Route Standard:** Minimum **2 independent evacuation routes per hazard zone**; maximum **1.5 km walking distance** to shelter.
- **Return Period Trap:** A "100-year flood" has **1% annual probability**, not a 100-year cycle. Probability in T years = $1 - (1 - p)^T$.
- **"Build Back Better" (BBB):** UN Sendai Framework 2015–2030 paradigm — three pillars: Risk Reduction, Sustainable Development, Resilience.
- **Sendai Framework 2015–2030:** 4 priorities: (i) Understanding disaster risk, (ii) Strengthening risk governance, (iii) Investing in resilience, (iv) Enhancing preparedness.
- **Kerala Context:** **KSDMA** (Kerala State DMA); **Rebuild Kerala Initiative (RKI)**; prone to floods, landslides, coastal erosion, lightning; **Wayanad** (landslides), **Alappuzha** (floods), **Kozhikode** (coastal erosion) are high-priority districts.
- **Kerala Wetland Atlas:** First-of-its-kind in India, identifies **2,653+ wetlands** — a unique town planning tool for flood mitigation.
- **73rd/74th Constitutional Amendments:** Enable local self-government (Panchayat, Municipality) participation in disaster planning under Section 41 of DM Act 2005.
- **NBC 2016, IS 1893 (Part 1):2016, IS 4326:2013, IS 13827:1993** — all critical standards to mention in answers.
- **NDMA Guidelines for specific hazards:** Earthquake (2007), Floods (2008), Cyclones (2008), CBRN (2010), Landslides (2009) — note the year-wise sequence.
- **SDMA vs DDMA Function:** SDMA = policy; DDMA = operational. The District Collector is the most important functionary in actual disaster response.
- **Damage Assessment Terminology:** PDNA (Post-Disaster Needs Assessment) — World Bank/UN/Asian Development Bank framework. Use this term in 14-mark answers for +1 mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Environmental and Pollution Control Acts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Environmental and Pollution Control Acts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Environmental and Pollution Control Acts** refer to the body of statutory legislation enacted by the Parliament and State Legislatures of India that regulate human activities impacting the natural environment, control emission and discharge of pollutants into air, water, and land, and establish institutional mechanisms for monitoring, enforcement, and penalty. In town planning, these Acts form the **regulatory backbone** governing land-use zoning, building permissions, industrial siting, heritage conservation, and the preparation of Development Plans / Master Plans.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition for Examinations:**
> *Environmental legislation in India is an umbrella term covering statutes aimed at prevention, control, abatement and management of environmental pollution, conservation of natural resources, and sustainable development of human settlements, enforced through designated regulatory authorities and tribunals.*

The principal statutes covered under KTU PECET527 Module 4 are:

| Sl. No. | Year | Act | Acronym |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1972 | Wildlife Protection Act | WPA |
| 2 | 1974 | Water (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act | Water Act |
| 3 | 1980 | Forest Conservation Act | FCA |
| 4 | 1981 | Air (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act | Air Act |
| 5 | 1986 | Environment Protection Act | EPA |
| 6 | 1991 | Public Liability Insurance Act | PLIA |
| 7 | 2000 | Biological Diversity Act | BDA |
| 8 | 2010 | National Green Tribunal Act | NGTA |
| 9 | 2016 | Solid Waste Management Rules | SWM Rules |
| 10 | 2006/2020 | EIA Notification | EIA Notification |
| 11 | 2011 | CRZ Notification | CRZ |
| 12 | 2017 | Wetland Conservation Rules | WCR |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a city as a **living human body**. The rivers flowing through it are the **blood vessels**, the air is the **lungs**, the forests and parks are the **immune system**, and the industries/commercial zones are the **digestive organs producing waste**.

Just as the human body has physiological rules ("don't eat plastic", "excrete only through kidneys") enforced by hormones and nervous signals, a town/ city has **Environmental Acts** which act as the **constitution of the city's metabolism**:
- They tell industries: *"You cannot throw untreated waste into the river"* (Water Act).
- They tell builders: *"You cannot build a factory in the lungs of the city (forest/ park)"* (Forest Conservation Act).
- They tell citizens: *"You cannot blow horns beyond 55 dB in residential zones"* (Noise Rules).
- They appoint **doctors (regulators)** like the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) and State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) to diagnose and punish the violators.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** Environmental Acts are the **"Rulebook of the City"** ensuring that growth does not destroy the very ecology that sustains it. For a town planner, ignoring them is equivalent to designing a building without a foundation — the plan collapses the moment a court order is issued.

### 1.3 Key Institutional Bodies

- **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** – Apex body under the Water Act 1974 and Air Act 1981. Headquartered in **Delhi**. Sets national emission/effluent standards.
- **State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs)** – State-level regulators granting **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)** to industries.
- **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)** – Nodal ministry issuing notifications like EIA, CRZ, Wetland Rules.
- **National Green Tribunal (NGT)** – Quasi-judicial body under NGTA 2010, sitting principally at **New Delhi**, with benches at Pune, Kolkata, Bhopal, Chennai, and Guwahati.
- **National Board for Wildlife (NBWL)** and **Wildlife Advisory Boards** – Under WPA 1972.
- **National Biodiversity Authority (NBA)** – Under BDA 2002.

### 1.4 Significance in Town Planning (KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome Mapping)

| Course Outcome (CO) | Relevance of Environmental Acts |
|---|---|
| CO4 | Plan environmentally sustainable human settlements |
| CO5 | Apply regulatory frameworks in zoning/land use |
| CO6 | Conduct EIA for proposed developments |
| CO7 | Understand legal procedures for clearance & compliance |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974

**Why it exists:** India's first comprehensive anti-pollution statute. Pre-1974, rivers like the Ganga, Yamuna, and Krishna were used as open sewers by municipalities and industries. The Act created the **CPCB and SPCBs** to restore water quality.

**Key Provisions (Section-wise):**

- **Section 3** – Constitution of the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB).
- **Section 4** – Constitution of State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs).
- **Section 17** – Functions of SPCBs: plan prevention/control, advise state govt., collect/disseminate data, lay down effluent standards.
- **Section 19** – SPCB's power to **inspect** any premises, take samples, and seize.
- **Section 20** – Power to **obtain information** from industries.
- **Section 24** – Prohibition of discharge of effluents into streams/wells/sewers **without consent**.
- **Section 25** – Application for **Consent to Establish (CTE)** and **Consent to Operate (CTO)**.
- **Section 26** – SPCB's power to refuse, withdraw, or cancel consent.
- **Section 33-A** – **Penalty for contravention** — imprisonment up to 1 year 6 months and/or fine up to ₹1 lakh; subsequent contravention — minimum 2 years imprisonment.
- **Section 64** – Joint liability of executives/officers in charge of the company.

**How it works in town planning:**
A town planner proposing an industrial zone adjacent to a river must:
1. Identify the receiving water body's **Class** (A, B, C, D, E as per CPCB).
2. Ensure industries obtain **CTE before construction** and **CTO before commissioning**.
3. Provide for an **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** in the zoning regulation.
4. Reserve land for a **Common Effluent Treatment Plant (CETP)** when the zone contains multiple polluting industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Class of Water Bodies (CPCB Classification):**
> - **Class A** – Drinking water source (conventional treatment).
> - **Class B** – Outdoor bathing.
> - **Class C** – Drinking water with conventional + disinfection.
> - **Class D** – Propagation of wildlife/fisheries.
> - **Class E** – Irrigation, industrial cooling, controlled waste disposal.

### 2.2 The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981

**Why it exists:** Extension of the Water Act framework to the atmosphere. Empowered SPCBs to deal with air pollution, particularly from **thermal power plants, brick kilns, and vehicular emissions**.

**Key Provisions:**

- **Section 19** – Power to declare **"Air Pollution Control Areas" (APCAs)** — no industrial activity with emissions is allowed without consent.
- **Section 20** – Power to **enter, inspect, take samples** of air/emissions.
- **Section 21** – Consent mechanism identical to Water Act.
- **Section 22A** – Restrictions on new industrial plants in APCAs.
- **Section 31A** – **Penalty** – imprisonment up to 6 years and/or fine; minimum fine ₹10,000, subsequent offence minimum ₹5,000/day.

**Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAQI — National Ambient Air Quality Index):**

| Pollutant | Standard (µg/m³, 24-hr) | Health Concern |
|---|---|---|
| PM2.5 | **60** | Respiratory |
| PM10 | **100** | Respiratory |
| NO₂ | **80** | Lung inflammation |
| SO₂ | **80** | Acid rain, asthma |
| CO | **4000 (8-hr)** | Hypoxia |
| O₃ | **100 (8-hr)** | Smog |
| Pb | **1.0** | Neurological |
| NH₃ | **400** | Irritation |

### 2.3 The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 (Umbrella Act)

**Why it exists:** Enacted in the aftermath of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984)** and the **Oleum Gas Leak in Delhi (1985)**. Often called the **"Umbrella Act"** because it provides the central government overarching power to protect "the whole of man-kind's environment".

**Section 3** – Power of Central Government to take all such measures as it deems necessary to protect and improve the environment and to prevent environmental pollution.

**Section 5** – Power to make rules for:
- Standards of quality of air, water, or soil for various areas.
- Maximum permissible limits of environmental pollutants.
- Procedures for handling hazardous substances.
- Location of industries.

**Section 6** – Power to **regulate the location of industries**.

**Section 7** – Power to **prescribe procedures** for environmental impact statements.

**Section 8** – Penalty — imprisonment up to **5 years** and/or fine up to **₹1 lakh**, with additional fine of up to **₹5,000/day** for continuing violations. **Minimum imprisonment is 1 year** for the first offence and **2 years** for subsequent offences.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** Section 6(1) is the legal basis for the **EIA Notification 2006** (and its 2020 amendment), the **Hazardous Waste Management Rules, Solid Waste Management Rules, Plastic Waste Management Rules, and Construction & Demolition Waste Management Rules**.

**Notification Hierarchy under EPA 1986:**

```
EPA 1986 (Section 3/5/6)
        │
        ├── EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020)
        ├── Hazardous Waste Management Rules 2008 (amended 2016)
        ├── Solid Waste Management Rules 2016
        ├── Plastic Waste Management Rules 2016 (amended 2021/2022)
        ├── Construction & Demolition Waste Management Rules 2016
        ├── E-Waste Management Rules 2016 (amended 2022)
        ├── Biomedical Waste Management Rules 2016
        ├── Noise Pollution Rules 2000
        ├── Wetlands Rules 2017
        └── CRZ Notification 2011 (superseded 2019)
```

### 2.4 The Forest Conservation Act, 1980

**Why it exists:** To check **deforestation** for non-forest purposes (dams, mining, townships). Prior to 1980, there was no statutory bar on diverting forest land. The Act was passed after the **Chipko Movement (1973)** and the **Silent Valley Movement (1978–84)** highlighted ecological destruction.

**Key Provisions:**

- **Section 2** – Restriction on **diversion of forest land for non-forest purpose** — prior approval of the **Central Government (MoEFCC)** is mandatory.
- **Section 3** – Constitution of **Forest Conservation Advisory Committee**.
- **Section 3A** – Penalty — imprisonment up to **15 days** (in some states 1 year) and/or fine.

**Non-Forest Purpose** under FCA includes:
- Cultivation of tea, coffee, spices, rubber, oilseeds.
- Clearing forest for housing, townships, industry, mining, road, dam, canal.
- Even re-afforestation is regulated.

**Application to Town Planning:**
- If a Master Plan proposes a road or housing layout on forest land, the **State Forest Department → MoEFCC** approval under FCA 1980 is mandatory.
- **Compensatory Afforestation** is required (typically 1:1 area, sometimes 1.5:1 or 1:2 for degraded land).
- **Net Present Value (NPV)** of forest land is to be paid as per **CAMPA (Compensatory Afforestation Fund Management and Planning Authority) Act, 2016**.

### 2.5 The Wildlife Protection Act, 1972

**Why it exists:** India's first wildlife law. Created a **protected area network** — **National Parks, Wildlife Sanctuaries, Conservation Reserves, Community Reserves**.

**Key Provisions:**

- **Section 2(24)** – Defines **"wildlife"** to include mammals, birds, reptiles, amphibians, fish, and invertebrates.
- **Section 9** – Prohibition of **hunting** of wild animals specified in Schedules I–IV.
- **Section 18** – Declaration of **Wildlife Sanctuaries** — human activity restricted, no destruction of habitat, no hunting.
- **Section 35** – Declaration of **National Parks** — highest degree of protection; **no grazing, no private rights, no removal of forest produce, no destruction of wildlife habitat**.
- **Schedule I** – Absolute protection (e.g., Indian lion, Bengal tiger, Indian rhino, Great Indian bustard).
- **Schedule II** – High protection.
- **Schedules III–IV** – Lesser protection.
- **Schedule V** – **Vermin** (crows, fruit bats, rats, etc.) — can be hunted.

**Town Planning Implication:**
- **Eco-Sensitive Zone (ESZ)** of 1–10 km is declared around every National Park and Wildlife Sanctuary by MoEFCC (as per 2011 guidelines). No commercial, industrial, mining activity within ESZ; only regulated activities.

### 2.6 The Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, 2000

**Why it exists:** Issued under **Section 25 of EPA 1986**. Air Act 1981 already had noise as part of "air pollutant", but specific rules were needed.

**Ambient Noise Standards:**

| Zone | Day (dB(A)) | Night (dB(A)) |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial | **75** | **70** |
| Commercial | **65** | **55** |
| Residential | **55** | **45** |
| Silence Zone (hospitals, schools, courts) | **50** | **40** |

> [!NOTE]
> "Day" = 6 AM to 10 PM. "Night" = 10 PM to 6 AM.
> 1 **dB(A)** = a 1 decibel increase on the A-weighted scale which approximates human ear response.

### 2.7 Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) Notification, 2019

**Why it exists:** India has a coastline of **7,516.6 km**. The first CRZ Notification came in **1991** to protect mangroves, coral reefs, and prevent haphazard construction along the coast.

**CRZ Categories under 2019 Notification:**

| Category | Description | Permitted Use |
|---|---|---|
| **CRZ-I A** | Ecologically Sensitive Areas (mangroves, coral reefs, sand dunes, nesting grounds) | No new construction except boat jetties for local communities |
| **CRZ-I B** | Intertidal Zone (between LTL and HTL) | Limited access |
| **CRZ-II** | Built-up Area within Municipal Limits | Reconstruction permitted subject to Floor Space Index (FSI) of 2014 plan |
| **CRZ-III** | Rural and Undeveloped Areas | Limited construction on landward side of NDZ |
| **CRZ-IV** | Water and Seabed Area | Only ecologically sound activities |

**NDZ = No Development Zone** (varies from 50 m to 200 m from HTL).

**Key Change in 2019 Notification:** Demarcation of High Tide Line (HTL) and Low Tide Line (LTL) by **Space Applications Centre (ISRO), Ahmedabad**, using satellite imagery.

### 2.8 EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2020)

**EIA = Environmental Impact Assessment.**

**Why it exists:** Mandates prior evaluation of environmental consequences of projects.

**Project Categories:**

| Category | Description | Process |
|---|---|---|
| **Category A** | Large projects (e.g., ≥500 MW thermal, ≥100 km highway) | Appraisal by **EAC (Expert Appraisal Committee) at MoEFCC** |
| **Category B1** | Moderate projects | Appraisal by **SEIAA (State EIA Authority)** |
| **Category B2** | Small projects, no significant impact | Exempted from full EIA, only Form-1 |
| **Category A (under B)** | Specific high-impact projects | Always at Central level |

**EIA Process Steps:**

1. **Screening** → Determine if EIA required.
2. **Scoping** → Identify key issues.
3. **Baseline Data Collection** (minimum 1 season, 3 months in many sectors).
4. **Impact Prediction & Evaluation**.
5. **Mitigation & Management Plan (EMP)**.
6. **Public Hearing** (Section 11 of EPA).
7. **Appraisal** by EAC/SEIAA.
8. **Grant or Rejection of EC (Environmental Clearance)**.
9. **Post-EC Monitoring** (six-monthly compliance reports).

**Validity:** EC valid for **7 years** for project construction phase, then **5 years** for production phase (extendable).

> [!WARNING]
> The 2020 draft amendment proposed significant dilution (post-facto clearance, single public hearing, etc.). The 2020 *EIA Draft Notification* is a major point of academic debate. The final amended EIA Notification, 2020 is in force but many provisions have been challenged in NGT.

### 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Statute | Year | Key Section | Core Concept | Penalty |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Water Act | 1974 | S.24, 25 | Consent to Establish & Operate | 1.5 yr jail + ₹1L |
| Air Act | 1981 | S.19, 21 | Air Pollution Control Area, Consent | 6 yr jail + ₹10,000 |
| EPA | 1986 | S.3, 5, 6, 7, 8 | Umbrella Act, EIA basis, location control | 5 yr jail + ₹1L + ₹5,000/day |
| Wildlife Protection Act | 1972 | S.9, 18, 35 | Hunting ban, Sanctuary, National Park | 3–7 yr jail |
| Forest Conservation Act | 1980 | S.2 | Forest land diversion | 15 days jail + fine |
| Noise Rules | 2000 | Sch.III | 50/55/65/75 dB(A) zones | ₹100–₹1L |
| CRZ | 2019 | Para 3–7 | NDZ 50–200 m | Seizure + demolition |
| EIA Notification | 2006/2020 | Para 2–14 | Screening → Public Hearing → EC | EC revocation + criminal prosecution |
| Solid Waste Rules | 2016 | Rule 4–22 | Segregation, processing, treatment | ₹10,000 → ₹1 Cr |
| NGT Act | 2010 | S.14, 15, 16 | Adjudication, Compensation, Appeal | Up to ₹100 Cr |

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Site Selection of Townships** — a town planner must reject sites falling within forest land, CRZ-I, ESZ, or within 500 m of industries without consent.
- **Layout Approvals** — building plans within 5 km of a Protected Area need **Wildlife Clearance**; near rivers need **CRZ clearance**; in coastal areas need NDZ compliance.
- **Infrastructure Costing** — environment compliance cost (ECP) typically 5–10% of project cost; planners must reserve this in DPRs.
- **EIA Preparation** — town planners often act as **EIA Coordinators** for SEZ, township, and industrial estate projects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Decision Flow — "Which Act is Applicable?"

A town planner encountering a new project must follow this **analytical matrix** to determine the applicable statutes. The following exhaustive logical derivation provides a step-by-step decision tree.

**Step 1: Identify the Project Footprint.**

$$A_{site} = A_{total} - A_{forest,restricted} - A_{CRZ,NDZ}$$

where:
- $A_{site}$ = Net developable area
- $A_{total}$ = Total land area under consideration (in hectares)
- $A_{forest,restricted}$ = Area under forest cover requiring FCA 1980 clearance
- $A_{CRZ,NDZ}$ = Area within No Development Zone of CRZ 2019

**Step 2: Compute the Effective Project Area.**

For town planning purposes, every hectare (ha) of land triggers a chain of regulatory obligations based on the **distance from ecologically sensitive receptors**.

$$
R = \min \left( d_{NP}, d_{WS}, d_{CRZ}, d_{ESZ}, d_{river}, d_{forest} \right)
$$

where $R$ is the **minimum distance** from the project boundary to the nearest ecological receptor. This is the **trigger distance** for the **most stringent applicable Act**.

**Step 3: Map Trigger Distance to Applicable Statute.**

$$
\text{Act} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{EPA 1986 + EIA 2006} & \text{if } A_{site} \geq A_{threshold} \\
\text{FCA 1980} & \text{if } d_{forest} = 0 \text{ km} \\
\text{CRZ 2019} & \text{if } d_{CRZ} \leq 200 \text{ m} \\
\text{WPA 1972} & \text{if } d_{NP} \leq 10 \text{ km or } d_{WS} \leq 1 \text{ km} \\
\text{Water Act 1974} & \text{if effluent discharge} \geq 10 \text{ kL/day} \\
\text{Air Act 1981} & \text{if } Q_{stack} \geq 0.2 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{min} \\
\text{Noise Rules 2000} & \text{if } L_{eq} \geq 50 \text{ dB(A)} \text{ in silence zone}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Number of Mandatory Clearances (N):**

$$
N_{clearance} = N_{EC} + N_{FCA} + N_{CRZ} + N_{WL} + N_{CTE} + N_{CTO} + N_{Municipal} + N_{NBWL}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Compliance Cost (CC):**

$$
CC = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{clearance}} \left( F_{i} + M_{i} \cdot t_{i} \right)
$$

where:
- $F_{i}$ = Fixed fee for clearance $i$
- $M_{i}$ = Monthly monitoring cost
- $t_{i}$ = Validity period in months

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — Buffer Zone Compliance

**Problem:** A town planner is to site a 200 ha new township (Tiruvalla Green Estate) at a location with the following constraints:
- 8 km from **Periyar Tiger Reserve** (National Park).
- 1.5 km from **Vembanad Lake** (Backwater, CRZ).
- 0.5 km from a **Reserved Forest** (R.F.).
- Adjoining a **Class B river** (Pamba River) used for outdoor bathing.
- Proposed **built-up area = 80 ha**, **open space = 70 ha**, **industrial = 50 ha**.

**Step 1: Applicable Acts Check.**

**Step 1a — Periyar Tiger Reserve at 8 km:**
- 8 km > 10 km is FALSE (because 8 < 10). The township is **WITHIN ESZ of Periyar Tiger Reserve**.
- ⇒ **Wildlife Clearance from NBWL is mandatory** under **WPA 1972**.

**Step 1b — Vembanad Backwater at 1.5 km:**
- The backwater is a tidal influenced water body ⇒ **CRZ applies**.
- CRZ-III area (rural/undeveloped) ⇒ NDZ of **200 m** from HTL.
- The township is at 1.5 km, which is **beyond NDZ** of 200 m, so it falls in CRZ-III.
- ⇒ **CRZ Clearance from Kerala Coastal Zone Management Authority (KCZMA)** is mandatory.

**Step 1c — Reserved Forest at 0.5 km:**
- Township boundary is 0.5 km from forest land.
- **Section 2 of FCA 1980** applies if any forest land is being diverted. Here, the township is **adjacent** to R.F. ⇒ diversion of even **approach road / utility corridor** needs FCA approval.
- ⇒ **FCA 1980 approval + Compensatory Afforestation (1:1) + NPV payment** is required.

**Step 1d — Pamba River (Class B):**
- Township is in the catchment.
- Industrial area (50 ha) will discharge effluents.
- ⇒ **Water Act 1974 — Consent to Establish (CTE) and Consent to Operate (CTO)** from Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB) is mandatory.
- ⇒ **ETP and ZLD (Zero Liquid Discharge) treatment** required.

**Step 1e — Township scale 200 ha:**
- 200 ha = 2 km² > 150 ha threshold.
- ⇒ **EIA Notification 2006 / 2020** applies.
- Project Category = **B1** (Township/SEZ under item 8(b) of Schedule) ⇒ **SEIAA, Kerala**.

**Step 1f — Industrial area = 50 ha:**
- 50 ha industrial ⇒ in the **Industrial Zone** ⇒ air emissions from any process.
- ⇒ **Air Act 1981 — Consent from KSPCB**.

**Step 2: Number of Clearances Required.**

$$
N_{clearance} = N_{WL} + N_{CRZ} + N_{FCA} + N_{EC} + N_{CTE} + N_{CTO} + N_{Municipal} = 7
$$

**Step 3: Compliance Cost.**

$$
CC = (5L + 0.5L \cdot 12) + (3L + 0.3L \cdot 12) + (2L + 0.2L \cdot 12) + (10L + 1L \cdot 24) + (2L) + (1L) + (2L)
$$

$$
CC = 11L + 6.6L + 4.4L + 34L + 2L + 1L + 2L = ₹61 \text{ Lakhs (approx.)}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion for the Town Planner.**

The site is **highly constrained** — 7 statutory clearances required, with significant time (18–24 months) and cost (≥ 6% of project cost). A prudent planner should:

1. Reduce industrial component to < 10 ha (avoids Category A).
2. Locate township beyond 10 km from Periyar (avoids ESZ).
3. Negotiate **NoC from State Forest Department** before FCA Stage-I.
4. Plan **Green Belt of 50 m** between industry and residential zones.

### 3.3 Compensatory Afforestation Calculation (Worked)

If the township requires 8 ha of forest land for an approach road:

$$
A_{CA,required} = k \cdot A_{forest,diversion}
$$

where $k$ is the **multiplier** (1.0 for dense, 1.5 for moderately dense, 2.0 for degraded, 0.5 for plantation forest).

$$
A_{CA,required} = 1.0 \cdot 8 = 8 \text{ ha}
$$

**NPV Calculation:**

$$
NPV = A_{forest,diversion} \cdot V_{NPV,per,ha}
$$

where $V_{NPV,per,ha}$ is as per MoEFCC Office Order F.No.5-3/2007-FC dated **23 March 2018** and subsequent revisions.

For tropical dry deciduous forest (most of Kerala):

$$
V_{NPV,per,ha} = ₹ 7,00,000/\text{ha to ₹ 12,00,000/ha}
$$

$$
NPV = 8 \text{ ha} \cdot ₹10,00,000/\text{ha} = ₹80,00,000 = ₹80 \text{ Lakhs}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NPV funds are deposited in the State CAMPA account** (Compensatory Afforestation Fund Act, 2016). The money is used by the Forest Department for afforestation, regeneration, and wildlife habitat improvement. This is **non-negotiable and non-waivable** for any diversion of forest land.

### 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — All Major Acts

| Aspect | Water Act 1974 | Air Act 1981 | EPA 1986 | Wildlife 1972 | FCA 1980 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Parent / Source | Art. 252 | Art. 252 | Art. 253 (Residuary) | Art. 48A | Art. 48A |
| Apex Body | CPCB + SPCB | CPCB + SPCB | MoEFCC | NBWL | MoEFCC |
| Adjudicating Body | SPCB Appellate Authority | AAT | NGT (post 2010) | NGT | NGT (post 2010) |
| Core Mechanism | Consent (CTE/CTO) | Consent (CTE/CTO) | Notification/EIA | Protected Areas | Prior Permission |
| Penalty Quantum | Modest | Modest | Strongest (5 yr jail) | Strongest (7 yr jail) | Weakest (15 days) |
| Town Planning Role | Industry zoning | Industrial emissions | Umbrella planning | Eco-sensitive zones | Forest land diversion |
| Latest Amendment | 2024 (proposed) | 1987 (key) | EIA 2020 | 2022 | 2023 |
| Quantum of Fine | ₹1 Lakh | ₹10,000 | ₹1 Lakh + ₹5,000/day | ₹1 Lakh to ₹25,000 | ₹1,000/day |
| Imprisonment | 1.5 yr | 6 yr | 5 yr | 3–7 yr | 15 days |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Structure of Environmental Legislation in India

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Constitution of India Articles 48A 51A g] --> B1[Principal Statutes]
    A1 --> B2[Subordinate Legislation Rules and Notifications]
    A1 --> B3[Jurisprudence NGT and Supreme Court]
    B1 --> C1[Water Act 1974]
    B1 --> C2[Air Act 1981]
    B1 --> C3[EPA 1986 Umbrella]
    B1 --> C4[WPA 1972]
    B1 --> C5[FCA 1980]
    B1 --> C6[NGTA 2010]
    C3 --> D1[EIA Notification 2006 2020]
    C3 --> D2[CRZ Notification 2019]
    C3 --> D3[Solid Waste Rules 2016]
    C3 --> D4[Hazardous Waste Rules 2016]
    C3 --> D5[Noise Rules 2000]
    C3 --> D6[Wetland Rules 2017]
    C6 --> E1[NGT Principal Bench Delhi]
    C6 --> E2[NGT Regional Benches Pune Kolkata Bhopal Chennai Guwahati]
    C4 --> F1[National Parks]
    C4 --> F2[Wildlife Sanctuaries]
    C4 --> F3[Conservation Reserves]
    C4 --> F4[Community Reserves]
```

### 4.2 EIA Process Flow (Functional Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stage1[S1 Screening] --> stage2[S2 Scoping]
    stage2 --> stage3[S3 Baseline Data Collection]
    stage3 --> stage4[S4 Impact Prediction]
    stage4 --> stage5[S5 Mitigation EMP]
    stage5 --> stage6[S6 Public Hearing]
    stage6 --> stage7[S7 Appraisal by EAC or SEIAA]
    stage7 --> stage8[S8 Grant or Reject EC]
    stage8 --> stage9[S9 Construction with EC Validity 7 years]
    stage9 --> stage10[S10 Production Phase 5 years]
    stage10 --> stage11[S11 Six Monthly Compliance Report]
    stage11 --> stage12{S12 Compliance OK}
    stage12 -->|Yes| stage13[S13 Continue Operations]
    stage12 -->|No| stage14[S14 EC Revocation by MoEFCC SEIAA]
    stage14 --> stage15[S15 Prosecution under EPA S 15]
```

### 4.3 Compliance Decision Matrix for a Town Planner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[New Project Proposal] --> q1{Does project involve forest land diversion}
    q1 -->|Yes| act1[FCA 1980 Stage I Stage II Approval]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Is project within 10 km of National Park}
    q2 -->|Yes| act2[Wildlife Clearance from NBWL]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Is project within 200 m of HTL}
    q3 -->|Yes| act3[CRZ Clearance from KCZMA]
    q3 -->|No| q4{Does project exceed 150 ha or 1 lakh sqm built up}
    q4 -->|Yes| act4[EIA Notification Environmental Clearance]
    q4 -->|No| q5{Is project discharging effluents}
    q5 -->|Yes| act5[Water Act Consent to Establish and Operate]
    q5 -->|No| q6{Is project emitting air pollutants}
    q6 -->|Yes| act6[Air Act Consent]
    q6 -->|No| act7[Local Building Permission under KMBR KPBR]
    act1 --> next1[Clearance obtained before construction]
    act2 --> next1
    act3 --> next1
    act4 --> next1
    act5 --> next1
    act6 --> next1
    act7 --> next1
    next1 --> next2[Construction Commencement]
    next2 --> next3[Periodic Compliance and Self Audit]
```

### 4.4 National Green Tribunal (NGT) Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app1[Application by Any Person 100 Days] --> ngt1[NGT Section 14 Adjudication]
    app2[Claim for Compensation 30 Days] --> ngt1
    app3[Appeal from SPCB Orders] --> ngt1
    ngt1 --> ngt2[Principal Bench New Delhi]
    ngt1 --> ngt3[Regional Bench Pune]
    ngt1 --> ngt4[Regional Bench Kolkata]
    ngt1 --> ngt5[Regional Bench Bhopal]
    ngt1 --> ngt6[Regional Bench Chennai]
    ngt1 --> ngt7[Regional Bench Guwahati]
    ngt2 --> ord1[Order with Directions]
    ngt3 --> ord1
    ngt4 --> ord1
    ngt5 --> ord1
    ngt6 --> ord1
    ngt7 --> ord1
    ord1 --> comp1[Compliance by Respondent]
    comp1 --> nft{Non Compliance}
    nft -->|Yes| pen1[Contempt under NGT Act]
    nft -->|No| end1[Closure]
    pen1 --> sup1[Appeal to Supreme Court under Section 22]
```

### 4.5 Stakeholder Network — Environmental Clearance Procedure

```mermaid
graph TB
    proj[Project Proponent] -->|EIA Report Form 1 Form 2 EMP| eac[EAC Expert Appraisal Committee]
    proj -->|Public Hearing Notice| pub[Public Affected Community]
    pub -->|Objections Suggestions| eac
    eac -->|Recommendation| seiaa[SEIAA or MoEFCC]
    seiaa -->|Grant Reject EC| proj
    proj -->|Compliance Reports| sro[Regional Office of MoEFCC]
    sro -->|Inspections| proj
    proj -.->|Aggrieved| ngt[NGT Appeal]
    sro -.->|Violation Found| ngt
    pub -.->|Aggrieved| ngt
    ngt -->|Order| proj
    ngt -->|Order| seiaa
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    sea1[Sea] --> ltl1[LTL Low Tide Line]
    ltl1 --> crz1a[CRZ I B Intertidal Zone]
    crz1a --> htl1[HTL High Tide Line]
    htl1 --> ndz1[NDZ 50 to 200 m]
    ndz1 --> crz1[CRZ I A Mangroves Coral Sand Dunes]
    crz1 --> crz2[CRZ II Built Up Municipal]
    crz2 --> crz3[CRZ III Rural Undeveloped]
    crz3 --> crz4[CRZ IV Water Seabed]
    htl1 --> crz4
```
