## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP PECET527]**
**"State any three salient features of the Environment Protection Act, 1986."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The EPA 1986 is an **Umbrella Act** enacted under **Article 253** of the Constitution following the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy, 1984**. It empowers the **Central Government** to take all necessary measures to protect and improve the environment and to prevent, control, and abate environmental pollution.
2. **Section 3** vests wide discretionary powers in the Central Government to coordinate actions of state governments, specify standards for emission/effluent quality, regulate industrial siting, and prescribe procedures for handling hazardous substances.
3. **Section 8** prescribes a stringent penalty — **imprisonment up to 5 years and/or fine up to ₹1 lakh** for the first offence, with an **additional fine of ₹5,000/day** for continuing violations; **minimum 1 year imprisonment** mandatory for first offence and 2 years for subsequent offence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Pattern:** Each salient feature = 1 mark. Students commonly lose marks for not citing the **Section numbers** — always mention Section 3, 5, 6, 7, 8 in the answer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP PECET527]**
**"Differentiate between National Park and Wildlife Sanctuary under the Wildlife Protection Act, 1972."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | National Park (S.35) | Wildlife Sanctuary (S.18) |
|---|---|---|
| Degree of Protection | **Highest** — no human activity allowed | High — limited rights of tribal/local residents preserved |
| Grazing | **Prohibited** | **Allowed** (in some) |
| Private Rights | **None** | Some existing rights protected |
| Removal of Forest Produce | **Not allowed** | Allowed under regulation |
| Declaration Authority | State Govt. with NBWL approval | State Govt. notification |
| Ecologically Sensitive Zone | **1–10 km** (uniform) | **0–1 km** (often notified) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**"Discuss in detail the salient features, institutional framework, and town planning implications of the Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974. Explain the consent mechanism and penalty provisions."**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Salient Features and Institutional Framework (7 marks)**

The Water Act 1974 was enacted under **Article 252** of the Constitution (initial adoption by 5 state legislatures). Its salient features are:

1. **Comprehensive coverage of water pollution** — it covers streams, rivers, wells, underground aquifers, and sea along the coast.
2. **Two-tier regulatory structure** — Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) at the apex and State Pollution Control Boards (SPCBs) at the state level.
3. **Prevention and Control** — emphasises prevention by design, not merely end-of-pipe control.
4. **Consent mechanism** — no person shall establish or operate any industry discharging trade effluent without prior consent of SPCB.
5. **Penalty provisions** — stringent imprisonment and fines with subsequent offender provisions.

**Institutional Framework:**

- **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) [Section 3]:** Constituted by the Central Government, headquartered in **Delhi**, with 5 official members and 2 non-official members chaired by a full-time **Chairman**. Functions include:
  - Coordinate actions of SPCBs.
  - Resolve disputes among SPCBs.
  - Lay down standards for water quality.
  - Collect and disseminate technical data.
  - Advise Central Government on water pollution matters.

- **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) [Section 4]:** Constituted by each State Government, with Chairman + Member Secretary + technical and non-technical members. Functions [Section 17]:
  - Plan comprehensive programmes for prevention/control.
  - Advise State Government on matters related to water pollution.
  - Collect/disseminate information.
  - Encourage, conduct, and participate in investigations/research.
  - Lay down effluent standards (within CPCB framework).
  - Prescribe mass emission standards.
  - Inspect sewage/trade effluents.

**(b) Consent Mechanism, Penalty, and Town Planning Implications (7 marks)**

**Consent Mechanism [Section 24–26]:**

- **Section 24:** No person shall, without the previous consent of the SPCB, — (a) establish or take steps to establish any industry, operation, or process; (b) bring into use any new or altered outlet; (c) discharge trade effluent into a stream, well, sewer, or land.

- **Section 25:** Application procedure — prescribed fees, details of industry, type/quantity of effluent, treatment facilities, and disposal arrangements. SPCB must grant consent within **4 months** of receipt; refusal must record reasons.

- **Two types of consent:**
  - **Consent to Establish (CTE)** — before construction/erection. Valid for **5 years**.
  - **Consent to Operate (CTO)** — before commissioning. Valid for **5 years** (renewable).

- **Section 26:** SPCB can refuse, withdraw, or cancel consent. Refusal is appealable before the **Appellate Authority** constituted under Section 28 (typically a **High Court Judge**).

**Penalty [Section 33-A and 33-B]:**

- Contravention: imprisonment up to **1 year 6 months** and/or fine up to **₹1 lakh**.
- **Subsequent contravention:** minimum imprisonment of **2 years** and fine up to **₹1.5 lakh**.
- **Continuing offence:** additional fine of **₹5,000/day** after conviction.
- **Section 64:** Officers-in-charge deemed guilty if offence committed by company.

**Town Planning Implications:**

1. **Industrial Zone Siting:** A town planner must ensure that industrial zones are not located upstream of drinking water sources for residential zones.
2. **Buffer Zones:** Minimum 100 m green buffer between industry and residential/commercial zones.
3. **Common Effluent Treatment Plants (CETP):** Master Plan must earmark 2–5% of industrial zone area for CETP.
4. **Receiving Water Body Classification:** Town planner must declare receiving water body's CPCB Class and design treatment accordingly.
5. **Storm Water Drainage:** Must not mix with industrial effluent, separate systems mandated.
6. **CPCB Class Mapping:** Overlay of CPCB water class on Master Plan's drainage map.
7. **Public Liability Insurance:** Industries handling hazardous substances must obtain insurance under PLIA 1991.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Loss of 3–4 marks):** Students forget to mention:
> 1. **Section 17** (functions of SPCB).
> 2. **Section 24** (the prohibition clause) — most students jump directly to Section 25.
> 3. The **Appellate Authority** provision (Section 28).
> 4. The **continuing offence clause** (Section 33-A) for daily fine.
> 5. The relevance of **Article 252** as the constitutional source.
> Always include a **concluding paragraph** linking to town planning; examiners award "application" marks liberally when you show real-world link.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**

**"Explain in detail the EIA Notification 2006 (as amended in 2020) and its relevance in town planning. Discuss the project categories, EIA process steps, and the role of public hearing."**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) EIA Notification 2006/2020 — Genesis, Project Categories, and Process (7 marks)**

**Genesis:**
The EIA Notification 2006 was issued under **Section 3(1) and Section 5(1) of EPA 1986** to make Environmental Impact Assessment mandatory for 39 categories of projects listed in **Schedule I**. It was the first comprehensive EIA regime in India, replacing the ad-hoc clearance system. The **EIA Amendment 2020** brought significant changes including the introduction of **EIA 2020 Notification** and a new **EIA Report form**.

**Project Categories [Schedule I]:**

| Category | Definition | Appraisal Authority | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Category A** | All projects with high environmental impact, irrespective of location | **EAC at MoEFCC** | Coal mines, ≥500 MW thermal, ≥100 km highway, nuclear, refineries |
| **Category B1** | Moderate impact projects | **SEIAA (State Level)** | Townships ≥150 ha, Industrial Estates ≥50 ha, Hotels ≥20,000 sqm |
| **Category B2** | Small/insignificant impact | **SEIAA** (auto-approval with Form-1) | Small-scale industries not in Category A |

**EIA Process Steps (Detailed):**

1. **Step 1 — Screening [Para 5]:** Determining whether the project requires an EIA Report and the level of assessment needed. B2 projects exempted; B1 and A require full EIA.
2. **Step 2 — Scoping [Para 6]:** Identifying the key issues and the ToR (Terms of Reference) for the EIA study. Conducted by EAC/SEAC.
3. **Step 3 — Baseline Data Collection [Para 7]:** Typically **one season of primary data (3 months)**. Covers air, water, soil, noise, biodiversity, socio-economic, land use.
4. **Step 4 — Impact Assessment [Para 8]:** Prediction and evaluation of impacts during construction, operation, and decommissioning phases.
5. **Step 5 — Mitigation & EMP [Para 9]:** Environment Management Plan with budget allocation, monitoring agency, frequency.
6. **Step 6 — Public Hearing [Para 11]:** As per Section 11 of EPA 1986, conducted by **SPCB / UTPCC** in the project vicinity.
7. **Step 7 — Appraisal [Para 12]:** EAC/SEAC examines the EIA Report and EMP, and recommends to SEIAA/MoEFCC.
8. **Step 8 — EC Grant [Para 13]:** SEIAA / MoEFCC grants or rejects EC within **105 days** (Category A) or **120 days** (Category B1) of receipt of final EIA Report.
9. **Step 9 — Validity:** EC valid for **7 years** for project commissioning; further **5 years** for production phase. Extendable.
10. **Step 10 — Post-EC Monitoring:** Six-monthly compliance reports to the Regional Office of MoEFCC / SEIAA.

**(b) Public Hearing, EIA 2020 Amendments, and Town Planning Relevance (7 marks)**

**Public Hearing [Para 13 of EPA 1986 and Rule 4 of EIA Rules]:**

- **Statutory basis:** Section 11 of EPA 1986.
- **Notice period:** Minimum **30 days** prior to hearing through state gazette, one national and one local newspaper, public notice at district collectorate, gram panchayat, SPCB website.
- **Venue:** District headquarters or nearby location.
- **Participation:** Any person residing within the project impact zone (typically 10 km radius) can participate.
- **Format:** SPCB/Uttar Pradesh Pollution Control Committee (UPPCB) records objections, suggestions, written submissions.
- **Output:** Minutes of Public Hearing (MoPH) form part of the final EIA Report.
- **Recent challenges:** 2020 draft amendment proposed single public hearing (vs current requirement of two for some projects) — heavily criticised.

**EIA 2020 — Key Changes:**

- **Project Categories Expanded:** 39 → 40+ categories.
- **Compliance Reporting:** Reduced from 6-monthly to annual for many sectors.
- **Validity:** Extended from 30 years to 10 years for mining leases.
- **EIA Report Validity:** 2 years from submission vs 1 year previously.
- **B2 Projects:** Exemption threshold raised; automatic exemption from full EIA.
- **New concept of "Construction Zone"** and "Buffer Zone" with distance criteria.
- **Risk-based classification** of industries (Red, Orange, Green, White) — Red categories always require full EIA.

**Town Planning Relevance:**

1. **Master Plan Integration:** Town planners must integrate EIA requirements into the **Master Plan / Development Plan** by reserving land for environmental buffers, green belts, ETP/CETP, and disaster evacuation.
2. **SEZ and Township Projects:** Any new township above **150 ha** or industrial estate above **50 ha** requires full EIA.
3. **Sequential Clearance:** Town planner coordinates **EC → CTE → CTO → Building Permit → Occupancy Certificate** in a single-window mechanism.
4. **Public Participation:** Town planners must build **public hearing / stakeholder consultation** into the planning process from the DPR stage itself.
5. **Carrying Capacity Analysis:** EIA includes a **carrying capacity** study that directly informs density of residential development.
6. **Cumulative Impact Assessment (CIA):** For projects within 10 km of existing projects, cumulative impact is critical.
7. **Post-EC Monitoring:** Master Plan must specify 6-monthly compliance reporting mechanism and designate a **Nodal Officer** for environment compliance.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Loss of 3–4 marks):**
> 1. **Missing the time limit** for EC grant (105/120 days).
> 2. **Not mentioning Section 11 of EPA** as the legal basis for public hearing.
> 3. **Forgetting ToR (Terms of Reference)** as a key step in scoping.
> 4. **Omitting the validity period** (7+5 years).
> 5. **EIA 2020 amendment confusion:** Many students are unaware of post-2020 changes — be specific.
> 6. **Role of SPCB vs CPCB:** SEIAA ≠ SPCB. SEIAA is a separate authority, not SPCB.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Article 48A (DPSP)** and **Article 51A(g) (Fundamental Duty)** are the **constitutional roots** of environmental protection in India.
- [ ] **EPA 1986 is the Umbrella Act** — every major environment rule in India is issued under it.
- [ ] **Article 252** is the constitutional basis for Water Act 1974 and Air Act 1981.
- [ ] **CPCB** is constituted under Water Act 1974 but its mandate extends to Air Act 1981 and EPA 1986 by cross-reference.
- [ ] **SPCB = State Level, CPCB = National Level**; both are statutory bodies.
- [ ] **Consent to Establish (CTE)** is needed **before construction**; **Consent to Operate (CTO)** is needed **before commissioning**.
- [ ] **CRZ 2019** classifies coastal area into CRZ-I A/B, CRZ-II, CRZ-III, CRZ-IV with NDZ of 50–200 m.
- [ ] **NDZ = No Development Zone** is measured from the **HTL (High Tide Line)**, not LTL.
- [ ] **FCA 1980** requires **prior Central Government approval** for any forest land diversion — State Government approval alone is insufficient.
- [ ] **NPV (Net Present Value)** of forest land is paid to **CAMPA account** — non-negotiable.
- [ ] **EIA Notification 2006 / 2020** has **two main project categories: A (central) and B1/B2 (state)**.
- [ ] **Category B1** projects need **full EIA + Public Hearing**; **Category B2** are exempted from full EIA.
- [ ] **EC validity:** 7 years (project completion) + 5 years (production) extendable.
- [ ] **NGT** was constituted under **NGTA 2010** — quasi-judicial body — **Principal Bench: New Delhi**.
- [ ] **NGT is bound by rules of natural justice** but is **NOT bound by the Code of Civil Procedure or the Indian Evidence Act**.
- [ ] **NGT penalty** can be up to **₹100 crore** and can include **deposit of Environmental Compensation** with CPCB/SPCB.
- [ ] **National Park (S.35)** has the **highest degree of protection**; **Wildlife Sanctuary (S.18)** allows limited private rights.
- [ ] **Wildlife Clearance** is granted by **NBWL (National Board for Wildlife)** chaired by the **Prime Minister**.
- [ ] **Eco-Sensitive Zone (ESZ)** is mandated for every National Park and Wildlife Sanctuary; width 0–10 km.
- [ ] **Noise Rules 2000** prescribe **50/55/65/75 dB(A)** for silence/residential/commercial/industrial zones.
- [ ] **Day = 6 AM to 10 PM**; **Night = 10 PM to 6 AM** for noise standards.
- [ ] **Solid Waste Management Rules 2016** mandate **source segregation** into **wet, dry, and hazardous** at household level.
- [ ] **Biomedical Waste Management Rules 2016** segregate waste into **Yellow, Red, White, and Blue** containers.
- [ ] **Hazardous and Other Wastes (Management and Transboundary Movement) Rules 2016** classify waste into **46 categories** with **Schedule I/II/III/IV** categories.
- [ ] **E-Waste Management Rules 2016 (amended 2022)** mandate **Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR)**.
- [ ] **Wetland Conservation Rules 2017** require the **State Wetland Authority** to delineate wetlands in each state.
- [ ] **Kerala State Pollution Control Board (KSPCB)** is the SPCB for Kerala; **CPCB regional office at Kochi** monitors Kerala.
- [ ] **Kerala Coastal Zone Management Authority (KCZMA)** was set up under **CRZ 1991** notification and is the state authority for CRZ clearance.
- [ ] **Biological Diversity Act 2002** mandates access and benefit sharing (ABS) for biological resources — relevant for town planning in biodiversity-rich areas (e.g., Western Ghats).
- [ ] **Public Liability Insurance Act 1991** is mandatory for industries handling hazardous substances.
- [ ] **The National Environment Tribunal Act, 1995** and **National Environment Appellate Authority Act, 1997** were superseded by the **NGT Act 2010**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequently Asked Exam Tips (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 1. Always cite **Section numbers** in answers — examiners award 1–2 marks for legal precision.
> 2. Always **distinguish EPA 1986 from Water/Air Act 1974/1981** — EPA is the umbrella Act.
> 3. Always mention **constitutional source (Article 252 or 253)** for Acts to demonstrate legal awareness.
> 4. Always conclude with **town planning implications** — it is the most differentiator between average and high-scoring answers.
> 5. For Part A questions, write **at least 3 points with brief explanation** — each point must be a complete sentence.
> 6. For Part B questions, structure your answer as **(a) Conceptual Framework, (b) Mechanisms and Provisions, (c) Town Planning Implications** to ensure full coverage of KTU 2024 CO4–CO7 outcomes.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Re-planning of Existing Towns: Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Re-planning of Existing Towns: Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Re-planning of Existing Towns (Re-planning):** Re-planning is the systematic process of surveying, analyzing, and restructuring the physical, social, economic, and infrastructural framework of an already developed town or city. The objective is to bring the obsolete, congested, or unplanned urban area up to contemporary standards of hygiene, efficiency, livability, and sustainability, **without entirely demolishing and rebuilding** it.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, re-planning is also referred to as **Town Improvement**, **Town Renewal**, or **Urban Renewal**. It differs fundamentally from the planning of a *new town* (like Chandigarh or Gandhinagar), because the planner must work with **pre-existing constraints** — existing buildings, road networks, ownership patterns, population densities, and historical heritage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Old House Renovation":**
> Imagine you have inherited a **75-year-old ancestral house**. The walls are cracked, the wiring is dangerous, the plumbing leaks, the kitchen is too small for a modern family, and there is no parking. You have **two choices**:
>
> 1. **Demolish and build a brand-new house** (this is *new town planning*).
> 2. **Renovate, retrofit, and re-arrange** the existing house — keep the strong foundation, preserve the heritage staircase, but rewire, replumb, add a new floor, and create a parking space (this is *re-planning*).
>
> Re-planning is essentially **Option 2 applied to an entire city**. The town is the "old house," and the town planner is the architect who must modernise it intelligently.

## 1.3 What are "Objects" of Re-planning?

The term **"Objects"** in the KTU syllabus is a classical town-planning vocabulary word meaning **"Objectives"** or **"Goals."** It originates from the British Town and Country Planning Acts and classical texts by authors like **Patrick Geddes** and **Sir Raymond Unwin**, where the *objects* of any planning proposal had to be formally stated at the outset.

Thus, the **"Objects of Re-planning of Existing Towns"** = the **principal goals and purposes** that justify and guide the act of re-planning an old, congested, or outdated urban area.

> [!NOTE]
> **In simple words:** "Objects" = **"WHY are we re-planning this old town? WHAT do we want to achieve?"**

The objects of re-planning can be grouped under **five broad headings** in KTU-style answers:

| S.No. | Object Category | Core Aim |
|-------|----------------|----------|
| 1 | **Public Health & Sanitation** | Eliminate disease, provide clean water, sewerage, drainage |
| 2 | **Traffic & Transportation** | Decongest roads, improve mobility, parking |
| 3 | **Housing & Living Conditions** | Reduce overcrowding, provide adequate housing |
| 4 | **Amenities & Recreation** | Provide parks, playgrounds, community spaces |
| 5 | **Aesthetics & Heritage** | Preserve heritage, improve civic appearance |

## 1.4 Why is Re-planning Necessary? (The Need)

Most old towns in India and globally suffer from one or more of the following:

- **Organic, unplanned growth** over centuries (e.g., Varanasi, Old Delhi, Madurai).
- **Congestion**: Very high population density, narrow streets, no ventilation.
- **Outdated infrastructure**: Old water pipes, no underground drainage, overburdened sewers.
- **Mixed land use**: Industries, residences, and commercial activities intermingled.
- **Traffic chaos**: Streets designed for bullock carts now carrying trucks and cars.
- **Encroachment**: Public spaces, footpaths, and water bodies illegally occupied.
- **Slum formation**: Unplanned settlements at city fringes or central pockets.
- **Heritage neglect**: Old buildings crumbling due to lack of conservation.

Re-planning addresses **all** of these issues in a structured manner.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Objects (Objectives) of Re-planning Existing Towns

The objects of re-planning are best understood as a **layered hierarchy** — moving from the most basic (health and safety) to the most aspirational (aesthetics and heritage).

### **Object 1: Improvement of Public Health and Sanitation**

This is the **foremost and most fundamental object** of re-planning. Historical town-planning movements (like the **Public Health Acts of 1848 & 1875 in Britain**) emerged precisely because of epidemics like cholera in overcrowded industrial towns.

**Specific Sub-Goals:**
- Provide a **pure and adequate water supply** through a piped network.
- Construct an **underground sewerage system** to remove wastewater.
- Provide **surface drainage** to prevent waterlogging and malaria.
- Remove **nuisances**: open drains, stagnant ponds, refuse heaps, tannery waste.
- Ensure **adequate ventilation** in dense areas by demolishing encroachments.
- Locate **noxious and dangerous industries** away from residential zones.
- Provide **public latrines and washing facilities** in slum areas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Metric (KTU standard):** As per **Indian Public Health Standards (IPHS)** and the **National Building Code (NBC)**, a minimum of **135 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** of water supply and **1 toilet per 50 persons** in dense urban areas is desirable. Re-planning must aim to achieve these standards.

---

### **Object 2: Improvement of Traffic and Transportation**

Old towns typically have streets that are **narrow, winding, and unsuited for modern vehicular traffic**. Re-planning must reorganize the road network.

**Specific Sub-Goals:**
- Widen existing arterial roads to a minimum of **30 m to 60 m** right-of-way.
- Provide a **hierarchical road network**: arterial, sub-arterial, collector, local.
- Create **ring roads, radial roads, and bypasses** to divert through-traffic.
- Designate **one-way streets** in the congested core.
- Provide **off-street parking** lots and multi-level parking.
- Improve **public transport** corridors (BRT, metro, bus terminals).
- Provide **pedestrian footpaths, cycle tracks, and zebra crossings**.
- Remove **encroachments** on roads and footpaths.
- Relocate **bus stands, railway stations, and goods sheds** to decongest the city core.

---

### **Object 3: Improvement of Housing and Living Conditions**

Old towns often have **overcrowded, dilapidated, and unsanitary housing**. Re-planning must improve residential quality.

**Specific Sub-Goals:**
- Reduce **population density** in congested zones through de-densification.
- Provide **low-income housing** and resettlement colonies for displaced slum dwellers.
- Enforce **building bye-laws**: minimum plot size, FAR/FSI limits, setbacks, height.
- Improve **ventilation and lighting** in existing dwellings.
- Provide **separate housing zones** for different income groups.
- Prevent **mixed incompatible land use** (e.g., industries inside residential colonies).
- Encourage **vertical development** in core areas to free up open space.

---

### **Object 4: Provision of Amenities, Services, and Recreation**

Re-planning must ensure the town has adequate **community facilities** for a decent quality of life.

**Specific Sub-Goals:**
- Provide **parks, playgrounds, and open green spaces**.
- According to the **URDPFI Guidelines (Ministry of Urban Affairs, GoI)**, a city should provide a minimum of **3.0 to 4.5 sq.m of open space per person**.
- Provide **educational facilities**: schools, colleges, libraries.
- Provide **health facilities**: primary health centres, hospitals, dispensaries.
- Provide **commercial facilities**: markets, shopping centres, hawker zones.
- Provide **religious and cultural facilities** with respect to community sensitivities.
- Provide **fire stations, police stations, post offices, banks** within easy reach.

---

### **Object 5: Preservation of Aesthetics, Heritage, and Civic Beauty**

Re-planning is **not** a license to demolish all old structures. A crucial object is to **preserve** the historic, cultural, and architectural character of the town.

**Specific Sub-Goals:**
- Identify and **conserve heritage buildings, monuments, and precincts** (e.g., UNESCO World Heritage Sites, ASI protected monuments).
- Create **heritage zones** with strict building controls.
- Improve **civic appearance**: tree-lined avenues, street furniture, signage.
- Demolish **visually blighting structures** that disfigure the townscape.
- Develop **waterfronts, ghats, and riverbanks** as public spaces (e.g., the Sabarmati Riverfront Development in Ahmedabad).
- Promote **landscape architecture** and urban forestry.

---

### **Object 6: Economic and Social Improvement**

A modern view of re-planning also includes **economic vitality and social equity**.

- Create **employment hubs** and industrial estates.
- Skill development and **livelihood rehabilitation** for displaced populations.
- **Inclusivity**: ensure women, elderly, disabled, and children are planned for.
- **Disaster resilience**: flood-proofing, fire safety, earthquake resistance.

---

### **Object 7: Environmental Sustainability**

Contemporary re-planning (2024 perspective) must address:

- **Storm water management** and **rainwater harvesting**.
- **Solid waste management** (segregation, composting, recycling).
- **Reduction of urban heat island** through green roofs and tree cover.
- **Renewable energy integration** in re-planned areas.
- **Protection of water bodies**, wetlands, and eco-sensitive zones.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

While re-planning is largely a **qualitative and policy-driven** subject, the following numerical standards are **frequently asked in KTU examinations** and must be memorised.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the most-tested planning standards. Master these values.**

| Parameter | Standard Value | Source / Authority |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| Water supply per capita | **135 lpcd** (minimum), **150–200 lpcd** (desirable) | CPHEEO Manual, India |
| Sewerage per capita | **80% of water supply** (i.e., ~110 lpcd) | CPHEEO Manual |
| Solid waste generation | **0.5 kg per capita per day** (Indian towns) | CPHEEO / MoHUA |
| Open space per person | **3.0 to 4.5 sq.m** | URDPFI Guidelines |
| Park size (neighbourhood) | **0.5 to 1.0 hectare** | URDPFI Guidelines |
| Population per hectare (gross density) | **250–400 persons/ha** (low density), **400–750** (medium), **750+** (high) | URDPFI |
| Minimum road width (arterial) | **30 m to 60 m** | IRC / KTMP |
| Minimum road width (local) | **9 m to 12 m** | NBC 2016 |
| Floor Space Index (FSI) | **1.0 to 2.5** for residential, varies by city | State Town & Country Planning Dept. |
| Building height limit (residential) | Typically **15 m** without fire lift, up to **24 m** with | NBC 2016 |
| Toilet ratio in dense areas | **1 toilet per 50 persons** | IPHS |
| School walking distance | **0.5 km** (primary), **1.0 km** (secondary) | URDPFI |
| Hospital radius (catchment) | **1 to 3 km** for primary, **5 to 10 km** for tertiary | URDPFI |
| Fire station radius | **3 to 5 km** | NBC 2016 |
| Parking standard | **1 ECS per 100 sq.m** of commercial floor area | URDPFI |
| Tree cover target | **33% of total area** (National Forest Policy aspiration) | MoEFCC, India |

> [!NOTE]
> **ECS** = Equivalent Car Space. 1 ECS ≈ **23 sq.m** of parking area (standard KTU reference value).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

For a Civil Engineering / Town Planning graduate, the objects of re-planning are **directly applied** in:

- **Smart Cities Mission (GoI, 2015–)** — 100 cities selected for retrofitting & re-planning.
- **AMRUT Mission** — providing water supply, sewerage, drainage in older towns.
- **HRIDAY (National Heritage City Development and Augmentation Yojana)** — heritage-sensitive re-planning.
- **Real-estate redevelopment projects** — slum rehabilitation, SRA schemes in Mumbai.
- **Urban local body (ULB) governance** — municipal corporations use these objects to draft City Development Plans (CDPs).
- **Town Planning Acts** — every state Town & Country Planning Act mandates a statement of "objects" before any re-planning scheme is notified.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line KTU exam recall formula:** *"Re-planning of existing towns has **seven objects**: Health, Transport, Housing, Amenities, Aesthetics, Economy, and Environment."* Memorise this order — it is the most-tested sequence in KTU board exams.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Process of Re-planning — An Exhaustive Step-by-Step Logical Framework

> [!NOTE]
> Re-planning is not a single act but a **sequential, iterative process**. The following is the standard KTU-prescribed logical sequence. Each step builds on the previous one.

### **Step 1: Reconnaissance Survey and Problem Identification**
- Conduct a **preliminary field visit** to understand the macro-level issues.
- Identify **black spots**: highly congested zones, flood-prone areas, encroachment hotspots.
- Consult **stakeholders**: residents, traders, elected representatives, municipal officers.
- Document **public grievances** and aspirations.

**Output:** A *Problem Inventory* listing all deficiencies.

---

### **Step 2: Detailed Socio-Economic Survey**
- Conduct a **household-level census** of the entire project area.
- Collect data on:
  - **Population**: total, density, age-sex distribution, literacy.
  - **Economy**: occupation, income, employment, industries.
  - **Housing**: type, ownership, condition, occupancy.
  - **Migration**: in-migrants, out-migrants, reasons.

**Output:** A *Socio-Economic Profile Report*.

---

### **Step 3: Physical and Infrastructure Survey**
- Map the **existing land use** in detail: residential, commercial, industrial, recreational, public/semi-public.
- Survey the **building stock**: age, condition, height, material.
- Survey **infrastructure**: roads, water supply lines, sewerage, drainage, electricity, telecom.
- Survey **topography, soil, drainage patterns, water bodies**.
- Map **encroachments** on public land, footpaths, nallahs.

**Output:** *Existing Land Use Map* (usually at 1:2000 or 1:5000 scale).

---

### **Step 4: Data Analysis and Diagnosis**
- Analyse the collected data to identify:
  - **Deficits**: how much open space is short? How many houses are substandard?
  - **Conflicts**: where is industrial use conflicting with residential use?
  - **Trends**: is density increasing? Is industry shifting?
- Use **planning standards** (URDPFI, NBC) as benchmarks.

**Output:** *Diagnostic Report* with quantified gaps.

---

### **Step 5: Formulation of Re-planning Proposals**
- Prepare a **Master Plan / Development Plan** showing:
  - Proposed land-use reorganisation.
  - Proposed road network (widening, new links, ring roads).
  - Proposed housing zones and densities.
  - Proposed locations for amenities, parks, industrial estates.
  - Proposed heritage conservation zones.
- Prepare **zonal sub-plans** for detailed local-level design.

**Output:** *Draft Re-planning Proposal / Master Plan*.

---

### **Step 6: Public Consultation and Statutory Approvals**
- Publish the draft plan for **public objections and suggestions** (as required by Town & Country Planning Acts).
- Hold **public hearings** with residents, RWAs, traders' associations.
- Revise the plan based on feedback.
- Obtain **statutory approval** from the State Town & Country Planning Department / Development Authority.

**Output:** *Approved Master Plan / Re-planning Scheme*.

---

### **Step 7: Phasing, Implementation, and Financing**
- Break the re-planning scheme into **phases**: short-term (1–3 yrs), medium-term (3–10 yrs), long-term (10–25 yrs).
- Identify **funding sources**: government grants, PPP, municipal bonds, land value capture.
- Implement through **Town Planning Schemes (TPS)** — a statutory tool where each owner's land is pooled, re-plotted with deductions for roads/amenities, and returned. The famous **TP Scheme model of Gujarat** is a KTU favourite.
- **Monitor and review** periodically.

**Output:** *Phased Implementation Programme with Financing Plan*.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Old Town Problems vs Re-planning Objects

This matrix is **the single most important KTU-exam tool**. It directly links every problem of an old town to the corresponding object of re-planning.

| S.No. | Problem in Existing Old Town | Corresponding Object of Re-planning | Typical Engineering / Planning Solution |
|-------|------------------------------|-------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 1 | Open drains, sewage in streets | **Health & Sanitation** | Underground sewerage network, covered drains |
| 2 | Contaminated water, water-borne diseases | **Health & Sanitation** | Filtered piped water supply, overhead tanks |
| 3 | Narrow, choked streets, traffic jams | **Traffic & Transportation** | Road widening, one-way streets, bypasses, ring roads |
| 4 | No parking, vehicles on footpaths | **Traffic & Transportation** | Multi-level parking lots, paid parking zones |
| 5 | Slums, kutcha houses, overcrowding | **Housing & Living Conditions** | Slum rehabilitation, SRA schemes, high-density redevelopment |
| 6 | No parks, no playgrounds | **Amenities & Recreation** | Create neighbourhood parks, tot-lots, playgrounds (≥ 3 sq.m/person) |
| 7 | Old monuments crumbling | **Aesthetics & Heritage** | Heritage zone, conservation plan, adaptive reuse |
| 8 | Industries in residential areas | **Housing / Health** | Zoning, relocation of industries, buffer green belt |
| 9 | No jobs, poverty | **Economic Improvement** | Industrial estates, skill training, market redevelopment |
| 10 | Flooding, waterlogging | **Environmental Sustainability** | Storm water drains, retention ponds, permeable pavements |
| 11 | No schools, hospitals nearby | **Amenities & Services** | Decentralised school and PHC locations within walking distance |
| 12 | Encroachment on footpaths, public land | **Traffic + Aesthetics** | Anti-encroachment drives, public space redesign |
| 13 | Mix of incompatible land uses | **Housing + Health** | Zoning regulations, land-use segregation |
| 14 | Unmanaged solid waste | **Health + Environment** | Door-to-door collection, segregation, composting plants |
| 15 | Civic apathy, dirty streets | **Aesthetics + Governance** | Public participation, Swachh Bharat interventions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In any KTU answer on "objects of re-planning," always present the answer as a **table** linking *problem → object → solution*. This is the format that scores full marks.

---

## 3.3 Mathematical Illustration: Quantifying an Object (Worked Example)

To satisfy the KTU expectation of **quantitative reasoning** even in a qualitative topic, here is a worked example showing how an object of re-planning is translated into a numerical target.

> **Example:** A congested old town has an **area of 200 hectares** and a **population of 1,50,000**. As per URDPFI guidelines, the desirable **open space standard** is **3.5 sq.m per person**. Calculate: (a) Total open space required, (b) Total open space available if the existing parks total **2.5 hectares**, (c) Additional open space to be created through re-planning.

### **Solution:**

**Given:**
- Town area $A = 200$ hectares
- Population $P = 1{,}50{,}000$ persons
- Standard $S = 3.5$ sq.m/person
- Existing open space $E = 2.5$ hectares

**Step 1:** Convert all areas to the same unit (sq.m).

$$1 \text{ hectare} = 10{,}000 \text{ sq.m}$$

$$A = 200 \times 10{,}000 = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ sq.m}$$

$$E = 2.5 \times 10{,}000 = 25{,}000 \text{ sq.m}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the total open space **required** by the standard.

$$R = P \times S = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 3.5 = 5{,}25{,}000 \text{ sq.m}$$

$$\Rightarrow R = 52.5 \text{ hectares}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **shortfall**.

$$\text{Shortfall} = R - E = 5{,}25{,}000 - 25{,}000 = 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ sq.m}$$

$$\Rightarrow \text{Shortfall} = 50.0 \text{ hectares}$$

**Step 4:** The re-planning proposal must create at least **50 hectares** of new open space to meet the URDPFI standard. This could be achieved by:
- Demolishing encroached structures on nallah banks and converting them to linear parks.
- Requiring **15% open space** in all new redeveloped housing plots.
- Acquiring **vacant land** on the town periphery and developing regional parks.

**Result:** The re-planning object "Provision of Amenities & Recreation" is now a **quantified, actionable target** of **50 hectares of new open space**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The same methodology** (Population × Standard − Existing = Gap) is used in KTU questions on water supply, sewerage, school requirements, hospital beds, parking spaces, etc. **Master this calculation template.**

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Re-planning of existing towns." Why is the term "objects" used in the context of town re-planning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Re-planning of existing towns is the **systematic process of restructuring the physical, social, and infrastructural framework of an already developed town** to bring it up to modern standards of health, efficiency, and livability, while preserving its heritage and minimising demolition.

The term **"objects"** is classical town-planning vocabulary borrowed from early 20th-century British planning literature (e.g., Patrick Geddes, Sir Raymond Unwin). It means **"objectives" or "goals"** — the formally stated purposes for which a re-planning scheme is being undertaken. Using the word "objects" instead of "objectives" preserves the **statutory and historical formality** of town-planning documents.

(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 2 marks on "objects" terminology.)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2023]`
**List any six objects of re-planning of existing towns.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per item, full sentence not required for 3-mark Q):**

The six principal objects of re-planning of existing towns are:

1. **Improvement of public health and sanitation** — water supply, sewerage, drainage.
2. **Improvement of traffic and transportation** — road widening, ring roads, parking.
3. **Improvement of housing and living conditions** — slum rehabilitation, bye-laws.
4. **Provision of amenities and recreation** — parks, playgrounds, community halls.
5. **Preservation of aesthetics and heritage** — conservation zones, urban forestry.
6. **Environmental sustainability** — solid waste, storm water, green cover.

(3 marks: ½ mark for each of six items listed correctly.)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** In Part B, students must answer **one full question** (either Q.A or Q.B). Each question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, typically carrying **7 marks each**. Both sub-parts **must** be answered for full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain in detail the **objects of re-planning of existing towns** with reference to public health, traffic, and housing.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the role of **Town Planning Schemes (TPS)** in the re-planning process with suitable examples.

#### **Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The re-planning of existing towns is guided by **three principal objects** as follows:

**1. Improvement of Public Health and Sanitation (2 marks):**
The foremost object of re-planning is to eliminate the **insanitary conditions** prevailing in old towns — open drains, contaminated water, lack of sewerage, and overcrowding. Re-planning provides a **piped water supply** of at least **135 litres per capita per day (lpcd)**, an **underground sewerage network**, and **surface drainage** systems. Noxious industries (tanneries, chemical plants) are **relocated** to designated industrial zones separated from residences by a green buffer. **Public latrines**, solid-waste collection, and vector control measures are introduced. Historically, this object was the **driving force** behind 19th-century public health legislation in Britain, and continues to be the priority in Indian AMRUT and Smart Cities missions.

**2. Improvement of Traffic and Transportation (2.5 marks):**
Old towns have **narrow, organic streets** unsuited for modern vehicular traffic. Re-planning re-engineers the road network into a **hierarchy** — arterial (30–60 m), sub-arterial, collector, and local streets. **Ring roads and bypasses** are constructed to divert through-traffic, **one-way streets** are introduced in the congested core, and **off-street parking lots** are built. **Public transport** (BRT, metro) corridors are integrated. **Pedestrian footpaths, cycle tracks, and zebra crossings** are designed for non-motorised transport. The objective is to **reduce congestion, accidents, and travel time** while improving accessibility.

**3. Improvement of Housing and Living Conditions (2.5 marks):**
Re-planning addresses **overcrowding, dilapidated housing, and slums**. It enforces **building bye-laws** (minimum plot size, setbacks, height limits, FAR), provides **resettlement colonies** for displaced slum-dwellers (e.g., the SRA scheme in Mumbai), and encourages **vertical development** to reduce horizontal sprawl. **Density is rationalised** — typically **250–400 persons per hectare** in residential zones. The result is **better ventilation, lighting, privacy, and dignity** for residents.

**Conclusion:** These three objects — health, traffic, and housing — form the **core triad** of re-planning, addressing the most pressing problems of any old town.

---

#### **Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Town Planning Schemes (TPS) — Definition and Role:**

A **Town Planning Scheme (TPS)** is a **statutory instrument** under the State Town & Country Planning Acts (e.g., the Gujarat Town Planning and Urban Development Act, 1976) that operationalises re-planning. The procedure, popularly known as the **Gujarat Model**, was pioneered in the 1920s and is now emulated by several Indian states.

**Procedure in Brief (3.5 marks):**
1. **Declaration of intention** by the Planning Authority.
2. **Preparation of a draft scheme** showing the existing and proposed layouts.
3. **Public objections and suggestions** are invited and heard.
4. **Final scheme is sanctioned** by the State Government.
5. **Land is pooled** from all owners in the scheme area.
6. **Deductions** are made for roads, parks, schools, and other public purposes (typically 40–50% of land).
7. **Reconstituted plots** are returned to original owners, now with **better access, shape, and value**.
8. **Cost of infrastructure** is recovered through a **Betterment Charge** or **Premium**.

**Role in Re-planning (3.5 marks):**
- **Land Assembly Without Forced Acquisition:** TPS allows the government to obtain land for public purposes **without the lengthy, litigious process of land acquisition**.
- **Equitable Distribution of Benefits and Burdens:** Every owner contributes proportionally and receives a reconstituted plot that is more valuable.
- **Provision of Infrastructure:** Roads, drains, water lines, parks, and schools are funded through the scheme.
- **Implementation Certainty:** Once sanctioned, the TPS is a legally binding plan with a clear financing mechanism.
- **Examples:** The TPS model has been used extensively in **Ahmedabad, Surat, and Gandhinagar (Gujarat)**, and has been adopted (with modifications) in **Maharashtra (DCPR), Karnataka, and Rajasthan**.

**Conclusion:** TPS is the most **practical, equitable, and legally robust tool** for re-planning existing towns, making the abstract "objects" of re-planning into a **concrete, financed, and implementable scheme**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **need and objects of re-planning** with specific reference to a typical congested Indian old town. Illustrate with examples of heritage-sensitive re-planning.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **role of public participation and statutory frameworks** in the re-planning of existing towns.

#### **Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Need for Re-planning (3 marks):**

A typical congested Indian old town (e.g., parts of Old Delhi, Varanasi, Madurai, Ahmedabad's old city) suffers from:
- **High density** (> 750 persons/hectare in core areas).
- **Organic street patterns** too narrow for emergency vehicles.
- **Absence of sewerage** in older pockets — only open drains.
- **Encroachment** on roads, nallahs, and heritage precincts.
- **Mixed land use** — residential, commercial, and small-scale industrial conflict.
- **Deteriorating heritage** buildings due to neglect and illegal modifications.
- **Traffic congestion** with average speeds dropping below 10 km/h in core areas.
- **Vulnerability to disasters** — fire, flood, building collapse.

**Objects of Re-planning (2 marks):** (list as in Question 1 above — Health, Transport, Housing, Amenities, Aesthetics, Economy, Environment.)

**Heritage-Sensitive Re-planning — Examples (2 marks):**

1. **HRIDAY (Heritage City Development and Augmentation Yojana):** Launched in 2015 for **12 heritage cities** including Varanasi, Ajmer, Amritsar, Gaya, Mathura, and Badami. It re-plans core areas by **conserving monuments, improving pedestrian access to heritage, and removing encroachments** in a culturally sensitive manner.

2. **Sabarmati Riverfront Development, Ahmedabad:** A heritage-sensitive re-planning that reclaimed encroached riverbanks, created **public promenades, gardens, and cultural event spaces**, while **preserving the historic Ellis Bridge and surrounding heritage precincts**.

3. **Varanasi Smart City:** Re-planning of the **Kashi Vishwanath Dham corridor** — demolishing encroachments around the temple, creating a wide pedestrian promenade, and integrating modern amenities **without disturbing the spiritual character** of the area.

**Conclusion:** Heritage-sensitive re-planning demonstrates that modernisation and conservation are **not opposed** but can be **synergistic** when guided by clearly stated objects.

---

#### **Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Role of Public Participation (3.5 marks):**

Public participation is **not optional but statutory** in re-planning. It serves to:
- **Legitimise** the re-planning scheme.
- **Surface local knowledge** — residents know flood spots, encroachment patterns, and cultural sensitivities that surveys may miss.
- **Reduce conflict and litigation** at the implementation stage.
- **Build community ownership** of the renewed area.

**Methods of Public Participation:**
- **Public hearings and consultations** at draft-scheme stage.
- **Written objections and suggestions** invited through gazette notifications.
- **Resident Welfare Associations (RWAs)** and **Community Participation Groups**.
- **Participatory GIS mapping** workshops.
- **Model Town Planning Schemes** of Gujarat, for example, mandate a **minimum 60-day objection period**.

**Role of Statutory Frameworks (3.5 marks):**

Re-planning is governed by a **layered legal framework**:
- **State Town & Country Planning Acts** (e.g., Kerala Town and Country Planning Act, 2016).
- **Development Authorities and Town Planning Departments** are the implementing agencies.
- **Master Plans / Development Plans** are the statutory blueprints (typically valid for 20–30 years).
- **Town Planning Schemes (TPS)** are the operational units of re-planning.
- **Building Rules and Bye-laws** regulate private construction.
- **Central Government schemes** (Smart Cities, AMRUT, HRIDAY, PMAY-U) provide funding and guidelines.
- **Environmental clearances** from the State Environment Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA) are required for large projects.
- **Heritage protection** under the **Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains Act (AMASR Act, 1958)** and state heritage laws.

**Conclusion:** Public participation ensures the re-planning is **democratic and inclusive**, while statutory frameworks ensure it is **legally valid, technically sound, and financially implementable**. Together, they convert the abstract "objects" of re-planning into a **lawful, participative, and executable reality**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** write "objectives" in a confused manner — list them in a **clear, numbered, hierarchical order** (start with Health, then Transport, then Housing, then Amenities, then Aesthetics). Examiners reward systematic answers.
> 2. **Do not** confuse **"objects" with "principles of town planning"** (which are zoning, greenbelt, neighbourhood unit, etc.). Objects = goals; Principles = methods.
> 3. **Do not** omit the **statutory/legal angle** in 14-mark answers. Every KTU 14-mark answer on re-planning must reference at least one **Town Planning Act, scheme, or authority**.
> 4. **Do not** present re-planning as synonymous with **slum clearance**. Slum clearance is only **one** sub-object under Housing. Re-planning is far broader.
> 5. **Do not** forget the **URDPFI numerical standards** in 14-mark answers — quoting 135 lpcd, 3 sq.m/person, etc., adds examiner's favour.
> 6. **Avoid generic statements** like "to make the city beautiful." Be **specific**: "to conserve the Mughal-era heritage precinct under the AMASR Act 1958."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final-minute revision block — memorise these points before walking into the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Re-planning** = improvement of an already-built town, **not** greenfield new town planning.
- ✅ **"Objects"** is a classical term meaning **"objectives / goals"** — used in statutory planning documents.
- ✅ **Seven Objects of Re-planning** (in KTU-mandated order):
  1. **Public Health and Sanitation** (foremost, historically driven by epidemics)
  2. **Traffic and Transportation** (decongestion, ring roads, parking)
  3. **Housing and Living Conditions** (bye-laws, slum rehabilitation, density control)
  4. **Amenities and Recreation** (parks, schools, hospitals — 3–4.5 sq.m open space per person)
  5. **Aesthetics and Heritage Conservation** (heritage zones, urban forestry)
  6. **Economic and Social Development** (jobs, livelihoods, inclusivity)
  7. **Environmental Sustainability** (storm water, solid waste, green cover, renewables)
- ✅ **Key standards to memorise:**
  - Water: **135 lpcd**
  - Solid waste: **0.5 kg/capita/day**
  - Open space: **3.0–4.5 sq.m/person**
  - Arterial road: **30–60 m**
  - Density: **250–400 persons/hectare (residential)**
  - Toilet: **1 per 50 persons**
- ✅ **Process sequence:** Reconnaissance → Socio-economic survey → Physical survey → Diagnosis → Draft Plan → Public consultation → Statutory approval → Phased implementation → Monitoring.
- ✅ **Town Planning Scheme (TPS)** = the most important **statutory implementation tool** in India (Gujarat model).
- ✅ **Public participation is mandatory**, not optional — minimum 60-day objection period.
- ✅ **Statutory bodies:** State Town & Country Planning Department, Development Authority, Municipal Corporation, SEIAA, ASI.
- ✅ **Indian flagship schemes** to quote: Smart Cities Mission, AMRUT, HRIDAY, PMAY-U, Swachh Bharat Mission (Urban).
- ✅ **Real-world examples to quote:** Sabarmati Riverfront (Ahmedabad), Kashi Vishwanath Dham (Varanasi), SRA schemes (Mumbai), Chandni Chowk redevelopment (Delhi), Hampi, Ajmer, Amritsar (HRIDAY cities).
- ✅ **Always end a 14-mark answer with a Conclusion** linking the objects back to the specific question context.
- ✅ **Use tables and diagrams** wherever possible — KTU examiners award bonus marks for visual presentation.
- ✅ **Anti-pitfall reminder:** Do NOT confuse "objects" with "principles" — they are different concepts in town-planning theory.

**End of Notes — Re-planning of Existing Towns: Objects | KTU 2024 Scheme | PECET527**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defects of Existing Towns
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Defects of Existing Towns

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Defects of Existing Towns** are the **physical, functional, social, and infrastructural inadequacies** that have accumulated in old/historic urban settlements over a long period of unplanned or inadequately planned growth. These defects manifest as **disorganized street patterns, traffic congestion, overcrowding, inadequate drainage, mixed land use, lack of open spaces, deteriorated buildings, and poor environmental conditions** — all of which lower the **Quality of Life (QOL)** and demand systematic **remedial town planning** interventions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, defects are categorized as:
- **Physical Defects** → narrow streets, encroached plots, dilapidated structures
- **Functional Defects** → inappropriate land use, traffic bottlenecks
- **Environmental Defects** → pollution, poor drainage, lack of greenery
- **Social Defects** → slums, overcrowding, lack of community facilities
- **Economic Defects** → declining commercial viability, loss of tax base

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Aging Human Body" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Town is Like a Human Body Aging Over Centuries**

Think of an old town as a **60-year-old person** who grew up without proper diet or exercise:

| Body's Aging Defect | Town's Equivalent Defect |
|---|---|
| Clogged arteries | Traffic congestion in narrow streets |
| Brittle/weak bones | Dilapidated buildings needing structural repair |
| Failing kidneys (poor filtration) | Inadequate drainage & sewage systems |
| Skin losing elasticity | Old building facades deteriorating |
| Poor eyesight | Lack of street lighting & wayfinding |
| Obesity / stiffness | Overcrowding & high Floor Space Index (FSI) |
| Memory loss / confusion | Loss of historic identity, mixed land use chaos |

Just as a **physician diagnoses defects** before prescribing treatment, a **town planner surveys and identifies defects** before proposing **improvement schemes, redevelopment plans, or master plan revisions**.

---

## 1.3 Why "Defects" is a Critical KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The concept of defects forms the **diagnostic foundation** for all subsequent planning actions:
> - **Town Planning Schemes (TPS)** under Kerala Town & Country Planning Act
> - **Town Improvement Trusts** operations
> - **Smart City Mission** retrofits
> - **Heritage Conservation** strategies
> - **Slum-free city** programs

The KTU examiner frequently asks students to **list defects, classify them, and suggest remedies** — making this a guaranteed **high-weightage topic** for both **Continuous Evaluation (CE)** and **End Semester Examinations (ESE)**.

---

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics Used in Assessing Defects

The following **measurable parameters** are used to quantify defects:

- **Road width ratio** → Available width : Required width (as per IRC/URDPFI Guidelines)
- **Plot coverage** → **Building footprint area** ÷ **Total plot area** (expressed in %)
- **Floor Space Index (FSI / FAR)** → **Total built-up area on all floors** ÷ **Plot area**
- **Population density** → Persons per hectare (standard: **250–300 persons/ha** for residential)
- **Open space ratio** → Open space ÷ Total area (minimum **15–20%** as per URDPFI)
- **Drainage gradient** → Slope in % (minimum **0.5% to 1%** for urban drains)
- **Building condition index (BCI)** → 0 to 10 scale rating structural health
- **Encroachment percentage** → Encroached area ÷ Right-of-Way (RoW) area

---

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Bloom's Taxonomy Mapping

| Cognitive Level | What Examiner Expects |
|---|---|
| **L1 – Remember** | List the defects of existing towns |
| **L2 – Understand** | Classify defects into categories |
| **L3 – Apply** | Identify defects in a given case study |
| **L4 – Analyze** | Compare defects of medieval vs colonial towns |
| **L5 – Evaluate** | Propose remedial measures with justification |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Comprehensive Classification of Defects

Town planning literature (and the KTU 2024 syllabus) categorizes defects into **FIVE broad families**. Each is unpacked below with structured bullet logic.

### 2.1.1 Physical / Structural Defects

These are defects visible in the **built fabric** of the town.

- **Narrow and irregular streets**
  - **Why:** Towns grew organically without geometric road planning.
  - **How:** Medieval bazaar towns had streets as narrow as **1.5 m to 3 m** — inadequate for two-way traffic, fire tenders, or modern vehicles.
  - **Consequence:** Traffic jams, accidents, emergency access blocked.

- **Inadequate road width & missing hierarchy**
  - Absence of **arterial, sub-arterial, collector, and local street** classification.
  - Right-of-Way (RoW) less than **6 m** in many heritage cores (URDPFI recommends **9 m to 24 m** for residential localities).

- **Building deterioration**
  - **Age-old load-bearing structures** with cracked walls, sagging roofs, termite damage.
  - Buildings exceeding their **design life of 60–80 years**.

- **Encroachments on public land**
  - Footpath vendors, parking on roads, illegal extensions into drains.
  - Reduces effective RoW by **30% to 50%** in extreme cases.

- **Dilapidated and unsafe structures**
  - Risk of collapse during monsoons / earthquakes.
  - **Kerala context:** Frequent building collapses reported in Calicut, Kochi old city areas.

---

### 2.1.2 Functional / Land-Use Defects

These arise from **poor zoning** and **incompatible adjacent uses**.

- **Mixed and incompatible land use**
  - **Why:** Lack of zoning regulations in pre-independence towns.
  - **How:** Industries, residential, and commercial activities co-exist haphazardly.
  - **Consequence:** Noise, pollution, safety hazards, reduced property values.

- **Improper plot subdivision**
  - Subdivision into **irregular, narrow, deep plots** unsuitable for modern construction.
  - Plots lacking access to **approach roads**.

- **Lack of functional hierarchy**
  - No clear **CBD, sub-centers, neighborhood units**.
  - Causes **over-centralization** at one point.

- **Obsolete industrial locations**
  - Old industrial units within **residential cores** (e.g., textile mills in Mumbai's Girgaon).

- **Inadequate or absent town center**
  - Scattered commercial activity rather than a planned hub.

---

### 2.1.3 Environmental / Sanitary Defects

These affect **public health and ecology**.

- **Inadequate drainage system**
  - Open drains, clogged nullahs, no stormwater network.
  - **Kerala's pain point:** Every monsoon, waterlogging due to **choked drains** in towns like Thrissur, Kottayam.

- **Poor solid waste management**
  - Absence of door-to-door collection, no scientific landfills.
  - Old dumping grounds contaminating groundwater.

- **Air and water pollution**
  - Vehicular emissions in congested streets (PM2.5 often **3–5× WHO safe limits**).
  - Industrial effluents in rivers (e.g., **Periyar River** pollution from Eloor industrial belt).

- **Lack of green cover / open spaces**
  - Tree cover less than **10%** (recommended: **20–33%** for tropical cities).
  - No parks, playgrounds, or breathing spaces.

- **Noise pollution**
  - Day-night average sound levels exceeding **55 dB(A)** in residential zones.

---

### 2.1.4 Social / Demographic Defects

These reflect **inequities in urban living**.

- **Overcrowding & high density**
  - Population density exceeding **500 persons/hectare** in slums.
  - **Slum pockets** without basic services.

- **Inadequate community facilities**
  - Schools, hospitals, anganwadis undersized or missing.
  - **Walking distance > 500 m** to primary school (URDPFI norm: 400 m).

- **Lack of social infrastructure**
  - No community halls, libraries, recreation centers.

- **Housing shortage**
  - **Kerala's housing deficit:** Estimated 4+ lakh units (as per recent state surveys).

- **Inequitable access to amenities**
  - Poor neighborhoods lack piped water, sewerage, electricity.

---

### 2.1.5 Economic & Administrative Defects

- **Declining economic base** → Old towns losing businesses to suburban malls.
- **Tax base erosion** → Encroachments & informal economy → Municipal revenue loss.
- **Fragmented ownership** → Multiple small plots complicate redevelopment.
- **Weak governance** → Multiple agencies (PWD, LSGD, KWA, KSEB) with overlapping jurisdictions.
- **Lack of finance for improvement** → Town Improvement Trusts under-funded.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Standards Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is the **single most important reference** for numerical/value-based questions in the KTU ESE.

| Parameter | Formula / Standard | Typical Value | Source / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Plot Coverage** | $PC = \dfrac{A_{built}}{A_{plot}} \times 100$ | **40% – 60%** | URDPFI Guidelines |
| **Floor Space Index (FSI)** | $FSI = \dfrac{\sum A_{floor}}{A_{plot}}$ | **1.5 – 2.5** for residential | Kerala Building Rules |
| **Population Density** | $D = \dfrac{P}{A}$ (persons/hectare) | **250 – 300** p/ha | URDPFI |
| **Open Space Ratio** | $OSR = \dfrac{A_{open}}{A_{total}} \times 100$ | **15% – 20%** minimum | URDPFI |
| **Road Hierarchy (RoW)** | Arterial **45–60 m**, Sub-arterial **30–45 m**, Collector **18–30 m**, Local **9–18 m** | As per road function | IRC:86-1983 |
| **Drainage Gradient** | $S = \dfrac{H}{L} \times 100$ | Minimum **0.5% to 1%** | IS:1742-1983 |
| **Network Density** | $ND = \dfrac{L_{road}}{A}$ (km/km²) | **10 – 25 km/km²** | URDPFI |
| **Population Coverage by Park** | 1 hectare park per **10,000 persons** | — | URDPFI |
| **Per Capita Open Space** | $\dfrac{A_{open}}{P}$ | **4 – 6 m²/person** | URDPFI |
| **Encroachment Index** | $EI = \dfrac{A_{encroached}}{A_{RoW}} \times 100$ | Should be **< 5%** | Site survey |

> **Critical Reminder:** Do **NOT** use the symbol `|` for absolute values inside any table — use $\vert$ or $\mid$ in LaTeX to prevent table-breaking bugs.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The defect-assessment framework is not academic fluff — it directly powers:

- **Master Plan preparation** under the Kerala Town & Country Planning Act, **2016 (KTCP Act)**.
- **Town Improvement Schemes** under **Town Improvement Trusts Act, 2000** (Kerala).
- **Smart City Proposal (SCP)** submissions for cities like Kochi.
- **Disaster Risk Reduction (DRR)** vulnerability mapping.
- **Property tax revision** based on infrastructure quality indices.
- **Heritage listing** under **AMASR Act, 2010** (Ancient Monuments and Archaeological Sites and Remains Act).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures & Numerical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Standard Step-by-Step Procedure for Defect Assessment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners reward **structured procedural answers**. Memorize this 6-step framework and apply it in any "identify defects and suggest remedies" question.

**STEP 1 — Reconnaissance Survey**
- Visual inspection of the area
- Identify glaring problems: narrow streets, encroachment, derelict buildings
- Document with **photographs, GPS coordinates, drone imagery**

**STEP 2 — Data Collection (Secondary)**
- Collect maps, building plans, census data, previous master plans
- Municipal records: property tax, building permissions, encroachment register

**STEP 3 — Primary Surveys**
- Door-to-door socio-economic survey
- Traffic volume counts (TVC) at peak/off-peak hours
- Building condition survey using **BCI (Building Condition Index) rating**
- Drainage flow measurement during storms

**STEP 4 — Quantitative Analysis**
- Compute **population density, FSI, plot coverage, network density**
- Compare each parameter against **URDPFI / IRC / KMBR standards**
- Generate **deviation report** (how much actual value deviates from standard)

**STEP 5 — Classification of Defects**
- Tag each defect as: **Physical / Functional / Environmental / Social / Economic**
- Map defects on **GIS-based thematic maps** (one map per defect category)

**STEP 6 — Remedial Measures & Priority Ranking**
- Short-term, medium-term, long-term interventions
- Use **Priority Index** based on severity × number of beneficiaries

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Calculating Population Density & Defect Diagnosis

> **Problem:** An old ward in Thrissur town has:
> - **Total area** = 18 hectares
> - **Residential population** = 6,840 persons
> - **Road network length** = 81 km (all classes combined — wait, this is per km² — re-check)
> - **Open space area** = 0.9 hectares
> - **Number of buildings** = 1,520, of which **210 are dilapidated**

**Find:**
1. Population density
2. Per capita open space
3. Percentage of dilapidated buildings
4. Identify the defects and suggest 3 remedies

### Solution — Exhaustive Step-by-Step

**Step 1: Population Density**

$$
D = \frac{P}{A}
$$

$$
D = \frac{6840 \text{ persons}}{18 \text{ ha}} = 380 \text{ persons/ha}
$$

**Interpretation:** Standard is **250–300 p/ha**. Value exceeds by **~27%** → **DEFECT: Overcrowding detected.**

---

**Step 2: Per Capita Open Space**

$$
OS_{per\ cap} = \frac{A_{open}}{P}
$$

$$
OS_{per\ cap} = \frac{0.9 \times 10000 \text{ m}^2}{6840} = \frac{9000}{6840} = 1.32 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}
$$

**Interpretation:** Standard is **4–6 m²/person**. Actual is **~30% of minimum** → **DEFECT: Severe open space deficiency.**

---

**Step 3: Percentage of Dilapidated Buildings**

$$
\%_{dilap} = \frac{N_{dilap}}{N_{total}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%_{dilap} = \frac{210}{1520} \times 100 = 13.82\%
$$

**Interpretation:** More than **10%** dilapidated buildings is a **structural safety hazard** → **DEFECT: Aging building stock.**

---

**Step 4: Identified Defects (Summary Table for the Answer Sheet)**

| Sl. No. | Defect | Category | Quantitative Evidence |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overcrowding | Social | 380 p/ha vs 300 p/ha standard |
| 2 | Open space deficiency | Environmental | 1.32 m²/p vs 4 m²/p standard |
| 3 | Aging building stock | Physical | 13.82% dilapidated |
| 4 | Inadequate drainage (assumed from monsoon profile) | Environmental | Site observation |
| 5 | Mixed land use (assumed) | Functional | Survey data |

**Step 5: Three Remedial Measures**
1. **Vertical densification control** — Restrict further FSI increase; promote **TOD (Transit-Oriented Development)**.
2. **Adaptive reuse of dilapidated buildings** — Convert to **public amenities** (library, community hall) under **Heritage Rejuvenation Scheme**.
3. **Acquisition of vacant/abandoned land** to create **pocket parks** (min 0.5 ha each) to meet URDPFI open space norms.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Network Density & Road Hierarchy Defect

> **Problem:** A heritage town in Kerala has the following road network:

| Road Class | Standard RoW (m) | Actual RoW (m) | Length (km) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Arterial | 45 – 60 | 12 | 4.5 |
| Sub-arterial | 30 – 45 | 9 | 7.0 |
| Collector | 18 – 30 | 6 | 12.5 |
| Local | 9 – 18 | 3.5 | 38.0 |
| **Total area** | — | — | **6.2 km²** |

**Find:**
1. **Network density**
2. **Average RoW deficit** across all classes
3. **Classify the defect**

### Solution

**Step 1: Network Density**

$$
ND = \frac{L_{total}}{A_{town}}
$$

$$
ND = \frac{4.5 + 7.0 + 12.5 + 38.0}{6.2} = \frac{62.0}{6.2} = 10.0 \text{ km/km}^2
$$

**Interpretation:** Standard is **10–25 km/km²**. The value is **at the lower bound** → **Marginal network density**, but the real defect is **RoW insufficiency**, not network length.

---

**Step 2: Average RoW Deficit (using mid-point standards)**

$$
\text{Deficit \%} = \frac{\text{Standard} - \text{Actual}}{\text{Standard}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Arterial} = \frac{52.5 - 12}{52.5} \times 100 = 77.14\% \text{ deficit}
$$

$$
\text{Sub-arterial} = \frac{37.5 - 9}{37.5} \times 100 = 76.00\% \text{ deficit}
$$

$$
\text{Collector} = \frac{24 - 6}{24} \times 100 = 75.00\% \text{ deficit}
$$

$$
\text{Local} = \frac{13.5 - 3.5}{13.5} \times 100 = 74.07\% \text{ deficit}
$$

$$
\text{Average RoW Deficit} = \frac{77.14 + 76.00 + 75.00 + 74.07}{4} = 75.55\%
$$

---

**Step 3: Defect Classification**

- **Primary defect:** Roads are **~75% narrower** than standards — a **CRITICAL physical defect**.
- **Secondary defect:** Inadequate hierarchy — arterial roads function as local streets.
- **Consequence:** Traffic congestion, pedestrian-vehicle conflict, fire-hazard (fire tender needs min **6 m clear width**).

**Step 4: Remedies (2-mark conceptual addition)**
1. **RoW widening** by setback enforcement + selective demolition.
2. **One-way circulation** to optimize narrow corridors.
3. **Pedestrianization** of select heritage streets.
4. **Bypass / ring road** for through traffic (long term).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Defect vs Remedy (Exam Quick-Reference)

| Defect | Short-term Remedy | Long-term Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| Narrow streets | One-way traffic, signage | Ring road, road widening |
| Mixed land use | Enforcement of use zones | Redevelopment & zoning |
| Inadequate drainage | Desilting, temporary pumping | Underground stormwater network |
| Dilapidated buildings | Condemnation notices, evacuation | Heritage adaptive reuse, retrofitting |
| Open space deficit | Pocket parks on vacant plots | Land pooling, FAR transfer |
| Slums | In-situ upgradation | Relocation + rental housing |
| Pollution | PUC enforcement, traffic rationing | EV adoption, green belt |
| Encroachment | Anti-encroachment drives | Strict building plan approval |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
