# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> **Define the term "defects of existing towns" and briefly explain any FOUR common physical defects with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~80 words):**

> **Definition:** Defects of existing towns are the accumulated physical, functional, environmental, and social inadequacies in old urban areas caused by unplanned growth, inadequate infrastructure, and lack of modernization, reducing livability and requiring remedial planning.
>
> **Four Physical Defects:**
> 1. **Narrow streets** — Medieval streets of 1.5–3 m width cannot accommodate two-way traffic (e.g., Jew Street, Kochi).
> 2. **Dilapidated buildings** — Age-old load-bearing structures with cracked walls and sagging roofs.
> 3. **Encroachments** — Footpath vending and parking reducing RoW.
> 4. **Irregular plot shapes** — Subdivided plots without proper access roads.
>
> *(Each defect: 0.5 mark; definition: 1 mark)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **List any SIX defects of existing towns and classify them under physical, functional, and environmental categories.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — Table format preferred):**

| Defect | Category |
|---|---|
| 1. Narrow streets | Physical |
| 2. Dilapidated buildings | Physical |
| 3. Encroachments on public land | Physical |
| 4. Mixed land use | Functional |
| 5. Improper plot subdivision | Functional |
| 6. Inadequate drainage | Environmental |
| 7. Loss of green cover | Environmental |

*(1 mark for listing 6 defects; 1 mark for correct classification; 1 mark for presentation)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of defects of existing towns** under five major categories. Provide at least **two examples** for each category. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **remedial measures** that a town planner would propose to address these defects, citing the relevant **Kerala / Indian regulatory framework** (URDPFI, KTCP Act 2016, Town Improvement Trusts Act). **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Q-A

#### (a) Classification of Defects — 7 Marks

**1. Physical Defects (1.5 marks)**
- Narrow, irregular streets; buildings without setbacks; encroachments; dilapidated structures.
- *Examples:* Streets of Fort Kochi (width < 3 m), aged warehouses in Mattancherry.

**2. Functional Defects (1.5 marks)**
- Mixed and incompatible land use; obsolete industrial pockets in residential zones; missing functional hierarchy; improper plot subdivision.
- *Examples:* Eloor industrial area adjacent to housing in Ernakulam; congested markets of Thrissur.

**3. Environmental Defects (1.5 marks)**
- Inadequate drainage; air and water pollution; loss of green cover; poor solid waste management; noise pollution.
- *Examples:* Periyar river pollution from Eloor; waterlogging in Kottayam town during monsoons.

**4. Social Defects (1.5 marks)**
- Overcrowding; slum formation; housing shortage; inadequate community facilities; inequitable amenity access.
- *Examples:* Slum pockets in Kozhikode; primary school access beyond 500 m in old Calicut wards.

**5. Economic & Administrative Defects (1 mark)**
- Declining economic base; fragmented ownership; weak governance; insufficient municipal finance.
- *Examples:* Closure of old textile units; multi-agency confusion between KWA, PWD, and Municipality.

---

#### (b) Remedial Measures & Regulatory Framework — 7 Marks

**1. Master Plan Revision under KTCP Act 2016 (2 marks)**
- Mandatory revision every **10 years** to incorporate current defect data and propose zonal re-organization.
- Section **48–52** of KTCP Act deals with preparation and enforcement of **Town Planning Schemes (TPS)**.

**2. Town Improvement Schemes (2 marks)**
- **Town Improvement Trusts Act, 2000 (Kerala)** enables acquisition and development of land for:
  - Road widening
  - Open space creation
  - Slum clearance and rehabilitation
  - Market modernization

**3. URDPFI Guidelines Application (1.5 marks)**
- Benchmark standards for road RoW, density, open space ratio, FAR/FSI, network density.
- All remedial designs should comply with URDPFI to access **Central scheme funding (AMRUT, Smart Cities Mission)**.

**4. Slum-Free City Plan (1 mark)**
- **In-situ upgradation** using **Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (Urban)** funding.
- Provide tenure security, basic services, and **rental housing** components.

**5. Heritage & Smart Retrofit (0.5 marks)**
- **AMASR Act 2010** for heritage structures; **Smart City SPV** for digital infrastructure retrofit.

---

> ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)**
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure** that a town planner should follow to **survey, identify, and document defects** of an existing town. Include the **surveys, parameters, and standards** to be used. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A heritage town in Kerala has the following data:
> - **Area:** 12 hectares
> - **Population:** 5,400
> - **Open space:** 0.6 hectares
> - **Total road length:** 42 km
> - **Buildings:** 1,200 (90 dilapidated)
>
> Calculate the **population density, per capita open space, network density, and percentage of dilapidated buildings**. **Identify the defects** based on URDPFI standards. **(7 marks)**

### Model Answer for Q-B

#### (a) Step-by-Step Defect Assessment Procedure — 7 Marks

| Step | Activity | Tools / Standards | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Reconnaissance survey** | Visual, photographs, drone | 1 |
| 2 | **Secondary data collection** | Old master plans, census, municipal records | 1 |
| 3 | **Traffic Volume Count (TVC)** | Manual counters, automatic sensors | 1 |
| 4 | **Building Condition Survey** | BCI rating, structural audit | 1 |
| 5 | **Socio-economic survey** | Questionnaire, door-to-door | 1 |
| 6 | **Drainage & utility survey** | GPS, GIS mapping, flow measurement | 1 |
| 7 | **Quantitative analysis** vs **URDPFI/IRC** standards + **GIS thematic mapping** + **remedy proposal** | Deviation report | 1 |

---

#### (b) Numerical Computations — 7 Marks

**1. Population Density (1.5 marks):**

$$
D = \frac{5400}{12} = 450 \text{ persons/hectare}
$$

**Standard:** 250–300 p/ha → **Defect: Overcrowding (~50% above standard).**

**2. Per Capita Open Space (1.5 marks):**

$$
OS_{pc} = \frac{0.6 \times 10000}{5400} = 1.11 \text{ m}^2/\text{person}
$$

**Standard:** 4–6 m²/person → **Defect: Severe open space deficiency (~75% below standard).**

**3. Network Density (1.5 marks):**

$$
ND = \frac{42 \text{ km}}{12 \text{ km}^2} = 3.5 \text{ km/km}^2
$$

**Standard:** 10–25 km/km² → **Defect: Severely inadequate road network (65% below minimum).**

**4. Percentage of Dilapidated Buildings (1.5 marks):**

$$
\%_{dilap} = \frac{90}{1200} \times 100 = 7.5\%
$$

**Threshold:** < 10% is borderline; **Defect: Aging building stock approaching critical.**

**5. Defect Identification Summary (1 mark):**

| Sl. | Defect | Category | Severity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overcrowding | Social | High |
| 2 | Open space deficit | Environmental | Critical |
| 3 | Inadequate road network | Physical | Critical |
| 4 | Aging buildings | Physical | Moderate-High |

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students LOSE marks:**
>
> 1. **Listing without classifying** — Examiner expects **defect + category + example**. A bare list fetches only 50% marks.
> 2. **Ignoring Kerala context** — When asked for "Indian context," still give **Kerala-specific examples** (Kochi, Kozhikode, Thrissur) — examiners reward local relevance.
> 3. **Numericals without units** — Always write **persons/ha, m²/person, km/km², %** — missing units = −0.5 to −1 mark.
> 4. **Comparing against wrong standards** — Use **URDPFI / IRC** for Indian context, **KMBR** for Kerala-specific building rules. Citing only "BIS" generically loses precision marks.
> 5. **Skipping the formula** — Always state the **formula before substitution** in numerical answers.
> 6. **No remedial measures** — Every defect MUST be paired with at least one remedy. Half the marks for "remedy" part are lost this way.
> 7. **Not drawing a sketch/diagram** — A simple line diagram of a typical old-town cross-section labeled with defects fetches **1–2 extra marks** in Part B answers.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — read this 5 times before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Defects = accumulated inadequacies in old towns (physical, functional, environmental, social, economic).
- ✅ **5 Categories** — memorize with **one example each**.
- ✅ **Key formulas:** Population density, FSI, Plot coverage, Open space ratio, Network density, Drainage gradient.
- ✅ **URDPFI standards:** Density 250–300 p/ha; Open space 15–20%; Per capita open space 4–6 m²; Network density 10–25 km/km².
- ✅ **Kerala regulatory acts:** KTCP Act 2016, Town Improvement Trusts Act 2000, KMBR, AMASR Act 2010.
- ✅ **Medieval vs Colonial town defects:** Medieval → organic maze streets; Colonial → grid-iron but with racial zoning, neglected native quarters.
- ✅ **Smart City & AMRUT** schemes are the modern **financing + implementation vehicles** for defect remediation.
- ✅ **Always pair** every defect with a **corresponding remedy** in answers.
- ✅ **Always show units** in numerical answers; missing unit = lost mark.
- ✅ **Draw a labeled diagram** wherever possible — examiners reward visual presentation.
- ✅ **State formula before substitution** — this is the #1 valuation expectation in KTU.
- ✅ **Mention "GIS-based mapping"** at least once — it's a 2024-scheme keyword.
- ✅ **Kerala-specific examples** (Kochi, Kozhikode, Thrissur, Kottayam) score higher than generic Indian examples.
- ✅ **Concept of "defect assessment" precedes "town planning scheme"** — recall this order in process questions.
- ✅ **Heritage conservation is a special case** — not all old is bad; many defects coexist with heritage value, requiring **adaptive reuse** not demolition.
- ✅ **Slum-free city** = In-situ upgradation + rental housing + tenure security (per PMAY-U).

---

### 🎯 Final Exam Day Strategy (3-Step)

1. **Read the question verb carefully** — "List" = 3 marks; "Explain" = 7+ marks; "Calculate + Identify" = numerical + conceptual.
2. **Allocate time:** Part A — 5 min/question; Part B — 18 min/question (with sub-parts).
3. **Always close the answer** with a 2-line **practical/implementation note** (e.g., *"This remediation can be implemented under AMRUT scheme funding, with Town Improvement Trust as the executing agency"*) — it shows **applied understanding** and lifts marks into the **excellent grade band**.

---

topics.title: Data Required for Re-planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Required for Re-planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Re-planning** is the systematic process of restructuring, redesigning, and reorganizing an existing built-up area or town to address the deficiencies of the current urban form, accommodate future growth, improve the quality of life, and bring the area in conformity with modern planning standards, regulations, and infrastructure requirements.

**Data Required for Re-planning** refers to the comprehensive set of qualitative and quantitative information systematically collected, recorded, analyzed, and interpreted about the existing conditions of a town/urban area to serve as the empirical foundation upon which rational re-planning proposals, master plans, and development schemes are formulated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic directly maps to **Course Outcome CO4**: *Plan urban infrastructure and formulate redevelopment strategies for existing built environments using empirical data analysis.* The data inventory forms the diagnostic phase analogous to a doctor's clinical history before prescribing treatment.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor treating a patient with chronic illness. Before prescribing any medicine, the doctor MUST first run diagnostic tests — blood reports, X-rays, MRI scans, allergy history, lifestyle data. Without these, any treatment is guesswork and potentially harmful.

**Re-planning a town is EXACTLY the same:**
- The **town** is the **patient**.
- The **town planner** is the **doctor**.
- The **data** (population, land use, infrastructure, traffic) is the **diagnostic report**.
- The **re-planning proposal** is the **prescription/treatment**.

If the planner designs roads, zones, and utilities without first knowing the *current* population density, traffic volume, building age, or drainage network — the plan will FAIL, just as a treatment without diagnosis can kill the patient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Principle:** "You cannot plan for the future unless you thoroughly understand the present." — This is the foundational philosophy of empirical town planning, championed by **Patrick Geddes** through his famous triad: *Survey → Analysis → Plan*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **standard metrics** are universally applied in KTU-evaluated re-planning exercises:

| Metric Type | Standard Unit / Benchmark |
|---|---|
| Population Density | **Persons per hectare (ppha)** — Range: 100–500 ppha |
| Floor Space Index (FSI / FAR) | Ratio (typically 1.0 – 4.0) |
| Building Height | **Meters (m)** — Restricted by zoning |
| Road Width | **Meters (m)** — Hierarchical: 7m, 12m, 18m, 24m, 30m, 60m |
| Plot Coverage | **Percentage (%)** — 40% – 75% |
| Open Space Requirement | **3.0 – 4.5 sq.m per person** (UDPFI Guidelines) |
| Water Supply | **135 – 200 litres per capita per day (lpcd)** |
| Sewage Generation | **80% of water supply** |

> [!NOTE]
> These metrics act as the *yardsticks* against which the *existing data* is compared. Any deviation triggers the *need for re-planning*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Data Required for Re-planning

The data inventory for re-planning is broadly classified into **seven major categories**. Each category addresses a specific dimension of the urban system.

### 2.1.1 Physical / Spatial Data
This is the **largest and most foundational** data category. It describes the tangible, built, and natural features of the town.

- **Topographic Data:** Contour maps, spot levels, slope analysis, natural drainage patterns. Collected using **Total Station**, **GPS**, **DGPS**, and **LiDAR** surveys.
- **Land Use Data:** Classification of every parcel into categories — residential, commercial, industrial, institutional, recreational, public/semi-public, transportation, agricultural, water bodies, vacant land.
- **Building Data:** Age of building, structural condition (good/fair/poor/dilapidated), number of floors, construction type (RCC, load-bearing, temporary), plinth area, ownership status.
- **Plot / Property Data:** Plot size, shape, frontage, setbacks, number of dwellings per plot, ownership pattern (freehold/leasehold).
- **Vacant Land Data:** Location, area, ownership, accessibility, suitability for development.

### 2.1.2 Demographic & Socio-Economic Data
This category captures the *human dimension* of the town.

- **Population Data:** Total population, density (ppha), growth rate, sex ratio, age distribution.
- **Household Data:** Average household size, number of families, BPL (Below Poverty Line) families.
- **Economic Data:** Occupational structure (primary, secondary, tertiary), income distribution, employment rate, working population ratio.
- **Migration Data:** In-migration and out-migration trends, reasons for migration.
- **Social Infrastructure Data:** Number of schools, hospitals, community halls, religious buildings per thousand population.

### 2.1.3 Infrastructure & Utility Data
- **Road Network:** Classification (arterial, sub-arterial, collector, local), right-of-way (RoW), carriageway width, surface type, condition, traffic volume.
- **Water Supply:** Source (river, well, reservoir), treatment capacity, distribution network length, per capita supply, coverage %, leakage %.
- **Sewerage & Drainage:** Network length, manholes, treatment plant capacity, coverage %, storm water drains.
- **Solid Waste Management:** Quantity generated (TPD), collection efficiency, disposal site location.
- **Electricity:** Sub-station capacity, network coverage, hours of supply, transformer locations.
- **Telecommunication:** Number of connections, network coverage.

### 2.1.4 Traffic & Transportation Data
- **Volume Counts:** PCU (Passenger Car Unit) per hour on major roads.
- **Origin-Destination (O-D) Surveys:** Travel patterns, trip purpose, modal split.
- **Parking Surveys:** On-street and off-street parking demand and supply.
- **Public Transport Data:** Route coverage, fleet size, frequency, ridership.

### 2.1.5 Environmental Data
- **Climatic Data:** Temperature, rainfall, humidity, wind direction (for orientation and building design).
- **Air Quality Data:** SPM, RSPM, NOx, SO2 levels.
- **Noise Levels:** Day/night dB readings.
- **Geological Data:** Soil type, bearing capacity, flood-prone zones, seismic zone.
- **Water Bodies:** Rivers, lakes, ponds — extent, quality, encroachments.

### 2.1.6 Legal & Administrative Data
- **Ownership Records:** Land ownership, tenure, encumbrances, mutations.
- **Zoning Regulations:** Existing land-use zoning, building bylaws.
- **Master Plan / Development Plan Status:** Current sanctioned plan, lapses, violations.
- **Government Lands:** Location, extent, encroachment status.

### 2.1.7 Economic & Financial Data
- **Property Values:** Market rates, guidance values, rental values by zone.
- **Municipal Finances:** Revenue, expenditure, outstanding debts, capital receipts.
- **Investment Patterns:** Private vs. public sector investments.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application in Re-planning |
|---|---|---|
| Population Density | $\rho = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Measures crowding; identifies need for vertical densification or decentralization |
| Population Forecasting (Geometric) | $P_n = P_0 (1+r)^n$ | Projects future demand for services |
| Decadal Growth Rate | $r = \left(\dfrac{P_n}{P_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | Determines rate of urban expansion |
| Floor Space Index (FSI) | $\text{FSI} = \dfrac{\text{Total Built-up Area}}{\text{Plot Area}}$ | Controls vertical growth; regulates density |
| Plot Coverage | $\text{Coverage} = \dfrac{\text{Ground Floor Plinth Area}}{\text{Plot Area}} \times 100$ | Determines open space availability |
| Open Space Norm | $\text{OS} = 3.0 \text{ to } 4.5 \, \text{m}^2/\text{person}$ | UDPFI standard for re-planning validation |
| Water Supply Norm | $\text{Q} = \text{Population} \times 135 \text{ to } 200 \, \text{lpcd}$ | Hydraulic design for upgraded network |
| Sewage Generation | $\text{S} = 0.80 \times \text{Q}$ | 80% of water supply becomes sewage |
| Per Capita Land | $\text{PCL} = \dfrac{\text{Residential Land Area}}{\text{Residential Population}}$ | Compared against UDPFI standard of **10–25 m²/person** |
| PCU (Passenger Car Unit) | $\text{PCU}_{\text{total}} = \sum_i (V_i \times E_i)$ | Traffic capacity assessment; $E_i$ = PCU equivalence factor |
| Trip Generation Rate | $T = f(\text{Land Use, Population, Income})$ | Predicts future traffic demand |
| Land Use Balance | $\sum \text{Land Use \%} = 100\%$ | Checks if any zone is over- or under-represented |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Vertical pipe symbols `|` have been **deliberately replaced** with the $\vert$ notation to preserve markdown table integrity. In your exam answer sheets, use the standard `|` symbol freely.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The re-planning data inventory is the **backbone of evidence-based urban engineering**. It enables:

- **Urban Local Bodies (ULBs)** to justify development plan revisions to State Town Planning Departments.
- **Smart City Mission** projects to base interventions on empirical deficiencies rather than political preferences.
- **Disaster Management** authorities to identify vulnerable buildings and plan evacuation routes.
- **Real Estate & Infrastructure** companies to assess market potential before investment.
- **NHAI / PWD** engineers to design road widening and flyover projects based on actual PCU counts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Population Forecasting Using Geometric Method

The **Geometric Progression Method** is the most commonly used model for re-planning exercises under KTU examination. It assumes that population grows at a **constant percentage rate** per decade.

### 3.1.1 The Governing Equation

$$P_n = P_0 \cdot (1 + r)^n$$

Where:
- $P_n$ = Forecasted population after $n$ decades
- $P_0$ = Population at the base/last known census year
- $r$ = Geometric mean decadal growth rate
- $n$ = Number of decades between base year and forecast year

### 3.1.2 Derivation of Geometric Growth Rate (r)

Given population $P_0, P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_k$ for $k$ consecutive decades:

$$P_1 = P_0 (1 + r)$$

$$P_2 = P_1 (1 + r) = P_0 (1 + r)^2$$

$$\vdots$$

$$P_k = P_0 (1 + r)^k$$

Solving for $r$:

$$\dfrac{P_k}{P_0} = (1 + r)^k$$

$$(1 + r)^k = \dfrac{P_k}{P_0}$$

Taking the $k$-th root on both sides:

$$1 + r = \left(\dfrac{P_k}{P_0}\right)^{1/k}$$

$$\boxed{r = \left(\dfrac{P_k}{P_0}\right)^{1/k} - 1}$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Typical):** A town had a population of **50,000** in **2001** and **75,000** in **2011**. Forecast the population in **2031** using the Geometric Method. Also calculate the water supply required at **150 lpcd**.

**Step 1 — Identify given values:**

$$P_0 = 50{,}000 \quad \text{(year 2001)}$$

$$P_k = 75{,}000 \quad \text{(year 2011, so } k = 1 \text{ decade)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the decadal growth rate $r$:**

$$r = \left(\dfrac{75{,}000}{50{,}000}\right)^{1/1} - 1$$

$$r = (1.5)^{1} - 1$$

$$r = 0.50 \quad \text{or} \quad 50\% \text{ per decade}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the number of decades to forecast:**

From 2011 to 2031:

$$n = \dfrac{2031 - 2011}{10} = 2 \text{ decades}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the geometric formula to forecast $P_n$:**

$$P_n = 75{,}000 \times (1 + 0.50)^2$$

$$P_n = 75{,}000 \times (1.5)^2$$

$$P_n = 75{,}000 \times 2.25$$

$$\boxed{P_{2031} = 1{,}68{,}750 \text{ persons}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate water supply requirement:**

$$Q = P_n \times \text{lpcd}$$

$$Q = 1{,}68{,}750 \times 150$$

$$Q = 2{,}53{,}12{,}500 \text{ litres/day}$$

$$Q = 253.125 \text{ Million Litres per Day (MLD)}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 253.13 \text{ MLD}}$$

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: PCU (Passenger Car Unit) Calculation

**Definition:** PCU is a standardized unit that converts different vehicle types into an equivalent number of passenger cars based on their dynamic and static占用 characteristics.

**Governing Equation:**

$$\text{PCU}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} V_i \cdot E_i$$

Where:
- $V_i$ = Volume of vehicle type $i$ (in vehicles/hour)
- $E_i$ = PCU equivalence factor for vehicle type $i$

**Standard PCU Equivalence Factors (IRC: 64-1990):**

| Vehicle Type | PCU Factor ($E_i$) |
|---|---|
| Car / Jeep / Van | 1.0 |
| Two-wheeler (Motorcycle / Scooter) | 0.5 |
| Auto-rickshaw | 1.0 |
| Bus / Heavy Truck | 3.0 |
| Light Commercial Vehicle (LCV) | 1.5 |
| Bicycle | 0.5 |
| Pedestrian | 0.2 |

**Worked Example:**

**Problem:** A road carries 600 cars, 400 two-wheelers, 100 buses, and 200 bicycles per hour. Calculate the total PCU/hour.

**Step 1 — Identify volumes and equivalence factors:**

$$V_{\text{car}} = 600, \quad E_{\text{car}} = 1.0$$

$$V_{2W} = 400, \quad E_{2W} = 0.5$$

$$V_{\text{bus}} = 100, \quad E_{\text{bus}} = 3.0$$

$$V_{\text{cycle}} = 200, \quad E_{\text{cycle}} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute PCU contribution of each category:**

$$\text{PCU}_{\text{car}} = 600 \times 1.0 = 600$$

$$\text{PCU}_{2W} = 400 \times 0.5 = 200$$

$$\text{PCU}_{\text{bus}} = 100 \times 3.0 = 300$$

$$\text{PCU}_{\text{cycle}} = 200 \times 0.5 = 100$$

**Step 3 — Sum the contributions:**

$$\text{PCU}_{\text{total}} = 600 + 200 + 300 + 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{PCU}_{\text{total}} = 1200 \text{ PCU/hour}}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

A two-lane road has a practical capacity of **2400 PCU/hour** (as per IRC standards). Hence:

$$\text{Capacity Utilization} = \dfrac{1200}{2400} \times 100 = 50\%$$

The road is operating at **50% saturation** — no immediate widening is needed.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Data-to-Planning Decision Mapping

This matrix is a **high-yield KTU tool** that links each data category to the specific re-planning intervention it informs.

| Data Category | Key Data Item | Re-planning Decision Triggered |
|---|---|---|
| Demographic | Density > 400 ppha | Vertical densification, FSI increase, slum upgradation |
| Demographic | Growth rate > 5% per decade | Expansion of municipal boundary, new townships |
| Land Use | Residential > 60% of total | Need for commercial, industrial, recreational zones |
| Buildings | Dilapidated structures > 20% | Urban renewal, redevelopment, heritage listing |
| Roads | Volume > 80% of capacity | Road widening, flyovers, one-way systems |
| Water Supply | Supply < 100 lpcd | New source development, network augmentation |
| Sewerage | Coverage < 70% | Underground sewerage network, STPs |
| Environment | Air quality AQI > 200 | Industrial relocation, green buffer zones |
| Open Space | < 3 m²/person | Acquisition of land for parks, playgrounds |
| Legal | Encroachment > 10% of govt. land | Strict enforcement, regularization schemes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always cite a *specific threshold value* and *the data that exceeded it* when justifying a re-planning intervention. Generic statements like "roads are congested" score zero; "PCU count of 2800/hr on a 2400 PCU/hr capacity road warrants widening" scores full marks.
