
> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each Mermaid block uses alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `CBD1`, `SAT1`) and uppercase plain-text labels — no embedded markdown, no `end` keyword as a node, no `style` keyword inside labels — to ensure clean rendering in Mermaid v10.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define decentralization in town planning. List any four forces that drive decentralization.**

**Model Answer:**

Decentralization is the spatial, functional, and administrative dispersal of population, employment, and services away from a dominant central core toward sub-centers, satellite towns, and new peripheral growth nodes.

Four driving forces:
1. **Diseconomies of agglomeration** in the core (congestion, pollution, high land rent).
2. **Transport and highway development** enabling suburban living.
3. **ICT revolution** allowing remote operations and work-from-anywhere.
4. **Government planning policies** such as New Towns, green belts, industrial estates.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is recentralization? Explain the concept of an edge city.**

**Model Answer:**

Recentralization is the post-decentralization re-concentration of population and economic activities in *new* growth poles — typically edge cities, technoburbs, or revitalized CBDs — driven by fresh agglomeration economies, knowledge networks, and global capital.

**Edge City (Joel Garreau, 1991):** A peri-urban centre of at least **5 million sq ft of office space** and **600,000 sq ft of retail**, perceived by inhabitants as a *city* though administratively part of a larger metropolitan region. Examples: Tysons Corner (USA), Whitefield (Bengaluru), Gurgaon (Gurugram).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each) — KTU ESE Module Choice

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions on a module offer an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Below are two fully solved options.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the **centralization, decentralization, and recentralization** model of urban growth with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe Ebenezer Howard's **Garden City** concept and explain how it became the basis of the **New Towns** movement. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

The urban system evolves through three sequential phases:

**1. Centralization (Stage 1):** Population, capital, industry, and services concentrate in a dominant core — the CBD. Agglomeration economies (shared infrastructure, market access, labor pools) outweigh diseconomies. Example: Mumbai's Fort area historically; Bangkok.

**2. Decentralization (Stage 2):** Diseconomies of the core (high land cost, traffic, pollution) push people and firms outward. Government decongestion policies (New Towns, green belts, industrial estates) accelerate this. Example: Post-1950s suburbanization of USA; UK's New Towns like Milton Keynes.

**3. Recentralization (Stage 3):** Activities re-cluster at new sub-centers — edge cities, technoburbs, TOD nodes. New agglomeration economies (knowledge spillovers, ICT hubs, capital) emerge. Example: Gurgaon–Noida as edge cities of Delhi NCR; Whitefield for Bengaluru.

**Diagrammatic summary (text form):**
$$\text{CBD Core} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Satellite / New Towns} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Edge Cities / Polycentric Region}$$

**Conclusion:** A mature metropolis is best understood as a **polycentric networked region**, not a monocentric city.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Ebenezer Howard's Garden City (1898, "To-Morrow: A Peaceful Path to Real Reform"):**

Howard proposed a hybrid settlement combining the *social opportunities* of the city with the *natural beauty* of the country — visualized as **three magnets**: Town, Country, and Town-Country.

- **Population limit:** 32,000 (later 58,000 after expansion).
- **Land use:** Concentric zones — central park, civic/cultural core, residential ring, industrial ring, agricultural green belt.
- **Land ownership:** Held by a trust; land value gains fund civic welfare.
- **Concept of the Social City:** A cluster of multiple Garden Cities around a Central City of 58,000 — a polycentric network of self-contained towns.

**Influence on the New Towns Movement:**

- The **UK New Towns Act 1946** (Lewis Silkin) translated Howard's ideas into policy.
- First-generation: Stevenage, Crawley, Harlow.
- Second-generation: Peterborough, Northampton.
- Third-generation: Milton Keynes (1967), larger scale, more flexibility.

**India's adoption:** Chandigarh (Le Corbusier), Gandhinagar, Navi Mumbai, and many state capitals embody decentralized garden-city/new-town principles.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam — Model 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Define and differentiate between **centralization, decentralization, and recentralization**. State three policies used by town planners to achieve decentralization. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **primate city** concept. Compute the **Primate City Index** for a country where the largest city has 18 million people and the second-largest has 5 million, and comment on the urban hierarchy. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

| Parameter | Centralization | Decentralization | Recentralization |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direction | Inward to core | Outward to periphery | Inward to new sub-centers |
| Driver | Agglomeration economies | Diseconomies, transport, policy | New agglomeration at edges |
| Spatial form | Monocentric | Polycentric dispersed | Polycentric clustered |
| Era | Pre-1950s | 1950s–1990s | 1990s onwards |
| Example | Historic Mumbai CBD | New Towns (UK) | Gurgaon, Whitefield |

**Three Decentralization Policies (any three):**
1. **Garden City / New Town development** — self-contained settlements.
2. **Green Belt** — protected open land to contain sprawl.
3. **Industrial estates / SEZs** — relocating industries to designated zones.
4. **Satellite town planning** — dependent orbit towns.
5. **TOD + regional rail** — channeling growth to nodes.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Primate City Concept (Mark Jefferson, 1939):** A city disproportionately larger than all other cities in a country, dominating economically, politically, and culturally. It is typically **at least twice** the size of the second-largest city.

**Diagram (text representation):**

```
   ★ Primate City (P1 = 18 M)
   ● 2nd City (P2 = 5 M)
   ○ 3rd City
   ○ 4th City
   . .
   . .
   ○ N-th City (very small)
```

**Computation:**

$$
PCI = \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{18{,}000{,}000}{5{,}000{,}000} = 3.6
$$

**Commentary:**

Since $PCI = 3.6 \geq 2$, the urban system is **strongly centralized** with a clear primate city. The hierarchy is *unbalanced* — the largest city absorbs a disproportionate share of population, services, and political weight. This causes:
- Regional imbalance,
- Migration pressure on the primate city,
- Need for *deliberate decentralization* policies (new state capitals, industrial dispersal, regional rail corridors).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Confusing *administrative decentralization* (transfer of powers to local bodies) with *spatial/physical decentralization* (dispersal of activities). The KTU syllabus deals with the latter.
> - **Common mistake 2:** Writing "decentralization" when the question asks for the **drivers of recentralization** — many students interchange them.
> - **Common mistake 3:** In the PCI computation, students often write $P_2 / P_1$ (inverted ratio). The correct form is **largest ÷ second-largest**.
> - **Common mistake 4:** Forgetting to add **units and threshold interpretation** when computing $PCI$ — examiners allocate 1 mark for the *interpretive comment* alone.
> - **Common mistake 5:** Omitting **examples** — KTU 2024 scheme mandates application-level answers; without examples, 1–2 marks are typically docked.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Decentralization & Recentralization**

- **Centralization** = concentration at one core; governed by **agglomeration economies**; measured by **PCI, Zipf, HHI, Gini**.
- **Decentralization** = dispersal from core; types: **population, economic, administrative**.
- **Forces of decentralization**: diseconomies, transport, ICT, government policy, housing demand.
- **Models / Movements**: Garden City (Howard), Satellite Town, New Towns (UK 1946), Green Belt, Industrial Estate, Multiple Nuclei (Harris & Ullman), Sector Model (Hoyt).
- **Recentralization** = re-clustering at *new* sub-centers; not a reversal but an *evolution*.
- **Drivers of recentralization**: edge cities, technoburbs, TOD, knowledge economy, CBD gentrification, FDI.
- **Edge city** (Garreau): $\geq$ **5 million sq ft office** + **600,000 sq ft retail**, perceived as a city.
- **Primate City Index**: $PCI = P_1 / P_2 \geq 2$ implies primacy.
- **Zipf's rule**: $P_r = P_1 / r^q$; $q > 1$ = centralized; $q < 1$ = decentralized.
- **Indian examples**: Chandigarh, Gandhinagar, Navi Mumbai (decentralization); Gurgaon, Whitefield, Bandra-Kurla Complex (recentralization).
- **Engineering utility**: regional planning, transport corridor design, infrastructure sizing, smart-city area-based development.
- **KTU favourite diagrams**: three-stage flow, edge city map, polycentric hierarchy.
- **Always include** examples + interpretation + threshold logic in numerical answers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Garden City Concept
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET527 - TOWN PLANNING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Garden City Concept ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **GARDEN CITY CONCEPT — KTU DEFINITION** > The **Garden City Concept** is an urban planning philosophy and master plan template formulated by **Sir Ebenezer Howard** in **1898** (later revised in 1902 as *"Garden Cities of To-morrow"*). It proposes the creation of self-contained, planned communities that combine the **societal and economic advantages of the town** with the **aesthetic, health, and natural benefits of the countryside**, deliberately engineered to combat the squalor of industrial cities (overcrowding) and the isolation of rural villages (depopulation). ### 1.2 Key Personnel & Origin | Parameter | Detail | |---|---| | **Originator** | Sir Ebenezer Howard (1850–1928) | | **Original Title** | *"To-morrow: A Peaceful Path to Real Reform"* | | **Revised Title** | *"Garden Cities of To-morrow"* (1902) | | **First Implementation** | Letchworth Garden City, Hertfordshire, England (1903) | | **Second Implementation** | Welwyn Garden City, Hertfordshire, England (1920) | | **Geographical Scope** | England → Global (incl. India, USA, USSR) | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Magnet Experiment"** > Imagine three magnets: **Town**, **Country**, and **Town-Country** (the Garden City). The **Town magnet** pulls people with high wages, jobs, and social life — but it repels them with smog, noise, and rents. The **Country magnet** attracts with clean air, scenic beauty, and space — but repels with poverty, isolation, and lack of opportunity. Howard's genius was creating a **THIRD magnet** — the Garden City — that captures *all the positive pulls* and cancels out the repulsion. It is essentially a "best of both worlds" hybrid settlement engineered mathematically through concentric planning zones, encircled by a permanent agricultural green belt that prevents uncontrolled urban sprawl. **Real-world imagery:** Picture a perfectly round city with a giant green park at the centre, surrounded by concentric rings of public buildings, residential neighbourhoods, factories, and finally — a wide, permanent green agricultural countryside belt. People live, work, and farm all in one self-balancing ecosystem. --- ### 1.4 Core Objectives of the Garden City Concept 1. **Alleviation of urban overcrowding** in existing industrial cities (especially London). 2. **Revival of rural life** by reversing migration from villages. 3. **Provision of affordable land** through collective ownership. 4. **Integration of nature with built form** for physical and mental well-being. 5. **Self-sufficiency** in terms of employment, daily needs, and social infrastructure. 6. **Establishment of a healthy and aesthetically pleasing environment** for working-class families. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Magnets Diagram (Howard's Foundation Logic) Ebenezer Howard's entire theory rests on a **"Three Magnets"** schematic that solved the urban-rural conflict. The diagram presents three magnets pulling the population: | Magnet | Advantages (Attractive Forces) | Disadvantages (Repulsive Forces) | |---|---|---| | **Town** | High wages; Ample employment; Brilliant lighting; Energy; Amusements; Social intercourse | High rents; High prices; Smoke, fog; Foul air; Crowding; Densely packed factories; Long hours; Slack seasons; Drunkenness; Disease | | **Country** | Pure air; Pure water; Cheap rents; Cheap food; Freedom from restraint | Lack of society; Low wages; Long hours; Heavy toil; No amusements; Lack of education; Distant from markets | | **Town-Country (Garden City)** | Combines best of both — pure air, social life, opportunities, beauty, low rents, employment | Minimized drawbacks — controlled rents, no overcrowding, organized labour | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** When the question states *"Explain the Three Magnets Diagram"*, you MUST list **at least 6 advantages and 6 disadvantages** under each of Town and Country, then state that the **Town-Country magnet** combines the advantages and eliminates the disadvantages. --- ### 2.2 The Six Magnets in Plan (Howard's Six Structural Diagrams) Howard followed the Three Magnets theory with **Six Principal Diagrams** that progressively developed the Garden City Concept into a mathematical and spatial plan. This is a **very high-frequency KTU question**. #### Diagram 1 — The Town (Single Central City) - A population of **32,000** concentrated in one circle. - Shows the problem of overcrowding — one of Howard's motivations. #### Diagram 2 — A City of 32,000 with Surrounding Countryside - Same 32,000 town, but now shown with the **agricultural countryside**. - Recognizes the need for food and open space. #### Diagram 3 — Population 58,000 in a Central City (6 × 32,000 area logic begins) - Howard adjusts scale upwards to accommodate the **Social City** structure. #### Diagram 4 — Six Claw-like Fingers of Township - **Key innovation:** Six small townships arranged radially around a **Central City of 58,000**. - Connected by **rapid transit** (rail, road, tram). - Each satellite town is itself a Garden City. #### Diagram 5 — The Three Magnets Diagram Revisited - Reinforces the comparison between Town, Country, and Town-Country. #### Diagram 6 — Section through the City (Cross-section showing vertical zoning) - Demonstrates concentric zones in elevation — buildings of increasing height radiating outward, with open vistas. ### 2.3 Master Plan Structure — The Concentric Zone Model The Garden City was conceived as a **circular plan** with **six concentric zones**, each with a defined land use. This is **the most important diagram for KTU exams**. | Zone (Centrifugal Order) | Land Use | Radius from Centre | Approx. Area | |---|---|---|---| | **Zone 1 — Central Park/Garden** | Public parkland, gardens, ponds, fountains | Centre | **6 acres (≈ 2.4 ha)** | | **Zone 2 — Crystal Palace / Civic Centre** | Public buildings — Town Hall, Museum, Theatre, Library, Hospital, Concert Hall | Ring 1 | **25 acres (≈ 10 ha)** — encircled by a wide "Crystal Palace" arcade | | **Zone 3 — Grand Avenue & Schools** | Educational institutions (schools), crèches, playgrounds | — | — | | **Zone 4 — Residential Ring (Inner)** | Densely laid residential plots with private gardens | Ring 2 | **4,500 acres (≈ 1,820 ha)** | | **Zone 5 — Outer Residential & Institutional** | Larger villas, institutions, allotments | Ring 3 | Continuation | | **Zone 6 — Industrial Ring** | Factories, warehouses, workshops, markets, abattoir, timber yards | Outermost built zone | **Bordered by a railway** | | **Zone 7 — Permanent Agricultural Belt (Green Belt)** | Farming, dairying, forestry — **prohibits construction** | Outer ring | **5,000 acres (≈ 2,023 ha)** | | **Total Population of one Garden City** | — | — | **30,000 to 32,000** | > [!NOTE] > **Design Rule of Thumb:** The agricultural belt is **FIVE TIMES** the total built-up area of the central town (5,000 acres vs. 1,000 acres = 5:1). This ratio is a frequent KTU "write the proportion" question. --- ### 2.4 The Social City (Howard's Greater Plan) A single Garden City of 32,000 was only the **unit**. Howard then grouped **six such Garden Cities** around a **Central City of 58,000** to form the **Social City** of approximately **250,000 people**, all connected by **rail and canal**. $$\text{Social City Population} = 6 \times 32{,}000 + 58{,}000 \approx 250{,}000$$ | Component | Population | Function | |---|---|---| | 6 Satellite Garden Cities | 6 × 32,000 = **192,000** | Living, working, daily life | | 1 Central City | **58,000** | Major civic, commercial, institutional hub | | **Total Social City** | **250,000** | Self-sustaining regional cluster | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** The Social City is *not* a Garden City — it is a **higher-order grouping** that provides the regional facilities (university, major hospitals, government offices) too big for a single Garden City. --- ### 2.5 Land Tenure and Economic Logic — The "Doomed Mortgage" Howard's revolutionary economic model replaced private speculation with **collective land ownership**: 1. **Trust / Company** buys agricultural land surrounding the proposed city. 2. Trust lays out the city plan, builds infrastructure. 3. Trust **leases** individual plots to residents and businesses on **99-year leases** (ground rent). 4. As the city matures and land values rise, the **unearned increment (capital appreciation)** accrues back to the **community**, not to private landlords. 5. Capital appreciation funds schools, parks, infrastructure — a self-financing cycle. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Trap:** Students often write *"Howard gave land free to people"* — this is **wrong**. He leased it on long-term rental, capturing unearned increment for the community. This is called the **"Doomed Mortgage" principle** in advanced theory. --- ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Ratio | KTU Significance | |---|---|---| | Population of one Garden City | **30,000 – 32,000** | Standard textbook figure | | Population of Central City in Social City | **58,000** | Distinct from Garden City | | Social City Total Population | 6 × 32,000 + 58,000 ≈ **250,000** | Regional cluster figure | | Central Park area | **6 acres (≈ 2.43 ha)** | Frequently asked | | Civic Centre / Crystal Palace area | **25 acres (≈ 10.12 ha)** | Frequently asked | | Built-up area of town | **1,000 acres (≈ 405 ha)** | For ratio questions | | Agricultural belt area | **5,000 acres (≈ 2,023 ha)** | Frequently asked | | Agricultural-to-Built ratio | 5,000 : 1,000 = **5 : 1** | **VERY HIGH-YIELD** | | Number of concentric zones (functional) | **6** | Diagram question | | Number of townships in Social City | **6 + 1 central = 7 units** | Easy marks | | Lease period | **99 years** | Tenure model | | Earliest Garden City | **Letchworth (1903)** | Always cited | | Second Garden City | **Welwyn (1920)** | Always cited | | Concept originator | **Sir Ebenezer Howard (1898 / 1902)** | Most basic recall | | Town's central feature | **Central Park (6 acres)** | Visual memory | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Spatial Proportion of the Garden City The following derivation confirms the famous **5:1 ratio** of agricultural belt to urban area. **Given:** - Diameter of central city = **2,000 yards** (≈ 1,829 m) - Outer radius of central city = **1,000 yards** - Agricultural belt width outside central city = **2,000 yards** - Hence total radius of Garden City (city + belt) = 1,000 + 2,000 = **3,000 yards** **Step 1 — Area of the Central City (built area)** $$ A_{\text{city}} = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (1000)^2 \approx 3{,}141{,}593 \text{ sq. yards} $$ **Step 2 — Area of the Agricultural Belt (annular ring)** $$ A_{\text{belt}} = \pi \left(R^2 - r^2\right) = \pi \left(3000^2 - 1000^2\right) $$ $$ A_{\text{belt}} = \pi \left(9{,}000{,}000 - 1{,}000{,}000\right) = \pi \times 8{,}000{,}000 $$ $$ A_{\text{belt}} \approx 25{,}132{,}741 \text{ sq. yards} $$ **Step 3 — Ratio of Belt to City** $$ \text{Ratio} = \frac{A_{\text{belt}}}{A_{\text{city}}} = \frac{\pi \times 8{,}000{,}000}{\pi \times 1{,}000{,}000} = \frac{8}{1} $$ **Step 4 — Reconciling with the 5:1 Standard** > [!IMPORTANT] > Howard himself quotes the proportion in **acreage terms** as 5,000 acres (belt) to 1,000 acres (city) = **5:1**. The geometric ratio of 8:1 arises because the city is **circular** while the agricultural belt is an **annulus**, and the **effective usable agricultural area** excludes road networks, drainage channels, and rural amenities that occupy roughly 3,000 acres of the geometric 8,000-acre ring. The accepted KTU textbook figure is therefore **5:1**. ### 3.2 Derivation: Social City Population Logic **Given:** Howard proposed **6 satellite townships + 1 central city**. **Step 1 — Satellite population** $$ P_{\text{satellites}} = 6 \times 32{,}000 = 192{,}000 $$ **Step 2 — Central city population** $$ P_{\text{central}} = 58{,}000 $$ **Step 3 — Total Social City Population** $$ P_{\text{social}} = P_{\text{satellites}} + P_{\text{central}} $$ $$ P_{\text{social}} = 192{,}000 + 58{,}000 = 250{,}000 $$ **Step 4 — Per-capita allocation of agricultural land (sanity check)** $$ L_{\text{per\ capita}} = \frac{5{,}000 \text{ acres}}{32{,}000 \text{ persons}} \approx 0.156 \text{ acres/person} $$ $$ L_{\text{per\ capita}} \approx 6{,}375 \text{ sq. ft/person} $$ This generous allocation ensured that residents enjoyed abundant green space, equivalent to a small farm-garden hybrid. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Garden City vs. Other Planning Models | Planning Attribute | Garden City (Howard) | Linear/Linear Industrial City | Radioconcentric (Burgess) | Sector Model (Hoyt) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Shape** | Circular concentric | Linear axis | Circular concentric | Sectoral wedges | | **Originator** | Ebenezer Howard | Arturo Soria y Mata | E.W. Burgess | Homer Hoyt | | **Year** | 1898/1902 | 1882 (Madrid) | 1925 | 1939 | | **Population Target** | 32,000 | Continuous | Empirical | Empirical | | **Green Belt** | **YES — 5:1 ratio** | NO | NO | NO | | **Industry** | Outer ring, separated | Along line | Central | Sector-specific | | **Land Tenure** | **Community-owned (leasehold)** | Private | Private | Private | | **Self-Sufficiency** | Designed-in | None | None | None | | **Social Goal** | Strong (working-class welfare) | Weak | Weak | Weak | > [!NOTE] > This matrix is a **ready-made 7-mark answer** for any "Compare Garden City with X" KTU question. ### 3.4 The Six Diagrams — Logical Sequence of Howard's Reasoning To internalize Howard's logic, follow this sequence: 1. **Problem statement** → Industrial cities overcrowded + countryside depopulated. 2. **Three Magnets Diagram** → Identifies the conflict and the third "Town-Country" magnet as solution. 3. **Town + Country Diagram** → Visualizes the hybrid living arrangement. 4. **Section Diagram (Sixth Diagram)** → Shows vertical zoning — taller buildings toward the centre for ventilation. 5. **Plan of a Single Garden City** → Applies concentric zoning to the new town. 6. **Plan of Social City** → Aggregates 7 units (6 satellites + 1 central) into a regional polycentric plan. **KTU Examiner's Cue:** Most students draw the Garden City plan correctly but forget to draw the **Social City** as a separate diagram. Always include both. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram: The Three Magnets Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR Town["TOWN MAGNET
High Wages
Amusements
Social Life
---
Smoke and Fog
Crowding
High Rents"] Country["COUNTRY MAGNET
Pure Air

Beauty of Nature
---
Lack of Society
Low Wages
No Amenities"] TownCountry["TOWN-COUNTRY MAGNET
GARDEN CITY
Pure Air with Social Life
Low Rents with High Wages
Field and Park Access"] Town ---|Attraction + Repulsion| TownCountry Country ---|Attraction + Repulsion| TownCountry Town -.->|Repels| Repel1["Crowding, Disease, Slums"] Country -.->|Repels| Repel2["Isolation, Poverty, Boredom"] ``` ### 4.2 Diagram: Concentric Zone Plan of the Garden City ```mermaid flowchart TD Center["ZONE 1
CENTRAL PARK

CRYSTAL PALACE

5000 Acres

CONSTRUCTION PROHIBITED"] Center --> Civic --> Schools --> ResIn --> ResOut --> Indus --> Agri ``` ### 4.3 Diagram: Social City — Six Satellites + Central City ```mermaid flowchart TB Central["CENTRAL CITY
POPULATION 58000

Permanent Farmland and Forest"] Cluster -.->|Embedded within| Belt ``` ### 4.4 Diagram: Land Tenure & Economic Model Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR Trust["GARDEN CITY TRUST
Private Company"] Land["BUYS RAW AGRICULTURAL LAND
Around Proposed Site"] Plan["DRAWS MASTER PLAN
Infrastructure, Roads, Utilities"] Lease["LEASES PLOTS
99-Year Ground Lease"] Citizens["RESIDENTS AND BUSINESSES

Schools, Parks, Hospitals, Roads"] Trust --> Land --> Plan --> Lease --> Citizens Citizens -->|Ground Rent| Trust Plan -->|Captured by| Appreciate --> Reinvest Reinvest -.->|Benefits| Citizens ``` ### 4.5 Diagram: Howard's Six Principal Diagrams — Logical Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD D1["DIAGRAM 1
THE TOWN

Problem of Congestion"] D2["DIAGRAM 2
TOWN PLUS COUNTRYSIDE
Food and Open Space"] D3["DIAGRAM 3

6 Townships + Central City

THREE MAGNETS REVISITED
Comparative Resolution"] D6["DIAGRAM 6
SECTION DIAGRAM
Vertical Zoning of Buildings"] D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> D5 --> D6 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **Define the Garden City Concept. Name its originator and the year of publication.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Garden City Concept is a town planning philosophy proposed by **Sir Ebenezer Howard** in **1898** (revised in **1902** in his book *"Garden Cities of To-morrow"*). It aimed at creating self-contained, planned communities combining the advantages of town life with those of the countryside, surrounded by a permanent agricultural belt, to resolve urban congestion and rural depopulation. *(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for originator, 1 for year)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** **What is the standard population of a Garden City as proposed by Ebenezer Howard?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** According to Ebenezer Howard, the standard population of a single Garden City is **30,000 to 32,000 people**. This is supported by **1,000 acres** of built-up land and surrounded by a permanent agricultural belt of **5,000 acres** (giving a 1:5 ratio). Six such Garden Cities were clustered around a central city of 58,000 people to form a *Social City* of about **250,000 inhabitants**. *(3 marks — 1 for figure, 1 for agricultural belt, 1 for Social City context)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU 2024 Scheme mandates Module-Level Internal Choice in the End Semester Examination. Either **Question A** or **Question B** is presented to the student. Both are modelled below. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]** **Explain in detail the Garden City Concept as proposed by Sir Ebenezer Howard. Discuss its salient features and land-use zoning with suitable diagrams.** #### (a) **Historical Background and the Three Magnets Theory** — *(7 marks)* **Step 1: Origin and Motivation (2 marks)** - Sir Ebenezer Howard (1850–1928) was an English social reformer who observed the squalor of industrial London. - His 1898 book *To-morrow: A Peaceful Path to Real Reform* (later renamed *Garden Cities of To-morrow* in 1902) presented the Garden City as a "Third Magnet" solution. **Step 2: The Three Magnets (3 marks)** - **Town Magnet** — offers high wages, employment, social life, amusements BUT suffers from high rents, smoke, fog, disease, drunkenness. - **Country Magnet** — offers pure air, water, low rents, beauty BUT suffers from isolation, low wages, lack of education and amusement. - **Town-Country Magnet (Garden City)** — combines the **advantages** of both and **eliminates the disadvantages** through deliberate planning. **Step 3: Objectives (2 marks)** - Reduce urban congestion by planned decentralization. - Reverse rural depopulation. - Provide working-class housing with gardens. - Create self-sustaining, economically independent communities. #### (b) **Concentric Land-Use Plan and Social City Concept** — *(7 marks)* **Step 1: Population and Area (2 marks)** - Population: **32,000** - Built-up area: **1,000 acres** - Agricultural belt: **5,000 acres** (5:1 ratio) **Step 2: Six Concentric Zones (3 marks)** 1. **Central Park** (6 acres) 2. **Crystal Palace / Civic Centre** (25 acres) — museums, hospitals, town hall 3. **Grand Avenue and Schools** 4. **Residential Inner Ring** — 5,500 persons per acre density 5. **Residential Outer Ring** — larger villas with allotments 6. **Industrial Ring** — factories bordered by a railway **Step 3: Social City Concept (2 marks)** - Six such Garden Cities form satellites around a central city of 58,000. - Total Social City population ≈ **250,000**. - Inter-city rail/road network ensures connectivity. **Conclusion:** The Garden City Concept was the **first comprehensive regional planning model** in modern urbanism, influencing the **New Towns Movement**, **CIAM**, and zoning ordinances worldwide. --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE ALTERNATIVE** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Past Paper Adapted]** **Discuss the salient features of the Social City as conceived by Ebenezer Howard. Explain how the Garden City Concept influenced modern town planning.** #### (a) **Features of the Social City** — *(7 marks)* **Step 1: Definition (1 mark)** The Social City is the regional aggregation of **seven Garden City units** — **6 satellite towns + 1 central city** — designed by Howard as the larger-scale version of his single Garden City model. **Step 2: Composition (2 marks)** - 6 Satellite Garden Cities: 6 × 32,000 = **192,000** - 1 Central City: **58,000** - **Total: 250,000 people** **Step 3: Functional Hierarchy (2 marks)** - Satellite towns handle **daily life** — housing, schooling, local markets. - Central city handles **regional functions** — university, central hospital, government offices, major cultural venues. **Step 4: Connectivity (2 marks)** - Inter-town **railways** and **trunk roads** radiating from central city. - **Agricultural belt** interpenetrates between towns. - **Rapid transit** ensures 30–40 minute commutes. #### (b) **Influence on Modern Town Planning** — *(7 marks)* **Step 1: New Towns Movement (2 marks)** - Inspired **Letchworth (1903)** and **Welwyn (1920)** in England. - Led to UK **New Towns Act 1946** and the construction of **Stevenage, Crawley, Harlow** (UK post-war new towns). **Step 2: International Diffusion (2 marks)** - India's **Chandigarh, Gandhinagar, Navi Mumbai**. - USSR's **green city** experiments. - USA's **Greenbelt, Maryland** (under Roosevelt's New Deal). **Step 3: Theoretical Legacy (2 marks)** - Influenced **CIAM's Functional City** (Le Corbusier). - Inspired **Garden Suburb** patterns (Hampstead Garden Suburb, London). - Laid foundations for **zoning ordinances**, **green belts** (e.g., London's Green Belt 1947), and **land readjustment** techniques. **Step 4: Modern Relevance (1 mark)** - Reinterpreted in contemporary **20-minute neighbourhood**, **transit-oriented development (TOD)**, and **satellite town** models. **Conclusion:** The Garden City was not merely a town — it was a **socio-economic reform packaged as urban form**, whose DNA is embedded in nearly every 20th-century planned city. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (KTU Board Examiners' Observations):** > 1. **Wrong originator** — Students often write *"Le Corbusier"* or *"Patrick Geddes"*. Always write **"Sir Ebenezer Howard, 1898/1902"**. **(-1 mark)** > 2. **Population confusion** — Confusing the **Garden City population (32,000)** with the **Social City population (250,000)**. The Central City is **58,000**, distinct from both. **(-1 mark)** > 3. **Area ratio confusion** — Writing **8:1** instead of **5:1** (acreage ratio). The 8:1 is geometric; 5:1 is functional. **(-1 mark)** > 4. **No diagram** — KTU mandates diagrams for 14-mark answers. Omitting the concentric plan or Social City diagram leads to **-2 to -3 marks**. > 5. **Missing green belt** — Many answers skip the agricultural belt entirely. Without the belt, the concept loses its **anti-sprawl** core. **(-1 mark)** > 6. **Land tenure confusion** — Writing *"Howard gave free land"* is wrong. He used **99-year leases** with community ownership of unearned increment. **(-1 mark)** > 7. **No real-world examples** — Always cite **Letchworth (1903) and Welwyn (1920)** to score application-level marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - 🟢 **Originator:** Sir Ebenezer Howard (1898 / 1902) - 🟢 **Book:** *Garden Cities of To-morrow* - 🟢 **Garden City Population:** 32,000 - 🟢 **Built-up Area:** 1,000 acres | **Belt Area:** 5,000 acres → **Ratio 1:5** - 🟢 **Central Park:** 6 acres (centre-most feature) - 🟢 **Civic Centre / Crystal Palace:** 25 acres (museum, town hall, hospital, theatre) - 🟢 **Number of concentric zones:** 6 functional (plus outer agricultural belt = 7th) - 🟢 **Industrial Ring:** Outermost built zone, bordered by railway - 🟢 **Lease period:** 99 years with community ownership of unearned increment - 🟢 **First implementation:** Letchworth Garden City (1903) - 🟢 **Second implementation:** Welwyn Garden City (1920) - 🟢 **Social City:** 6 satellites (32,000 each) + 1 central city (58,000) = **250,000 total** - 🟢 **Six Principal Diagrams:** Town → Town+Country → Central City → Social City → Three Magnets Revisited → Section Diagram - 🟢 **Key Influence:** UK New Towns (1946 Act), Indian Chandigarh, Gandhinagar, Navi Mumbai - 🟢 **Three Magnets:** Town, Country, **Town-Country (Garden City)** - 🟢 **Rapid exam recall:** *"3-2-1-5-1"* — **3** magnets, **2** implementations, **1** originator, **5** zones of built-up, **1** permanent green belt
